characters.web: Classic vertical, wheel-shaped orb web with a tight closed centre (hub) and a sticky spiral - the snare most people picture when they think 'spider web'. The spider often sits head-down in the middle, or hides in a nearby retreat with a signal thread to the hub. A few Portuguese members break this mould: Cyrtophora citricola spins a dense, finely-meshed horizontal sheet ('tent') web, often in colonies on cactus, agave or shrubs, and Zygiella leaves one open pie-slice sector missing from its orb.
characters.eyes: Eight small eyes in two rows, set on the front of a fairly low head. The four middle eyes form a small square/trapezoid box in the centre; the remaining two pairs sit out at the sides, often the two side eyes of each group close together and slightly raised. None of the eyes is greatly enlarged - quite different from the two huge front eyes of a jumping spider. The forehead (clypeus) is low.
characters.body_shape: Usually a large, plump, rounded spider. The abdomen is high, broad and often bulbous, frequently widest and most swollen toward the front, sometimes with a pair of humps or shoulder bumps (e.g. Araneus, Gibbaranea) - though some are flattened (Nuctenea, which presses into bark cracks), elongate (Larinia, Mangora), or have lobed/scalloped edges (Argiope lobata). The head region (cephalothorax) is much smaller than the abdomen.
characters.legs: Relatively short, strong and noticeably spiny - stout legs bristling with stiff spines, held in an X or two forward pairs + two back pairs when sitting in the web. Not the long thin spindly legs of a Tetragnatha or daddy-long-legs.
characters.colour: Hugely variable: browns, greys, reddish, yellow, green, sometimes with bold patterns. Many show a leaf-like or oak-leaf 'folium' marking on the abdomen, and Araneus diadematus famously has a white cross of dots. Argiope are spectacular - A. bruennichi with yellow-black tiger banding, A. lobata silvery and lobed. Araniella is plain apple-green; Mangora pale green-yellow with dark marks; Cyrtophora mottled grey-brown with paired humps.
characters.behaviour_habitat: Sit-and-wait orb builders. Found in gardens, hedgerows, scrub, reeds, woodland edges, walls and around buildings, doorways and windows (Zygiella, Nuctenea, Larinioides often near human structures and water). The web is rebuilt regularly. They subdue prey by wrapping it in silk. Most are harmless and most often noticed by their large conspicuous webs in late summer and autumn.

field_questions.ask: Is the web a flat, round, wheel-shaped orb with a solid (filled-in) centre, hanging more or less vertically?
field_questions.yes_means: Strongly points to Araneidae (orb-weavers). A horizontal dome/tent sheet web on cactus or shrubs, in colonies, also fits this family (Cyrtophora).

field_questions.ask: Is the spider a plump, round-bodied spider with a much larger abdomen than head, sitting head-down in or beside the web?
field_questions.yes_means: Typical Araneidae body plan and posture.

field_questions.ask: Are the legs short and strongly spiny (covered in stiff bristles) rather than long and thin?
field_questions.yes_means: Consistent with Araneidae and helps separate them from the long-legged Tetragnathidae.

field_questions.ask: Are all eight eyes small and similar in size (no single huge pair), with four in a small box in the middle of the face?
field_questions.yes_means: Fits Araneidae and rules out jumping spiders (Salticidae) and wolf spiders (Lycosidae), which have prominent enlarged eyes.

lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Also build orb webs, but the web hub is open (a small hole, not a tight closed centre) and the spiders are slender with very long thin legs and (in Tetragnatha) huge forward-pointing jaws; Araneidae are plump-bodied with short spiny legs and a closed hub.

lookalikes.family: Theridiidae (comb-footed / cobweb spiders, incl. false widows)
lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Build irregular 3-D tangle (cobweb) snares, not a flat wheel orb, and have smooth almost spineless legs with a comb of serrated bristles on the back tarsi; Araneidae legs are conspicuously spiny.

Build small horizontal sheet or hammock webs and are mostly tiny (a few mm) with a noticeably high forehead (clypeus higher than twice the front-eye width) and chelicerae bearing stridulating ridges; Araneidae have a low clypeus and no such ridges.

Often treated within or split out of Araneidae; Zygiella x-notata (common on window frames) is told by its orb with one open pie-slice sector and a signal thread to a tubular retreat - a behavioural rather than gross-anatomy difference.

source.title: Araneidae - interactive genus key, Spiders of Europe (araneae.nmbe.ch)
source.authors: Nentwig W, Blick T, Bosmans R, Gloor D, Hänggi A, Kropf C, Stäubli A

deeper_key.notes: araneae.nmbe.ch provides a real, working genus-level dichotomous key for Araneidae (16 main couplets plus several 'further genera' such as Cyrtophora and Zygiella listed outside the main dichotomy). It reliably reaches GENUS in the field from external features (abdomen tubercles/shape, prosoma colour, abdomen hairiness/spines, trichobothria, sternum colour). It does NOT take you to species: within rich genera like Araneus, Argiope, Larinioides, Araniella and Neoscona, separating species almost always requires examining the genitalia (epigyne of females, palp of males) under a microscope, supported by abdominal pattern, range and habitat. Several couplets in the key themselves point to the male palp (median apophysis) and epigyne, which need magnification. So: genus from a good photo is realistic; species usually needs a specimen and a scope, plus a species-level reference such as the World Spider Catalog or regional revisions. Note also a couple of couplets rely on figure comparison ('habitus as fig.') rather than purely worded characters.

records.found_check: All ten photographed species are genuine Araneidae (or, for Zygiella, within the Araneidae/Zygiellidae complex placed in Araneidae by the araneae.nmbe.ch key) and the family characters fit them: Araneus diadematus and Gibbaranea gibbosa key out at couplets 1-3 via their anterior abdominal tubercles/humps and eye-size difference (closed-hub vertical orb, plump spiny-legged body). Argiope bruennichi (yellow-black transverse bands) and Argiope lobata (silvery, laterally lobed abdomen) match couplet 5 exactly. Mangora acalypha matches couplet 9 (the plumose-trichobothria/green-pale genus). Larinioides cornutus keys to couplet 11. Araniella cucurbitina is the uniformly green abdomen of couplet 12. Nuctenea umbratica is the extremely flattened, bark-dwelling abdomen of couplet 13. Zygiella x-notata sits among the 'further genera' (its hallmark missing-sector orb on window frames). Two honest caveats fully consistent with the family: (1) Cyrtophora citricola is a true araneid but builds a horizontal, non-sticky tent/sheet web in colonies rather than the classic vertical sticky orb - confirmed Araneidae despite the atypical web; (2) Zygiella's family placement is debated (Araneidae vs Zygiellidae/Phonognathidae), but the source key and current WSC treatment retain it in/under Araneidae. The 'short spiny legs + plump body + small similar-sized eyes + closed-hub orb' family signature holds for the other eight without exception.

characters.web: Free-living hunters, no snare web. They stalk and pounce on prey by sight, often using a silk drag-line as a safety tether when they jump. They build small flat silk retreats (sleeping/moulting/egg sacs) tucked under bark, stones, leaves or in wall crevices, but never a prey-catching web.
characters.eyes: The single most reliable family feature. Eight eyes arranged in three rows on a flat-fronted, box-like head. The front row has one HUGE pair of forward-facing central eyes (the anterior median eyes) that look like a pair of big dark headlights or 'searchlights', flanked by a smaller pair. Two more pairs sit further back on top of the head; the rearmost pair is set wide and can be hard for beginners to spot because they are small, but they are always there. Overall the eyes occupy a square (quadrangular) field when viewed from above. If a small spider seems to look straight back at you with two big round eyes, it is almost certainly a jumping spider.
characters.body_shape: Small and compact with a high, blunt, almost rectangular front head region (cephalothorax) that is tall and squared-off at the front where the big eyes sit. The head region is usually as tall as it is, giving a 'face'. Body often short and rounded; some are flattened (wall-dwellers) and a few are ant-mimics with a narrow waist and elongated body.
characters.legs: Eight short, sturdy legs held ready to spring; not long and spindly. Front legs (legs I) are often noticeably thicker or longer, especially in males, and may be raised in display. Dense tufts of adhesive hairs (scopulae) on the feet let them grip glass and walls and walk upside down. They move in quick darting hops rather than a steady run.
characters.colour: Hugely variable. Many are drab grey/brown/black, but a great number are vividly patterned with white, orange, red, iridescent metallic blue/green, or bold black-and-white stripes. Males are frequently brighter and more strikingly marked than females. Bodies are usually furry-looking with short dense hairs and often have contrasting bands, spots or a coloured 'face'.
characters.behaviour_habitat: Active by day in sunshine. Famous for turning to face and 'watching' you, and for short, accurate jumps. Found on walls, fences, tree trunks, rocks, fence posts, low vegetation, leaf litter and around buildings, especially in warm sunny spots. In Portugal they are common on south-facing walls, in gardens, scrubland and along sunny paths.

field_questions.ask: Does it have two big round forward-facing eyes that seem to look right at you, like headlights?
field_questions.yes_means: Almost certainly Salticidae (jumping spider) - this huge central pair of eyes is the family's signature.

field_questions.ask: Is the front part of the head tall, blunt and box-like (a 'face' with the eyes on the flat front), rather than low and rounded?
field_questions.yes_means: Consistent with a jumping spider; their squared-off high head differs from most other families.

field_questions.ask: Does it move in short, quick hops/jumps and turn to face you, instead of running smoothly away or sitting in a web?
field_questions.yes_means: Strong sign of Salticidae - they are visual stalk-and-pounce hunters, not web-sitters.

field_questions.ask: Is it a small (usually under 1.5 cm), compact, often furry spider out in the open on a sunny wall, fence or plant by day?
field_questions.yes_means: Fits the typical jumping-spider profile; combined with the big eyes it is a confident family ID.

lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Crab spiders also sit in the open and don't build snare webs, but their eyes are all small and similar (no giant headlight pair), and they hold their long front two leg-pairs out sideways crab-fashion and scuttle sideways rather than hopping and facing you.

Lynx spiders are agile day-hunters with spiny legs, but their six rear eyes form a hexagon high on the head and the front eyes are small - none of the big forward 'searchlight' pair, and their legs are long and conspicuously spiny.

Wolf spiders also have two prominent large eyes facing forward, but theirs sit in a low, rounded head (not a tall box-like face), they are larger and longer-legged, run fast along the ground rather than hopping, and females carry an egg sac attached to the spinnerets at the rear.

Some ant-mimicking sac/ground spiders look like the ant-mimic jumping spiders (Myrmarachne, Leptorchestes, Synageles), but they lack the big forward-facing median eyes; if the small 'ant' has two enlarged front eyes it is the jumping-spider mimic.

source.title: Araneae - Spiders of Europe: Genus-level identification key for Salticidae
source.authors: Nentwig W, Blick T, Bosmans R, Gloor D, Hänggi A, Kropf C, Stäubli A (Spiders of Europe / araneae.nmbe.ch)

deeper_key.notes: araneae.nmbe.ch provides a working dichotomous GENUS-level key for European Salticidae (transcribed below; the main dichotomous branch runs to ~24 couplets and ~4-5 levels deep, plus ~48 further genera handled outside the main branch, including Menemerus, Icius, Hyllus, Cosmophasis, Afraflacilla, Anasaitis and others). Realistic ceiling: a careful naked-eye / phone-photo user can reach FAMILY confidently from the eyes, and with a good macro photo can often reach GENUS using colour, body shape and eye-region proportions. SPECIES-level identification is generally NOT reliable from photos alone: many genera (e.g. Euophrys, Pseudeuophrys, Talavera, Heliophanus, Pellenes, Sitticus/Attulus) require examining the adult male palp or the female epigyne (genitalia) under a microscope, and several couplets here already depend on tibial apophyses and cheliceral teeth that need magnification. Use this genus key as a guide, then confirm species against araneae.nmbe.ch species pages and the Iberian checklist (Branco, Morano & Cardoso 2019); be prepared that a definitive species ID often needs a specimen and genitalia under a scope.

records.found_check: Confirmed: all three photographed species are Salticidae and fit the family characters above. Salticus scenicus (zebra jumping spider) appears in the genus key at couplet 22 (diagonal/transverse pairs of white stripes -> Salticus) and matches the big-headlight-eyes, compact body and bold black-and-white striping described. Philaeus chrysops appears at couplet 19 (male head region black, abdomen red with a black stripe -> Philaeus), matching the 'vivid red, big-eyed' character note. Menemerus semilimbatus is one of the ~48 further genera the key lists outside the main branch (genus Menemerus); it is a flattened, grey-white-haired wall-dweller with the typical forward-facing large eyes and a white carapace side-band, fully consistent with the family eye and body-shape characters. No conflicts found.

characters.web: Snare web present. A messy, irregular three-dimensional tangle of criss-crossing silk (a 'cobweb' or tangle-web), usually in a corner, under a ledge, in low vegetation or stonework. Many species add 'gumfoot' lines: near-vertical sticky threads anchored to the ground that snap up and trap walking insects. The web is NOT a flat geometric spiral like an orb-weaver's.
characters.eyes: Eight eyes arranged in two rows of four. In most theridiids the lateral eyes of each side nearly touch one another; in the widow spiders (Latrodectus) the front and back lateral eyes are clearly separated by at least one eye-diameter. The rear (posterior) eyes often look brighter/silvery and the front-middle pair (anterior median) can look pale or pearly. You usually need a phone macro photo to see eye layout clearly.
characters.body_shape: Abdomen distinctly rounded, bulbous and globe-like (often nearly spherical), typically much larger than the smaller cephalothorax (head/leg region). The spider often hangs upside-down in its tangle web. A few small genera have a hardened or humped abdomen, but the classic look is a fat round ball on slender legs.
characters.legs: Legs slender and fairly long, held tucked in. The family's signature is a 'comb' on the fourth (rearmost) pair: a row of curved, serrated bristles on the underside of the last leg segment (tarsus IV), used to fling silk over prey. This comb is tiny and usually only visible under magnification, so it is a confirming microscope/strong-macro feature rather than a naked-eye one.
characters.colour: Hugely variable. Many are drab brown, grey or yellowish with pale spots or a band; many are glossy. The famous members are glossy and boldly marked: false widows (Steatoda) are shiny dark brown/black with a pale cream crescent or band around the front of the abdomen; true widows (Latrodectus) are glossy black with red, orange or yellow spots/blotches on the abdomen and frequently a red mark underneath.
characters.behaviour_habitat: Sit-and-wait snare builders that hang inverted in the tangle. Extremely common around and inside human structures (walls, sheds, window frames, eaves, garden furniture) as well as in low vegetation, leaf litter, rock crevices and dry stone walls. Several (Steatoda nobilis, the widows) are well established in warm, dry, sunny parts of Portugal.

records.common_group: comb-footed spiders / cobweb or tangle-web spiders (includes true widows and false widows)

field_questions.ask: Is the web a messy 3-D tangle of criss-crossing threads (not a flat circular orb)?
field_questions.yes_means: Consistent with Theridiidae; rules out orb-weavers (Araneidae).

field_questions.ask: Is the abdomen a fat, rounded, almost spherical ball, clearly bigger than the front body section?
field_questions.yes_means: Typical theridiid body shape.

field_questions.ask: Is the spider hanging upside-down in its web rather than sitting in a tube, funnel or sheet?
field_questions.yes_means: Matches the inverted resting posture of cobweb spiders.

field_questions.ask: Is it glossy with bold markings - a cream crescent/band, or red/orange spots on a dark abdomen?
field_questions.yes_means: Points to the conspicuous theridiids: false widows (Steatoda) or true widows (Latrodectus).

lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Linyphiids build a horizontal sheet or hammock web and hang beneath it, and are mostly tiny and flatter-bodied; they lack the serrated comb on tarsus IV. Theridiids make an irregular 3-D tangle (often with sticky gumfoot lines) and have a more globular abdomen.
Orb-weavers build a flat, wheel-shaped spiral web and have strong spines on their legs. Theridiids build a 3-D tangle with no neat spiral and have smooth, comb-bearing legs.

Look almost identical (globular abdomen, tangle web, comb on leg IV) and were historically lumped with theridiids; reliably separated only under a microscope (Nesticidae have a paracymbium on the male palp and differ in genital structure). Habitat hint: nesticids favour dark, damp, sheltered spots (caves, cellars, under stones).

Pholcids have extremely long, thin legs and a longer, more cylindrical (not globe-like) body, and bounce/vibrate the web when disturbed. Theridiids have proportionally shorter legs and a rounder abdomen.

source.title: Araneae - Spiders of Europe: dichotomous genus-level identification key for Theridiidae
source.authors: Nentwig W, Blick T, Bosmans R, Gloor D, Hänggi A, Kropf C, Stäubli A (key for Theridiidae contributed by Barbara Thaler-Knoflach)

deeper_key.notes: araneae.nmbe.ch provides a genuine online dichotomous key that reaches GENUS for European Theridiidae (about 39 numbered couplets covering ~40 genera; key contributed by Barbara Thaler-Knoflach). It does NOT key to species. Most of the diagnostic couplets rely on microscope-only characters - relative eye sizes and spacing, sclerotisation/humps on the abdomen, male frontal protrusions, tarsus-vs-metatarsus length, and especially the shape of the male palp and the female epigyne. For naked-eye/phone use it is only partly tractable: the very conspicuous genera can often be placed without a scope (e.g. Latrodectus - widows - keys out early at couplet 4 because its lateral eyes are clearly separated and it is glossy black with red/orange markings; Steatoda - false widows - is glossy and stout with a pale frontal band). Getting from genus to species almost always requires examining the genitalia (epigyne of the female, palp of the male) under magnification, and for some genera examining mature specimens only. Honest ceiling: confident family ID by web + body shape in the field; genus sometimes in the field for the showy ones but generally microscope-assisted; species essentially always microscope/genitalia work, ideally checked against the araneae.nmbe.ch species pages or the World Spider Catalog.

records.found_check: All three personally photographed species are Theridiidae and fit the family characters. Steatoda nobilis (false widow): glossy, stout, globular abdomen with a pale cream crescent/band around the front, female ~9.5-14 mm, male ~7-11 mm - matches the bulbous-abdomen + tangle-web + comb-foot description, and in the genus key its lateral eyes are NOT widely separated, so it does not key out at couplet 4 (correctly separating it from the true widows). Latrodectus tredecimguttatus and Latrodectus lilianae (Mediterranean/Iberian black widows): glossy black globular abdomen with red/orange spots or blotches, female ~7-15 mm, male much smaller - these are the genus that keys out at couplet 4 of the araneae.nmbe.ch key because their anterior and posterior lateral eyes are clearly separated by at least one diameter. The 1-15 mm size range and the eye-row arrangement stated in the family characters cover all three.

characters.web: Free-living ambush hunter, no snare web. Sits motionless on flowers, leaves or bark and grabs prey that comes close; builds only a silk egg-sac and drag lines, never a catching web.
characters.eyes: Eight small eyes in two gently curved (recurved) rows of four. All eight are roughly equal and fairly small; the side (lateral) eyes usually sit on small raised bumps or a low ridge, so the eye region can look slightly knobbly or 'horned'. Often ringed with white. No single huge pair like a jumping spider has.
characters.body_shape: Squat, broad and somewhat flattened, with a wide rounded-to-triangular abdomen and a crab-like outline. Holds the body low and can scuttle sideways and backwards. Some (Thomisus, Pistius) have the rear of the abdomen widened, angular or raised into shoulders/humps.
characters.legs: The two FRONT pairs of legs (I and II) are clearly longer and noticeably stouter than the two short rear pairs (III and IV). The long front legs are held open and curved forward like crab claws to seize prey; rear legs are short and often spiny. Legs are laterigrade (twisted so they splay sideways), giving the sideways crab walk.
characters.colour: Hugely variable. Either dull camouflage browns/greys (often the bark- and ground-dwelling Xysticus/Ozyptila types), or vivid flower colours - white, yellow, pink or green - in the flower-ambushers. Some, notably Misumena vatia, slowly change between white and yellow over days to match their flower. Synema globosum is a striking shiny black with a bright red, orange or yellow abdomen.
characters.behaviour_habitat: Sit-and-wait ambush predators. Flower-hunters (Misumena, Thomisus, Synema, Diaea, Ebrechtella, Heriaeus, Runcinia) wait on or in blossoms, often in sunny meadows, scrub and gardens. Other genera (Xysticus, Ozyptila, Coriarachne) lurk on leaf litter, low vegetation, tree bark and stones. Active spring to autumn; widespread across Portugal from coast to mountains.

field_questions.ask: Are the front two pairs of legs clearly longer and thicker than the back two pairs, and held open sideways like crab pincers?
field_questions.yes_means: Strong sign of a crab spider (Thomisidae) - this leg pattern is their hallmark.

field_questions.ask: Is the spider sitting still on a flower or leaf with no web, rather than hanging in a snare?
field_questions.yes_means: Consistent with Thomisidae, which are ambush hunters that never build a catching web.

field_questions.ask: Is the body squat, broad and a bit flattened (crab-like outline) rather than long and slender?
field_questions.yes_means: Fits the broad crab-spider body shape.

field_questions.ask: Are there eight small eyes of fairly similar size in two curved rows, with the side eyes on little bumps - and NO single pair of large forward eyes?
field_questions.yes_means: Matches Thomisidae; rules out jumping spiders (which have two huge front eyes).

lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Running crab spiders. Their leg lengths are more even and leg II is often the LONGEST (not the obviously beefy front pairs of true crab spiders), the body is flatter and more elongate, and they are fast, restless runners rather than motionless flower-sitters.

Huntsman spiders are also laterigrade and crab-like but are much larger and long-legged, with all four leg pairs long and spanning, and they run fast over walls and bark rather than sitting on flowers.

Jumping spiders also sit in the open and stalk prey, but they have two very large forward-facing eyes in the front row and a more compact, hairy body; crab spiders have eight smallish even eyes and longer crab-like front legs.

Flatties are extremely flattened, fully laterigrade with all legs splayed in a near-flat disc, and have a distinctive front eye row of six; crab spiders are thicker-bodied with only the front two leg pairs enlarged. (Not native to mainland Portugal, but possible confusion in images.)

source.title: Araneae - Spiders of Europe: Determination key for the genera of Thomisidae
source.authors: Nentwig W, Blick T, Bosmans R, Gloor D, Hänggi A, Kropf C, Stäubli A

deeper_key.notes: araneae.nmbe.ch provides a working online dichotomous key to the European genera of Thomisidae (version 06.2026), transcribed in full below (about 13 couplets resolving ~15 commonly keyed genera, with Bassaniodes, Pherecydes, Psammitis, Spiracme, Firmicus, Misumenops and Bassaniana noted as additional/limited-occurrence). Several distinctive flower genera (Thomisus, Synema, Misumena, Heriaeus, Pistius, Diaea, Runcinia) can be reached by external features visible in a good photo. BUT the two largest and most species-rich Portuguese genera, Xysticus and Ozyptila, are split mainly on spination and especially on genitalia: reliable species-level identification within these (and the Ozyptila/Cozyptila split) requires examining the male palp or female epigyne under a microscope. So for many of Portugal's 59 species the realistic ceiling from a field photo is GENUS, not species - honest expectation: species-level ID for the showy flower spiders, microscope-only for most Xysticus/Ozyptila. No comprehensive Portugal-specific species key exists; use araneae.nmbe.ch species pages plus genitalia for the final step.

couplets.character: Shape of rear abdomen and position of lateral eyes

options.text: Rear of abdomen angularly widened; lateral eyes on a shared raised projection of the head
Otherwise

couplets.character: Colour and where it lives

options.text: Red-brown to grey-black; mainly on shrubs
Males pale yellow to yellow-green, females white/pink; often on flowers

couplets.character: Body colour pattern

options.text: Prosoma and legs shiny brown-black, abdomen bright yellow/red/orange with black pattern
Otherwise

couplets.character: Hairiness and colour

options.text: Whole body with long off-standing hairs; bright green or yellow
Otherwise

couplets.character: Whether anterior and posterior lateral eyes share one projection

options.text: Anterior and posterior lateral eyes on a shared projection
Anterior and posterior lateral eyes on separated projections

couplets.character: Spacing of the median eyes

options.text: Distance between the front median eyes equals that between the rear median eyes
Distance between the front median eyes smaller than between the rear median eyes

couplets.character: Shape of the abdomen tip

options.text: Abdomen with a tail-like prolongation or a tip-like elevation
Otherwise

couplets.character: Form of body and abdomen

options.text: Abdomen elevated, posterior wedge-shaped; brown-grey
Prosoma longer than wide; abdomen long and slender with a cone-shaped rear

couplets.character: Presence of a hump between the lateral eyes

options.text: Hump present between the lateral eyes
Otherwise

couplets.character: Anterior eye row and body flattening

options.text: Anterior eye row nearly straight; body strongly flattened; brown with yellow pattern
Anterior eye row clearly bent; body not strongly flattened

couplets.character: Colour of prosoma and legs

options.text: Prosoma and legs green; abdomen creamy yellow with brown pattern
Yellow, brown, grey or black

couplets.character: Median eye quadrangle shape and ventral spines on tibia I (microscope)

options.text: Median eye quadrangle width >= length; tibia I with >=4 pairs of ventral spines
options.leads_to: Xysticus (including segregate genera Bassaniodes, Psammitis, Spiracme)

options.text: Median eye quadrangle width < length; tibia I with 2 pairs of ventral spines

couplets.character: Genitalia (microscope): male palp / epigyne and cymbium

options.text: Male palp with a tegular apophysis; epigyne with hoods
Male palp without tegular apophysis; cymbium with a tutacular groove

records.found_check: All three photographed species fit the family characters well. Synema globosum: shiny black prosoma/legs with a bright red-orange-yellow abdomen keys straight out at couplet 3 (Synema); a flower-ambushing crab spider with enlarged front legs and no web. Thomisus onustus (female 7-11 mm, male 2-4 mm): pink/white/yellow with the angularly widened, somewhat triangular rear abdomen described at couplets 1-2 (Thomisus); flower ambusher, no snare web. Misumena vatia (female 6-9 mm, male 3-4 mm): broad flat crab-like body, white/yellow with slow colour change, lateral eyes on a shared projection with evenly spaced median eyes - keys to Misumena at couplet 6; classic flower ambusher that builds no catching web. All confirm the family's diagnostic enlarged front leg pairs, broad body, eight small eyes (lateral eyes on bumps), and webless ambush lifestyle.

characters.web: Free-living ground hunter — builds no snare web. Many dig a silk-lined burrow in soil (Lycosa tarantula and large Hogna females live in burrows); others wander leaf litter and low vegetation with no retreat at all.
characters.eyes: The giveaway. Eight eyes in three rows: a bottom row of four small eyes, then a middle row of two very large forward-facing eyes (often visible as a 'headlight' pair in a phone photo), then a top row of two medium eyes set wider and further back on the head. The two big middle eyes plus the 4-2-2 stacking is the classic wolf-spider face.
characters.body_shape: Robust, longish oval body that sits low to the ground; head region (cephalic part of the carapace) is somewhat raised and squared-off at the front to carry the big eyes. Not flattened like a huntsman, not bulbous like an orb-weaver.
characters.legs: Eight sturdy, spiny, run-fast legs of moderate length (not the splayed crab-like sprawl of a huntsman). Often faintly banded or annulated. Built for chasing prey on foot rather than hanging in a web.
characters.colour: Cryptic earth tones — browns, greys, tan, sometimes blackish — matching soil and litter. Most have a distinct pale (cream to orange-yellow) lengthwise median stripe down the carapace with darker lines radiating from it, and a dark spear-shaped cardiac mark on the front of the abdomen. Undersides can be darker, sometimes black on the belly in big species.
characters.behaviour_habitat: Ground-dwelling hunters of open, sunny, dry to damp habitats: grassland, scrub, fields, dunes, woodland edges, gardens, riverbanks. Active runners by day or night; eyes shine in torchlight. The clincher is the female carrying a round silk egg sac attached to her spinnerets at the rear of the abdomen, and later carrying the hatched spiderlings piled on top of her abdomen.

field_questions.ask: Does the spider have two large eyes side by side in the middle of the face, with four small eyes in a row below them and two more eyes set wider on top?
field_questions.yes_means: The 4-2-2 wolf-spider eye pattern — strongly points to Lycosidae.

field_questions.ask: Is it running on the ground or sitting at a burrow mouth rather than waiting in a web?
field_questions.yes_means: Free-living ground hunter, consistent with wolf spiders (they build no snare web).

field_questions.ask: Is a female dragging a round egg sac attached to the tip of her rear end (spinnerets), or carrying a swarm of babies on her back?
field_questions.yes_means: Almost certainly Lycosidae — egg sac fixed to the spinnerets and brood-carrying is their signature (nursery-web spiders carry the sac in the jaws instead).

field_questions.ask: Is it an earth-toned brown/grey spider with a pale stripe down the middle of the carapace and a dark mark at the front of the abdomen?
field_questions.yes_means: Typical wolf-spider colour scheme; supports Lycosidae alongside the eye and behaviour checks.

lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Nursery-web spiders (e.g. Pisaura) look very similar and have a wolf-like eye layout, but the female carries her egg sac held under the body in her jaws (chelicerae and palps), not stuck to the spinnerets, and builds a tent-like nursery web for the young — wolf spiders never do either.
Huntsman spiders are flatter with long crab-like legs held out to the sides, and lack the two enlarged middle eyes — their eyes are smaller and more even in size.

Ground/stealthy spiders also run on soil, but they have small, similar-sized eyes (no big middle pair) and prominent cylindrical, often widely spaced spinnerets sticking out behind — no enlarged forward eyes.

Jumping spiders also have two huge front eyes, but those eyes are an enormous pair facing straight forward on a short, square 'face', the body is small and compact, and they jump rather than run on open ground.

source.title: Spiders of Europe — interactive determination key, Family Lycosidae Sundevall, 1833 (genus-level key)
source.authors: Nentwig W, Blick T, Bosmans R, Kropf C, Stäubli A

deeper_key.notes: araneae.nmbe.ch provides a genus-level dichotomous key for Lycosidae (about 25 genera keyed in the main run, with further genera listed). It gets you reliably to GENUS but most steps lean on genitalia (male pedipalp median apophysis/embolus/tegular processes, female epigyne) examined under a stereomicroscope — these are NOT field characters. Some early splits use visible traits (body size, carapace profile, eye-size ratios, leg/tibia spine counts, presence/absence of a bright median band, cheliceral tooth number), but precise tooth counts and spine pairs still want magnification. Below genus, the great majority of the 58 Portuguese Lycosidae can only be identified to SPECIES from adult genitalia under a scope, often needing a regional revision (e.g. Roberts, or Bosmans' North-African/Mediterranean keys) — juveniles frequently cannot be named at all. For the user's two species: Lycosa tarantula and Hogna radiata are large and reasonably distinctive (Lycosa keys out at couplet 3 by its hook-shaped median apophysis and distinct pedicel; Hogna at couplet 23 via 3 cheliceral teeth and elongated opisthosoma with one palea process), but confident species naming still rests on genitalia and locality. Couplets below are transcribed from the live key; the genus endpoints are exact, the character wording is condensed.

couplets.character: Carapace profile and body size

options.text: Thorax gradually descending behind the head
Steep drop in the region behind the fovea

couplets.character: Width of eye rows; tarsal scopulae/spinules

options.text: Second (posterior) eye row wider; scopulae plus spinules present
Eye rows about equal; dense scopulae only

couplets.character: Shape of median apophysis; pedicel

options.text: Hook-shaped median apophysis; distinct pedicel
Wide median apophysis; monotypic genus

couplets.character: Eye diameter ratio; apophysis form

options.text: Eye ratio 1.3-1.6
Eye ratio about 0.7

couplets.character: Opisthosoma marking; tegulum

options.text: No cardiac mark; deep tegular hollow
Cardiac mark present; no hollow

couplets.character: Spine pairs on metatarsus I

options.text: 3-4 pairs of spines
2 pairs of spines

couplets.character: Palp/patella colour; body size

options.text: White patellae; body up to about 4 mm
Patellae not white; body over 4 mm

couplets.character: Median band on carapace

options.text: No median band
Bright median band present

couplets.character: Leg pattern; body size

options.text: Legs spotted/annulated; under 15 mm
Legs unspotted; 15-18 mm

couplets.character: Tibia spine pairs; coloration/leg length

options.text: 4-7 spine pairs; long legs
Up to 3 spine pairs, or clear pattern

couplets.character: Cephalic pattern; AME vs ALE eye size

options.text: Two dark bars on head; anterior median eyes larger than anterior lateral
Forked head marking; AME about equal to ALE

couplets.character: Sides of head; eye-size ratio; body size

options.text: Head sides vertical; eye ratio 0.7 or less
Head sides sloping; eye ratio 0.7 or more

couplets.character: Number of cheliceral teeth

options.text: 3 teeth
2 teeth

couplets.character: Opisthosoma form; palea processes

options.text: Globular opisthosoma; two palea processes
Elongated opisthosoma; one palea process

couplets.character: Synembolus and median apophysis form

options.text: Synembolus reduced to a prominence
Synembolus well-developed

records.found_check: Both user-photographed species are confirmed Lycosidae on araneae.nmbe.ch and fit every family character above. Lycosa tarantula: large burrowing wolf spider (female to ~30 mm, male ~19 mm), earth-toned, ground-dwelling, lives in a silk-lined burrow — matches the no-web, ground-hunter, burrow, and 4-2-2 eye traits. Hogna radiata: confirmed Lycosidae (genus Hogna), female 12.5-25 mm and male 9-18 mm, with exactly the described carapace pattern (broad cream-to-orange median stripe with dark radiating lines, dark cardiac mark on abdomen, eyes circled in black), in warm dry grassland/open woodland — a textbook fit for the colour and habitat characters. The size range '4-30' was widened on the lower end to cover small Portuguese genera (e.g. Aulonia ~4 mm, small Pardosa) while the upper end (30 mm) is set by Lycosa tarantula females. Both species key out cleanly in the transcribed genus key (Lycosa at couplet 3, Hogna at couplet 23).

characters.web: Builds a large, flat, horizontal SHEET of dry (non-sticky) silk with a tubular FUNNEL retreat opening at one edge (or sometimes the middle) where the spider waits head-out. The sheet is dense and conspicuous, often spanning grass, low shrubs, rock crevices, wall corners, sheds and house interiors. The spider does not hang in the web upside-down; it sits at the funnel mouth and dashes out across the top of the sheet when prey vibrates it.
characters.eyes: Eight eyes, all similar in size and roughly pearly/dark, arranged in two horizontal rows of four (4 over 4). The head end of the body is narrowed toward the eyes. No dramatically enlarged front pair (unlike jumping spiders) and not a wide spread of just a few big eyes. The 4+4 layout is hard to read on a phone photo without a macro shot, so the spinnerets and web are usually more reliable in the field.
characters.body_shape: Longish, fairly flat-bodied spider with an oval abdomen that tapers slightly to the rear. Cephalothorax (front body) is pear-shaped, narrowing toward the eyes. The standout family feature is a pair of long, finger-like SPINNERETS sticking out clearly from the tip of the abdomen, the rear pair about twice as long as the front pair and often visible as two little tails in a phone photo. Body length (legs excluded) ranges from tiny to large; the biggest house species are bulky.
characters.legs: Long, fairly slender, hairy legs that make the spider look bigger and faster than its body suggests; front legs (leg I) are about as long as or clearly longer than the body. Legs usually bear visible spines and are often faintly banded or ringed. Fast runners across the funnel sheet.
characters.colour: Mostly browns, tans, greys and dull yellow-browns. The cephalothorax frequently shows two darker longitudinal stripes (a paler central band between them); the abdomen typically carries two rows of paler chevrons/spots or a herringbone pattern down the midline. Some (e.g. Agelena) are more strongly marked with pale and dark bands; some house species (Eratigena/Tegenaria) are plainer mottled brown.
characters.behaviour_habitat: Funnel-web sitters, not roaming hunters. Outdoors: Agelena labyrinthica spins sheet-and-funnel webs in grass, heather, brambles and low vegetation, often dew-covered and obvious on summer mornings. Indoors and around buildings: Eratigena and Tegenaria (house/cardinal/giant house spiders) build funnels in wall corners, cellars, sheds, under stones and behind furniture, and males wander into houses in late summer/autumn. The spider rests at the funnel entrance and bolts back inside when disturbed.

records.common_group: funnel weavers (funnel-web / grass funnel-weaver spiders; includes house spiders and the labyrinth spider)
records.found_check: Both photographed taxa key out cleanly within this family's genus key and fit the family characters. Eratigena spp. land at the genus key's final couplet 17 (cheliceral retromargin with 6+ teeth, two-branched retrolateral tibial apophysis); these are the typical plain mottled-brown, long-legged 'house/giant house spider' type found indoors with corner funnel webs - consistent with the family characters above (4+4 eyes, long projecting spinnerets, funnel-and-sheet web, two dark stripes on the carapace). Agelena labyrinthica keys at couplet 15 (patellar protuberance absent, two tibial apophyses, short broad embolus) and is the classic outdoor grass funnel-weaver with a strongly patterned body and a large dew-catching sheet-and-funnel web in low vegetation - again a textbook fit for the family characters. No conflict found.

field_questions.ask: Is there a flat sheet of dry web with a tube/funnel retreat at one side that the spider sits in (rather than a sticky orb or a messy cobweb)?
field_questions.yes_means: Strongly points to Agelenidae - the funnel-and-sheet web is the family signature.

field_questions.ask: Can you see two short finger-like 'tails' (spinnerets) projecting from the rear tip of the abdomen in the photo?
field_questions.yes_means: Long projecting posterior spinnerets are a key Agelenidae trait (rear pair about twice the length of the front pair).

field_questions.ask: Is it a longish brown/grey spider with long hairy legs and an oval abdomen, often with two darker stripes on the front body?
field_questions.yes_means: Matches the typical funnel-weaver body form and colouring.

field_questions.ask: Was it indoors in a wall/ceiling corner, cellar or shed (likely Eratigena/Tegenaria), OR outdoors with a sheet web in grass/heather/brambles (likely Agelena)?
field_questions.yes_means: Both habitats are classic Agelenidae; helps split house funnel-weavers from the outdoor labyrinth/grass funnel-weavers.

lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Wolf spiders are free-living ground hunters with NO funnel web and a distinctive eye pattern of two big forward-facing eyes plus a back row of large eyes (not the agelenid 4+4 rows of similar small eyes). If it has a funnel sheet web and small evenly-sized eyes, it is Agelenidae, not Lycosidae.

Similar long-legged brown look, but pisaurids do not build a sheet-and-funnel snare and females carry the egg-sac in the jaws then build a nursery tent. Presence of a permanent funnel web points to Agelenidae.

These also make sheet webs and can look very similar. The clearest naked-eye/photo cue is the conspicuously LONG projecting posterior spinnerets of Agelenidae; in the related families the spinnerets are much shorter and barely project (Hahniidae even have spinnerets in a single transverse row). Reliable separation often needs a microscope.

Lace-weavers build a bluish, woolly (cribellate) lace-like web around a crevice rather than a smooth dry sheet with a clean funnel, and lack the long projecting spinnerets. Web texture (woolly/lacy vs flat sheet) is the easiest tell.

source.title: Spiders of Europe - Interactive genus key for Agelenidae
source.authors: Nentwig W, Blick T, Bosmans R, Gloor D, Hänggi A, Kropf C (araneae.nmbe.ch)

deeper_key.notes: araneae.nmbe.ch provides a working online dichotomous key that reaches GENUS for European Agelenidae (it resolves 18 primary genera and lists 7 further genera, 25 total). It does NOT go to species online. Critically, nearly every couplet relies on microscope-only genitalic characters - male palp structures (patellar/tibial apophyses, conductor shape, embolus form) and the female epigyne/vulva - plus fine features like cheliceral tooth counts and trochanter notching. So even reaching the correct genus from a field photo is usually NOT possible; you can often only say 'Agelenidae' in the field. The two genera most relevant to Portugal's house fauna, Eratigena and Tegenaria, are split ONLY by cheliceral teeth and palp/tibial-apophysis details under magnification (couplet 17). For species (Portugal has ~25 species across genera such as Eratigena, Tegenaria, Histopona, Textrix, Agelena, Allagelena, Malthonica) there is no reliable online dichotomous key; species ID typically requires an adult specimen and examination of genitalia under a stereomicroscope against primary taxonomic literature (e.g. Bolzern, Burckhardt & Hänggi 2013, Zool. J. Linn. Soc., revising the Tegenaria-Malthonica complex). Treat anything below family from a photo as tentative.

couplets.character: Prosoma proportions, leg build, presence of male palp patellar apophysis / cymbial furrow, epigynal teeth

options.text: Prosoma in eye region not much narrower than behind; relatively stout legs; male palp with patellar apophysis (lost in Inermocoelotes); cymbium with lateral furrow; distinct epigynal teeth anteriorly/lateromedially
Prosoma in eye region distinctly narrower than behind; relatively long slender legs; male palp rarely with patellar apophysis; cymbium never with lateral furrow

couplets.character: Patellar apophysis; epigynal anterior atrial margin

options.text: Patellar apophysis absent; anterior atrial margin without posterior extension
Patellar apophysis present; anterior atrial margin with posterior extension

couplets.character: Conductor shape; epigynal atrium size

options.text: Conductor strongly elongated, broad, flat, spiral with 1-2 loops; epigynal atrium large
Conductor short; epigynal atrium small

couplets.character: Patellar apophysis width; embolus; epigynal teeth; copulatory ducts

options.text: Patellar apophysis broad; embolus slender; epigynal teeth normal length; copulatory ducts short
Patellar apophysis slender; embolus wide; epigynal teeth strongly elongated; copulatory ducts large

couplets.character: Notching of trochanters III and IV

options.text: Trochanter III and IV notched
All trochanters straight or only slightly curved

couplets.character: Spines on patellae and tarsi III/IV

options.text: Dorsal AND lateral spines on patellae III & IV; tarsus IV with 1-2 ventral spines
Only dorsal spines on all patellae

couplets.character: Colulus development; male palp patellar apophysis; median apophysis

options.text: Colulus a medially slightly divided plate (sometimes two hairy plates); palp patellar apophysis sometimes present; median apophysis absent
Colulus strongly reduced (only hairs); palp patellar apophysis absent; median apophysis present

couplets.character: Eye development; dorsal tarsal trichobothria count; cheliceral retromargin teeth

options.text: Eyes fully developed; tarsi with <7 dorsal trichobothria; cheliceral retromargin with 1-2 larger + several smaller teeth
Eyes very small or lacking; tarsi with 7+ dorsal trichobothria; several equally large retromarginal teeth (cave-adapted)

couplets.character: Curvature of eye rows; patellar spines; colulus form

options.text: Posterior eye row considerably recurved/procurved, anterior row procurved or slightly recurved; patellae I-II with dorsal AND prolateral spines; colulus clearly two hairy plates
Both eye rows more or less straight (eyes may be reduced); patellae with dorsal spines only; colulus a trapezoidal plate

couplets.character: Direction of eye-row curvature; size of posterior median eyes; feathery hairs

options.text: Eye rows recurved (dorsal view); posterior median eyes largest; feathery hairs absent or unlike Tegenaria s.l.
Eye rows procurved; posterior median eyes never largest; feathery hairs present

couplets.character: Conductor laterodorsal projection; palp femur prominence; epigynal atrium

options.text: Conductor with conspicuous laterodorsal projection; palp femur with flat prominence; no patellar apophysis; epigyne with large atrium
Conductor without laterodorsal projection; femoral apophysis absent; patellar apophysis sometimes present; epigyne with posterior pocket or raised median portion

couplets.character: Conductor complexity; median/patellar apophysis; epigyne form

options.text: Conductor simple; median apophysis absent; patellar apophysis absent; epigyne with posteriorly protruding pocket
Conductor more complex; median apophysis present; patellar apophysis present; epigyne with slightly raised median portion

couplets.character: Anal tubercle spikes; conductor helical or not; epigynal division

options.text: No spikes at anal tubercle; conductor helical, strongly protruding; epigynal plate with 2 clearly divided copulatory openings
Anal tubercle with long dark spikes; conductor not helical; epigynal plate not completely divided longitudinally

couplets.character: Embolus form; conductor complexity; epigynal scapus

options.text: Embolus thin and filamentous; conductor simple; epigynal atrium with protruding scapus
Embolus broad/short or spiral with membrane; conductor complex; no scapus

couplets.character: Patellar protuberance; number of tibial apophyses; embolus; spermathecal head/apophyses

options.text: Patellar protuberance absent; two tibial apophyses; embolus short and broad; spermathecal head interior; spermathecal apophyses present
Patellar protuberance with long spine; one tibial apophysis; embolus long and spiral with membrane; spermathecal head lateral/medial; spermathecal apophyses absent

couplets.character: Length of conductor apex; epigyne/vulva

options.text: Extraordinarily long apex/terminal end of conductor; epigyne and vulva as figured
Different

couplets.character: Cheliceral retromargin tooth number; retrolateral tibial apophysis branching; conductor form

options.text: Cheliceral retromargin with 6+ teeth (decreasing proximally); retrolateral tibial apophysis mostly two-branched; lateroventral ridge absent; conductor with membranous/massive transversal ridge
Cheliceral retromargin with 3-6 large subequal teeth; retrolateral tibial apophysis mostly three-branched or with strong bulge + lateroventral ridge; conductor lamelliform

characters.web: Orb web with an OPEN hub — a horizontal-to-tilted wheel-shaped web with a hole or open gap in the centre instead of a tight silk button. Often spun low over water, on reeds/grasses (Tetragnatha), or in dark damp places like cave mouths, wells, drains and tunnels (Meta). Pachygnatha adults build no web and roam in low vegetation/leaf litter.
characters.eyes: Eight eyes of fairly similar size in two roughly straight rows of four; eyes spread fairly wide apart, not bunched. (Useful family clue more than a species clue, and hard to see without a macro photo.)
characters.body_shape: Two clear body sections. In the classic genus Tetragnatha (stretch spiders) the abdomen is very long, narrow and almost cigar-shaped, and the spider holds its legs stretched out front-and-back along a grass stem so it looks like a sliver. In Meta/Metellina the abdomen is shorter and rounded-oval, more like a typical orb-weaver. Pachygnatha has a stout rounded abdomen.
characters.legs: Long and slender, especially the front pair; legspan can far exceed body length (a 15 mm Meta menardi can span ~50 mm). Legs carry obvious spines in Meta/Metellina; Tetragnatha legs look smoother; Pachygnatha legs are nearly spineless. Often held outstretched in a straight line in Tetragnatha.
characters.colour: Variable. Tetragnatha typically shiny silvery-green to yellow-brown with a metallic, often silvered elongated belly and faint dark stripes. Meta/Metellina are brown to reddish-brown or olive, the abdomen frequently with a darker leaf-like or fiddle/heart-shaped central mark and pale flecks; Meta menardi looks satiny dark brown to black with a contrasting paler rear and (in menardi) banded legs.
characters.behaviour_habitat: Almost always tied to damp or sheltered places. Tetragnatha sits stretched along grass and reed stems beside ponds, marshes and streams. Meta menardi and relatives live in permanent darkness or deep shade — cave entrances, mines, tunnels, cellars, wells and culverts — and hang in or beside the open-hub orb. Pachygnatha hunts at ground level in damp meadows.

field_questions.ask: Is the spider sitting on its orb web with an open hole in the very centre (no tight silk button at the middle)?
field_questions.yes_means: Open-hub orb is typical of Tetragnathidae and is the single best web clue separating them from Araneidae, whose hub is filled in.

field_questions.ask: Does it have a very long, slender, almost cigar-shaped belly and does it stretch its legs out in a straight line along a grass or reed stem near water?
field_questions.yes_means: Classic stretch-spider posture of genus Tetragnatha, the commonest Tetragnathidae you will meet in the open.

field_questions.ask: In a sharp photo of the head, are the jaws (chelicerae) noticeably long and angled outwards (especially in a slim male)?
field_questions.yes_means: Enlarged, forward-projecting, divergent jaws are the family's namesake 'long-jawed' feature, strongest in Tetragnatha.

field_questions.ask: Is it a brownish orb-weaver hanging in or just outside a cave, mine, tunnel, well or cellar in permanent shade?
field_questions.yes_means: Very likely Meta or Metellina, the dark-loving members of this family.

lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Araneidae orb webs have a CLOSED, filled-in hub (a tight silk centre) and the spider sits at the middle; Tetragnathidae leave the hub open. Araneid jaws are normal-sized, not the long divergent jaws of Tetragnatha.
Linyphiids build flat or domed SHEET webs (hammocks), never an open-hub orb, and are mostly tiny dark money-spiders with normal short jaws.
Comb-footed spiders spin an irregular tangle (cobweb), not an orb, and have a round bulbous abdomen with short legs and short jaws — no elongated belly or long jaws.
Cellar/daddy-long-legs spiders also live in dark damp spots but have extremely thin whip-like legs, a small body, a tangle web (not an orb), and no enlarged jaws; they often bounce when disturbed.

source.title: Spiders of Europe — Genus-level identification key for Tetragnathidae
source.authors: Nentwig W, Blick T, Bosmans R, Gloor D, Hänggi A, Kropf C, Stäubli A

deeper_key.notes: The araneae.nmbe.ch online tool provides a GENUS-level key only (3 short couplets, resolving Tetragnatha, Pachygnatha, Meta and Metellina); it explicitly lists further European genera that it does not key out dichotomously (Dolichognatha, Glenognatha, Zygiometella). It does not key Portuguese species within a genus. Genus is realistically the deepest a non-expert reaches in the field. Tetragnatha is the hardest and largest Portuguese genus and is notoriously a microscope-and-genitalia job: reliable species ID nearly always needs a stereo microscope to examine the male palp and the female epigyne (and, in Tetragnatha, the cheliceral teeth and the male palp's conductor), so most specimens stop at 'Tetragnatha sp.' from a photo. Meta vs Metellina and the Meta menardi / Meta bourneti pair likewise require genital examination for certainty. For species, go beyond this tool to the full Spiders of Europe species pages (araneae.nmbe.ch) and regional/genus revisions, or expect to confirm under a scope.

couplets.character: Length/slenderness of abdomen, legs and jaws

options.text: Opisthosoma (abdomen) and legs very long and thin; chelicerae long, divergent, with many teeth
Opisthosoma not prolonged; legs and chelicerae only sometimes prolonged

couplets.character: Leg spines and cheliceral build (and palp/epigyne)

options.text: Legs without spines (patella sometimes with one spine); chelicerae robust, sometimes prolonged
Legs with many spines; paracymbium bipartite; epigyne simple

couplets.character: Shape of the male palp paracymbium and of the epigyne

options.text: Dorsal part of the paracymbium very broad, distally with lobes; epigyne with a very broad groove
Both parts of the paracymbium distinctly longer than wide, distally tapering; epigyne with broad median part, lateral parts with round ends

records.found_check: Both personally photographed species fit the family characters. Tetragnatha extensa keys out at couplet 1: it has the very long thin abdomen and legs and the long, divergent, many-toothed chelicerae (araneae.nmbe.ch gives body length male 6-9 mm, female 5-12.2 mm; silvery-green/yellow-brown elongated body; lives on herbaceous plants in humid open habitats always near water — exactly the stretch-spider profile and the 'near water on a grass stem' field question). Meta menardi keys past couplets 1 and 2 (abdomen not elongated, legs spiny) to couplet 3 (broad paracymbium / broad-grooved epigyne) = Meta; it is a large (~15 mm body, ~50 mm legspan) dark brown-to-black orb-weaver of caves, tunnels and other dark damp places with an open-hub orb — matching the 'brownish orb-weaver in a cave/mine/cellar' field question and the Meta/Metellina lookalike notes. Both build orb webs with an open hub and have the elongated gnathocoxae diagnostic of the family.

characters.web: Loose, irregular, three-dimensional tangle web (a messy 'cobweb', no spiral orb, no neat sheet) built in a corner, under furniture, in cellars, sheds, caves, rock overhangs or outdoor sheltered spots. The spider hangs upside-down beneath it. When disturbed it often whirls/bounces its whole body in a blur on the web (a strong field giveaway).
characters.eyes: Eight eyes in the two common Portuguese species (Pholcus, Holocnemus): six set in two raised side-by-side groups (triads) of three, plus a tiny separate central pair in front. A few small members (Spermophora, Spermophorides) have only six eyes because that central pair is missing. Hard to count on a tiny living spider; the clumped 'two bunches of three' look is the cue.
characters.body_shape: Small body slung under hugely long legs. Two main local looks: Pholcus has a long, narrow, sausage/cylinder-shaped abdomen (longer than tall) on a pale, slightly pear-shaped front body; Holocnemus has a more oval/rounded, marbled abdomen. Front body (cephalothorax) often shows a faint central mark or groove.
characters.legs: Eight extremely long, thin, thread-like legs, roughly 5-6 times the body length, giving a 'daddy-long-legs' look. Legs are flexible and slightly knuckled at the joints, often with a darker patch at each knee; the spider stands with legs spread wide.
characters.colour: Translucent, pale: greyish, sandy, yellowish-tan or pale brown. Pholcus phalangioides is dull pale grey-brown, often with a faint greyish blotch on top of the abdomen. Holocnemus pluchei is more strongly patterned ('marbled'): pale with brown mottling on top and a dark stripe underneath the abdomen. Legs typically pale with faint darker rings at the joints.
characters.behaviour_habitat: Strongly tied to human structures (synanthropic): cellars, garages, sheds, bathrooms, ceiling corners, behind doors; also caves, rock crevices and sheltered outdoor vegetation in the warmer south. Hangs motionless upside-down under its tangle web by day; vibrates rapidly when threatened. Common and year-round indoors across Portugal.

source.title: Spiders of Europe — interactive genus-level key to Pholcidae
source.authors: Nentwig W, Blick T, Bosmans R, Gloor D, Hänggi A, Kropf C, Stäubli A

deeper_key.notes: araneae.nmbe.ch provides a working genus-level key for Pholcidae (12 numbered couplets, ~12 keyed genera plus several extra-European genera listed but not keyed). It reaches GENUS, not species. The first split (6 vs 8 eyes) and gross body size/shape are doable from a good photo, but most deeper couplets rely on microscope-only male palp structures (procursus/paracymbium flaps and processes, cheliceral apophyses and modified hairs) and female epigyne shape/pockets. So even at genus the back half of the key needs a specimen under magnification. For Portugal the realistic naked-eye targets are: long cylindrical abdomen + >4 mm body = Pholcus; marbled abdomen with dark ventral stripe and faintly spotted legs = Holocnemus pluchei. Going to SPECIES (e.g. separating the several Portuguese Pholcus, or Spermophora senoculata vs Spermophorides) almost always requires examining the male palp or female epigyne under a stereomicroscope and comparing to species pages on araneae.nmbe.ch or the World Spider Catalog. Below genus there is no reliable purely-visual field key.

couplets.character: Number of eyes

options.text: Six eyes (the central anterior median pair absent)
Eight eyes

couplets.character: Front body (prosoma) shape and eye-mound height

options.text: Prosoma with a median furrow; eye triads raised on a median turret, very high in males
Prosoma without median furrow; eye triads on very low humps

couplets.character: Male palp procursus and female epigyne pockets

options.text: Male procursus with a ventral flap; posterior epigynal plate without pockets
Male procursus without a ventral flap; posterior epigynal plate with a pair of pockets

couplets.character: Prosoma profile (domed vs furrowed/pitted)

options.text: Prosoma evenly domed, without median furrow or pit
Prosoma with a median furrow or pit

couplets.character: Body size and abdomen shape

options.text: Large (body length >4 mm) with a cylindrical opisthosoma
Small (body length 3 mm or less) with a short opisthosoma

couplets.character: Male procursus dorsal process / female external pockets

options.text: Procursus with a long dorsal process; female genitalia without external pockets
Procursus without a dorsal process; female genitalia with a pair of external pockets

couplets.character: Body size and prosoma furrow vs pit

options.text: Small (body length ~3 mm), prosoma with a median furrow
Larger (body length 5-10 mm), prosoma with a median pit

couplets.character: Abdomen height vs length

options.text: Opisthosoma higher than long
Opisthosoma longer than high

couplets.character: Leg markings

options.text: Legs with many small black spots / lines
Legs without small black spots / lines

couplets.character: Abdomen rear profile and male cheliceral apophyses

options.text: Opisthosoma pointed dorso-posteriorly; male chelicerae with two pairs of frontal apophyses
Opisthosoma rounded dorso-posteriorly; male chelicerae with a single pair of frontal apophyses

couplets.character: Male cheliceral hairs

options.text: Male chelicerae frontally with many modified (club-shaped) hairs
Male chelicerae frontally with apophyses each bearing 1-3 modified hairs

couplets.character: Male femur I spines and epigyne outline

options.text: Male femur I with ventral spines; epigynum roughly triangular
Male femur I without spines; epigynum roughly rectangular

field_questions.ask: Are the legs extremely long and thread-thin — roughly 5 or more times the length of the little body?
field_questions.yes_means: Strongly fits Pholcidae (a 'daddy-long-legs spider' build); rules out most stout, normal-legged families.

field_questions.ask: Is the web a loose, messy 3-D tangle (not a neat round orb and not a flat sheet), with the spider hanging upside-down underneath?
field_questions.yes_means: Typical Pholcidae web and posture.

field_questions.ask: When you disturb it, does it spin or bounce its whole body in a rapid blur on the web?
field_questions.yes_means: Classic Pholcidae defence behaviour — almost diagnostic in the field.

field_questions.ask: Is it indoors or in a sheltered spot (cellar, shed, ceiling corner, garage, cave or rock crevice), pale/translucent grey-tan, with a small body?
field_questions.yes_means: Matches the synanthropic, pale-bodied Pholcidae habit and colour.

records.found_check: Both photographed species key out cleanly under the family and genus characters. Pholcus phalangioides: eight eyes (couplet 1b), evenly domed prosoma (4a), large (~8-9 mm body) with a long cylindrical abdomen (5a) -> genus Pholcus; pale grey-brown, hangs in a tangle web indoors, whirls when disturbed — all consistent with the family characters above. Holocnemus pluchei: eight eyes (1b), prosoma with a median pit (4b -> 7b), larger body (8a opisthosoma longer than high), faintly spotted/marked legs (9a -> couplet 10), abdomen rounded behind with a single pair of male cheliceral apophyses (10b) -> genus Holocnemus; the marbled abdomen with a dark ventral stripe and pale faintly-banded legs matches the colour/body-shape characters. The long thin legs, irregular tangle web, upside-down posture and synanthropic habitat fit both. No contradictions.

lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Comb-footed / cobweb spiders also build irregular tangle webs, but they have a fat, rounded, bulbous abdomen and relatively shorter legs (not the thread-thin 5x-body legs), and they do not do the whole-body whirling spin when disturbed.

lookalikes.family: Pholcidae vs harvestmen (Opiliones, not true spiders)
lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Outdoor 'daddy-long-legs' harvestmen look superficially similar but have a single oval body with no waist, only two eyes on a central turret, and build no web; Pholcidae have a clear waist between front body and abdomen, multiple eyes, and live in a tangle web.

Tiny pale cave/litter spiders that can share the six-eye / clumped-eye look, but in Leptonetidae the eyes sit as a group of four with a separate pair set well behind, they are minute litter/cave dwellers, and they lack the conspicuous long sprawling legs and synanthropic tangle web of Pholcidae.

Recluse spiders also have six eyes arranged in three pairs, but they are flat-bodied, brownish, free-running ground/wall hunters with moderate legs and no hanging tangle web — unlike the long-legged, web-suspended, upside-down Pholcidae.

characters.web: Two styles, both built by females. Eresus and Adonea live in a short silk-lined burrow in the ground (a few cm deep) topped by a small shield- or funnel-shaped cribellate (woolly, hackled) web at the surface. Stegodyphus builds a dense whitish woolly cribellate sheet-and-tangle web with a tubular retreat, slung in or on low bushes and dry shrubs (often on umbellifers/Apiaceae). The silk has a fuzzy, combed (cribellate) texture rather than sticky glue droplets. Adult males wander and do not maintain a snare web.
characters.eyes: Eight small eyes spread out across the front of the head rather than bunched in a tight cluster. The side (lateral) eyes are pushed far apart toward the corners, so the eye group spans nearly the whole width of the head. The four middle eyes form a small quadrangle: in Eresus/Adonea/Loureedia it is much wider than long; in Stegodyphus it is almost as wide as long. Hard to read precisely without magnification, but the wide spread is visible in a good phone photo.
characters.body_shape: Stocky, compact and robust. Carapace (head/front body) is broad, high and somewhat squarish, often looking jumping-spider-like or even beetle-like. Abdomen is broad and rounded. Overall a chunky, not slender, spider.
characters.legs: Short, thick and strong relative to the body (not long and spindly). The hind legs bear a cribellum-and-calamistrum spinning apparatus (a special silk-combing setup) which is a family trait but needs magnification to see. In male Eresus kollari the legs are dark, ringed with white, with reddish hairs on the back pairs.
characters.colour: Densely velvety and usually dark. Females are mostly uniformly black/very dark (Eresus females velvety black, often with yellowish hairs on the head; Stegodyphus female dark red-brown carapace with rows of white hairs and a pale white-haired abdomen marked with two long black stripes). Males of Eresus are spectacular: a bright shiny red (or orange-red) abdomen with two-to-three pairs of bold black spots, like a ladybird, the rest of the body black. The whole body has a matte velvety sheen from dense hairs.
characters.behaviour_habitat: Warm, dry, sunny (xerothermic) places: open scrub, garrigue, dry grassy or stony slopes, low bushes. In Portugal concentrated in the south/southwest (Algarve, Setubal and similar). Eresus/Adonea females are sit-and-wait burrow dwellers; the ladybird-red males roam in spring looking for mates. Stegodyphus is colonial as juveniles, then adults become largely solitary in their bush webs. The spiders are generally uncommon and localised.

field_questions.ask: Is the spider very stocky and compact, with a broad high head-region and short thick legs (not long and spindly), looking almost beetle- or jumping-spider-like?
field_questions.yes_means: Consistent with Eresidae; velvet spiders are robust and short-legged, never slender.

field_questions.ask: Does the body look velvety, matte and densely furry rather than shiny and smooth?
field_questions.yes_means: Supports Eresidae; they are covered in dense velvety hairs.

field_questions.ask: Is it either (a) a roundish dark spider in a silk-lined burrow/tube in the ground in a hot dry sunny spot, or (b) a spider in a dense whitish woolly web on a low dry bush or shrub?
field_questions.yes_means: Burrow with a small shield/funnel web = Eresus or Adonea; woolly sheet web on vegetation = Stegodyphus. Both are Eresidae.

field_questions.ask: If it is a male: is the abdomen bright red or orange with several bold black spots, like a ladybird?
field_questions.yes_means: Almost certainly a male Eresus (ladybird spider), an Eresidae.

field_questions.ask: Are the eyes spread out wide across the front of the head, with the side eyes pushed far apart toward the corners, rather than bunched in a tight cluster or set as two big forward headlights?
field_questions.yes_means: Supports Eresidae and helps rule out jumping spiders (which have two huge front eyes).

lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Jumping spiders are also stocky and short-legged, but have two huge forward-facing central eyes dominating the face like headlights; Eresidae have small eyes spread wide across the head with no big front pair. Jumping spiders also hunt actively in the open with no snare web, whereas female Eresidae sit in a burrow tube or woolly bush web.

Theridiids in messy webs have a rounded, often glossy abdomen and long thin legs, and their silk has sticky glue droplets. Stegodyphus is much chunkier with short legs and builds a dry, woolly, cribellate (combed, fuzzy) web with no glue droplets.

lookalikes.family: Atypidae / Nemesiidae and other burrowing mygalomorphs (purse-web and trapdoor relatives)
lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: These burrow-dwellers have fangs that strike straight down (two parallel downward fangs) and are usually larger and glossier; Eresus has fangs that pinch sideways (crosswise) and a velvety body, and its burrow is capped by a small woolly cribellate web rather than a smooth silk purse or hinged trapdoor.

source.title: Eresidae genus key — araneae, Spiders of Europe
source.authors: Nentwig W, Blick T, Bosmans R, Gloor D, Hänggi A, Kropf C, Stäubli A

couplets.character: Shape of the median ocular quadrangle (the small square of the four middle eyes) and where the web is built

options.text: Median ocular quadrangle much wider than long; lives on the ground in a burrow inside a silken tube
Median ocular quadrangle almost as wide as long; web built in or on vegetation

couplets.character: Proportions of the cephalic (head) region, palpal conductor shape, and vulva structure (genitalia — needs a microscope)

options.text: Cephalic region wider than long; palpal conductor with a bifid (forked) process; vulva with a compact duct system and an anterior lobe (only southern Spain)
Cephalic region longer than wide; palpal conductor without a bifid process; vulva variable, without an anterior lobe

deeper_key.notes: The araneae.nmbe.ch online key is a GENUS-level key and was transcribed in full above (it is short — two couplets). It cleanly splits Stegodyphus (web on vegetation, eye quadrangle nearly square) from the ground-burrowing genera, then Loureedia (S Spain only) from Eresus using palp/vulva characters that require a microscope. Note: this key does not separately resolve Adonea, which also occurs in Portugal (A. algarvensis, an Algarve endemic) and is ground-dwelling like Eresus; distinguishing Adonea from Eresus, and identifying species WITHIN Eresus (e.g. E. kollari vs E. sedilloti) reliably requires examining the male palp or the female vulva (epigyne) under magnification, ideally with a recent revision such as Kovacs et al. or the velvet-spider atlas (Miller et al. 2012, ZooKeys). In the field you can usually reach genus and often species by sex+habitat+colour: a ladybird-red spotted male in dry scrub is almost certainly Eresus kollari (the common Iberian ladybird spider); a woolly bush web with a striped pale-and-black spider is Stegodyphus lineatus. But the honest ceiling for confident species ID across the whole family is microscope examination of genitalia from an adult specimen — naked-eye/photo ID should be treated as provisional below genus.

records.found_check: Both user-photographed species fit the family characters. Eresus kollari (araneae.nmbe.ch/data/342): female body 9-16 mm, velvety black with yellow head hairs, short robust legs, lives in a short ground burrow capped by a small shield/funnel cribellate web; male 8-11 mm with the diagnostic shiny red abdomen bearing 2-3 pairs of black spots — the classic 'ladybird spider'. Stegodyphus lineatus (araneae.nmbe.ch/data/1701): body 8-15 mm (male) / 10-23 mm (female), dark red-brown carapace with rows of white hairs, pale white-haired abdomen with two long black stripes, builds a dense woolly cribellate web on low bushes/Apiaceae in hot dry sites. Both are stocky, short-legged, velvety, with the wide-spread eye arrangement and cribellate silk that define Eresidae, and both occur in southern Portugal (Stegodyphus and Eresus kollari are both recorded from Faro/Setubal regions). The 8-23 mm family size range above brackets both species. The Portuguese fauna also includes Eresus sedilloti and the Algarve-endemic Adonea algarvensis, accounting for the 5-species total."

characters.web: Free-living nocturnal hunter, no snare web. It does not build a web to catch prey. By day it shelters in a flattened silken sac/cell it spins under a stone, log or bark; at night it roams and grabs prey (especially woodlice/isopods) by hand.
characters.eyes: Only 6 eyes (most spiders have 8). The six are clustered close together in a tight oval/horseshoe group at the front of the head, all touching or less than one eye-width apart - looks like a single small dark cluster rather than rows. This 6-eyes-in-a-tight-clump look is the headline field clue.
characters.body_shape: Long, narrow, somewhat cylindrical body. The front part (carapace/head) is fairly flat, rectangular and longer than wide; the abdomen is a smooth, soft, elongated oval (sausage-shaped) with no bold pattern. In Dysdera the jaws (chelicerae) are strikingly large and forward-projecting, ending in long curved fangs - oversized relative to the head.
characters.legs: Eight fairly long, slender, smooth legs of similar length, often with scattered spines on the rear legs. In the Dysderinae (which includes Dysdera) the leg tips have claw tufts (small adhesive hair pads) and the back metatarsi have brush-like scopulae; other dysderid genera lack tufts and show 3 claws. Not a field-separable detail without a lens.
characters.colour: Two-toned and rather plain. Front body (carapace), jaws and all legs are reddish-brown to red-orange (sometimes quite bright orange-red); the abdomen contrasts as a dull pale grey, cream, pinkish or beige with no stripes or markings. The big shiny reddish jaws stand out.
characters.behaviour_habitat: Ground-level and cryptic. Found under stones, logs, loose bark, in leaf litter and moss, and in damp dark places like cellars, caves and quarries; common in gardens. A specialist predator of woodlice (pillbugs/sowbugs), which its long fangs are built to pierce. Active at night, hides in its silk cell by day.

source.title: Dysderidae - Genus identification key (Spiders of Europe)
source.authors: Nentwig W, Blick T, Bosmans R, Gloor D, Hänggi A, Kropf C, Stäubli A

deeper_key.notes: The araneae.nmbe.ch online key reaches GENUS level for Dysderidae across ~16 European genera (Dysderinae, Harpacteinae, Rhodinae). For Portugal the genera you will realistically meet are Dysdera (by far the most species-rich here, ~30+ PT species), Harpactea, Harpactocrates, Parachtes, Dasumia and Rhode. The genus key is tractable to transcribe and the first levels are captured in couplets below. CEILING: it does NOT go to species. Reliable species ID within Portugal - especially in the large genus Dysdera - almost always requires a microscope and an adult: males are determined from the palpal bulbus shape, females from the vulva/internal genitalia (and cheliceral tooth pattern). Many Portuguese Dysdera are narrow-range Iberian endemics that are very hard or impossible to separate from photos alone; juveniles and damaged specimens often cannot be named below genus. If no species key exists for your local fauna, stop at genus and note locality, as distribution greatly narrows the candidates.

couplets.character: Claw tufts and tarsal scopulae; shape of sternum junctions (subfamily split)

options.text: All tarsi with claw tufts, rear metatarsi with scopulae; front sternum border with labial junction much wider than maxillar junction (Dysderinae)
Front tarsi without claw tufts, with 3 claws (Harpacteinae / Rhodinae)

couplets.character: Shape of male palpal bulbus

options.text: Male bulbus cylindrical
Male bulbus globular

couplets.character: Cheliceral teeth, labium and eye characters

options.text: 3-4 cheliceral teeth in one row (plus matching labium/eye characters)
Other combination

couplets.character: Cheliceral tooth arrangement

options.text: Chelicerae with 4 teeth in two rows
Chelicerae with 3 promargin and 2 retromargin teeth

couplets.character: Detail of globular bulbus

options.text: Simple bulbus with elongated distal section
Bulbus with 2 subapical apophyses

couplets.character: Sternum junctions and chitinous pattern (subfamily split within 3-clawed dysderids)

options.text: Sternum front edge with equal junctions, particular chitinous pattern (Harpacteinae)
Wider labial junction with continuous chitinous strips (Rhodinae)

couplets.character: Sternum surface and bulbus

options.text: Dull sternum with characteristic bulbus
Other

couplets.character: Eye arrangement and sternum (within Harpacteinae)

options.text: Eyes forming a circle, shining sternum
Different eye/sternum arrangement

couplets.character: Leg spination and prosoma shape (Rhodinae)

options.text: Spineless legs with a central prosoma lobe
Other; distal bulbus tooth/spine rows separate Mesostalita/Stalita/Parastalita from Stalitella

field_questions.ask: Does it have only 6 eyes bunched in a tight little cluster (not 8 eyes in rows)?
field_questions.yes_means: Strongly points to Dysderidae or another six-eyed family (Oonopidae, Segestriidae, Sicariidae) - then use the other clues.

field_questions.ask: Is the front body, jaws and all the legs reddish-brown/orange while the abdomen is a plain pale grey or cream?
field_questions.yes_means: Classic two-tone of a woodlouse hunter (Dysdera/dysderid); few other spiders show this exact contrast.

field_questions.ask: Are the jaws (fangs) noticeably big and projecting forward, oversized for the head?
