field_questions.yes_means: Typical of Dysdera, the most likely woodlouse-hunter you will find; built for piercing woodlice.

field_questions.ask: Did you find it on the ground under a stone/log/bark or in litter, with no web around it?
field_questions.yes_means: Consistent with a free-living dysderid hunter rather than a web-builder.

records.found_check: Test case Dysdera crocata fits every family character above. Per its araneae.nmbe.ch page it has the dysderid 6-eye configuration, a red-brown prosoma and stout red-brown chelicerae with long fangs, a plain abdomen, an elongated body, builds no web, and lives under stones/logs/litter and in cellars - exactly the free-living woodlouse-hunter profile. It keys into the Dysderinae (claw tufts + scopulae) and then to genus Dysdera via the cylindrical male bulbus and a single row of 3-4 cheliceral teeth (couplets 1-3). The only family character that is genus-specific rather than universal is the very enlarged forward jaws, which is a Dysdera hallmark and matches the test specimen.

lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Also have 6 eyes and an elongated body, but their first THREE pairs of legs point distinctly forward and they DO make a silk tube with radiating trip-lines in a crack or hole - dysderids make no such snare and hold legs normally.
Tiny six-eyed spiders under ~2-3 mm, often with a hardened plate on the abdomen; dysderids are bigger (>3 mm, usually much larger) and reddish two-toned.

Six eyes too, but arranged in 3 widely separated pairs (not one tight clump), legs long and uniformly tan, no oversized forward jaws, and a violin mark on the carapace; dysderids have a tight eye cluster and big red jaws.
Superficially similar plain ground-living hunters, but they have 8 eyes - counting eyes immediately rules them out as Dysderidae.

characters.web: Free-living hunter, no snare web. Zodariids do not build a capture web at all; they are active ground hunters. Some Zodarion build a small flask- or igloo-shaped retreat of silk and sand grains under a stone to moult or guard eggs, but nothing you would catch prey in.
characters.eyes: Eight small eyes (one Portuguese genus, Trygetus, is not present here; all Portuguese species have 8) clustered close together on the front of the head. In Zodarion the eyes sit in a compact roughly heart-shaped group with the front-middle pair (anterior median eyes) the largest. They are dark and easy to overlook because the whole eye group is tiny on such a small spider. Not the big forward pair-of-headlights look of a jumping spider.
characters.body_shape: Small and ant-like. The abdomen often has a narrow waist-like constriction near the front and frequently a hardened shiny plate (scutum) on its upper surface, giving a segmented, ant-mimic silhouette. Carapace fairly high and rounded. The overall jizz is a tiny, fast, glossy creature that genuinely looks like an ant scurrying on open ground.
characters.legs: Eight legs, fairly long and slender for the body. Classic field giveaway: the spider often holds the front pair of legs raised and waves them like an ant's antennae while it moves. Legs frequently paler (yellow to yellow-brown) than the body. No greatly thickened/swollen front femur (that would point to the lookalike family Palpimanidae).
characters.colour: Typically a two-tone, fairly glossy look. Carapace and legs often yellow to orange- or red-brown; abdomen darker - brown, violet-brown or blackish, sometimes with paler sides or pale spots/bands. In Zodarion alentejanum the carapace, chelicerae and legs are yellow while the abdomen is brown above with pale sides and underside.
characters.behaviour_habitat: Ground-dwelling, day-active hunters of warm, dry, open places: sandy soil, coastal dunes, garrigue/scrubland, stony ground, pine woodland edges, found running on bare ground or sheltering under stones and in leaf litter. The hallmark behaviour is ant-eating (myrmecophagy): they live near ant trails and nests, mimic ants in size, colour and antennal-waving movement, and prey on them. Strongly associated with the Mediterranean climate of southern and central Portugal.

field_questions.ask: Is it a small spider (under about 5 mm body) that genuinely looks like a glossy ant running on open dry ground?
field_questions.yes_means: Strongly consistent with Zodariidae (Zodarion), the dominant ant-mimic group in Portugal.

field_questions.ask: Does it raise and wave its front pair of legs like an ant's antennae as it moves?
field_questions.yes_means: Classic Zodarion (Zodariidae) ant-mimic behaviour.

field_questions.ask: Is there NO catching web - it is hunting actively on the ground, often near ants, stones or sand?
field_questions.yes_means: Fits Zodariidae, which are free-living hunters with no snare web.

field_questions.ask: Are the eight eyes a tiny tight cluster on the front of the head (not two big forward-facing headlight eyes)?
field_questions.yes_means: Consistent with Zodariidae and rules out jumping spiders (Salticidae).

lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Salticids have two enormous forward-facing front eyes (the headlight pair). Zodariids have eight small eyes in a tiny tight cluster and no big front eyes.

Gnaphosids have long, clearly cylindrical, well-separated spinnerets sticking out at the rear; in Zodariidae only one pair of spinnerets is clearly visible (the rest reduced). Gnaphosids are also not ant-shaped and do not antennae-wave.

These also mimic ants, but Zodarion typically shows a hardened dorsal scutum on a glossy abdomen and the constant front-leg antennal-waving; check the eye/spinneret combination and the heart-shaped Zodarion eye group.

Palpimanids have a strongly thickened/swollen front femur (leg I) and a serrula on the chelicera; Zodariidae have normal slender front legs and no serrula. This is the direct alternative at couplet 9 of the family key.

source.title: araneae - Spiders of Europe: Determination key for the genera of the family Zodariidae
source.authors: Nentwig W, Blick T, Bosmans R, Gloor D, Hänggi A, Kropf C, Stäubli A

couplets.character: Chilum (small sclerite between the cheliceral bases)

options.text: Chilum absent
Chilum present

couplets.character: Number of eyes

options.text: Six eyes, anterior median eyes enlarged, clypeus high
Eight eyes

couplets.character: Eye field shape and cheliceral bases

options.text: Anterior median eyes largest, eye field heart-shaped, cheliceral bases fused
Anterior eyes larger than posterior, cheliceral teeth present, clypeus high

couplets.character: Chilum morphology

options.text: Chilum composed of two triangular parts, clypeus low
Chilum single

couplets.character: Relative size of lateral vs median eyes

options.text: Lateral eyes larger than median eyes, clypeus high
Eyes of similar size

couplets.character: Chilum shape and clypeus height

options.text: Chilum wide, clypeus low
Chilum single and triangular, clypeus high

deeper_key.notes: The araneae.nmbe.ch genus key (6 couplets) cleanly reaches GENUS and is realistic in the field for the Portuguese fauna: of its terminal genera, only Zodarion, Selamia and Amphiledorus occur in Portugal, and the great majority of the 28 Portuguese species are Zodarion. The key characters (chilum presence/shape, clypeus height, exact eye-field shape and relative eye sizes) are very small structures that in practice need a good macro photo or a hand lens/microscope, but they are not genitalic. Getting BELOW genus to a named species is the hard ceiling: Zodarion species are separated almost entirely by genitalia - the male palp (embolus, retinaculum, branch shape) and the female epigyne/vulva - examined under a microscope, often only after clearing. Many records are female- or juvenile-based and cannot be named to species at all, and several Portuguese Zodarion (e.g. Z. alentejanum) were described from one sex only. So honestly: family and genus are achievable from good photos plus behaviour; species ID generally requires an adult of the right sex and microscopic genitalic study, and for some specimens species-level ID is simply not possible. For species-level work consult Pekar et al. 2011 (Zootaxa 2814) and the 2025 integrative revision of the Iberian rubidum and styliferum groups (Zootaxa 5624).

records.found_check: Zodarion alentejanum (the user's photographed species) fits the family characters cleanly: it is a small ground-dwelling ant-hunting Zodarion (female 3.3-4.1 mm), builds no snare web, has a yellow carapace/chelicerae/legs with a brown abdomen and pale sides, and lives in dry sandy Mediterranean habitats of the Alentejo - all matching the Zodariidae profile and the heart-shaped Zodarion eye-field route of the genus key (couplet 3 -> Zodarion). Note: it was described from females only, which reinforces the deeper_key caution that species-level confirmation in this group often hinges on having the right sex and microscopic genitalia.

characters.web: Free-living nocturnal hunter, no snare web. By day it hides in a dense flattened silk sac/retreat (looks like a small white cocoon or rolled leaf) spun in folded leaves, grass-head curls, under bark, or in house corners and behind picture frames. The silk tube, not a catching web, is the structure you see.
characters.eyes: Eight small eyes, all roughly the same size, set in two gently curved rows of four across the front of the head. There is no big-eyed staring pair as in jumping or wolf spiders. Hard to count without a phone macro photo.
characters.body_shape: Soft, smooth, slightly shiny body. The front body section (cephalothorax) is pear-shaped and a bit raised; the abdomen is a plump, smooth oval. No spines, no obvious patterning bumps. The chelicerae (jaws) are long and project forward, especially in males, ending in long needle-like black fangs that are often the darkest, most noticeable feature.
characters.legs: Long, slender, pale legs. The FIRST (front) pair of legs is clearly the longest - noticeably longer than the second pair - which is a key family trait. Leg tips (tarsi) and fang tips are often darkened, contrasting with the pale legs. Legs point neither strongly outward nor inward.
characters.colour: Pale and washed-out overall: straw-yellow, cream, pale green, beige or pinkish. Front body often yellow-brown to orange or reddish. Abdomen pale yellow to greenish, sometimes with a faint darker streak (cardiac mark) down the front-centre, sometimes plain. The dark fangs and dark leg/foot tips stand out against the pale body.
characters.behaviour_habitat: Active hunters at night in the herb and shrub layer of warm, open habitats - grassland, scrub, meadows, gardens, field margins - and frequently indoors on walls and ceilings. They run fast when disturbed. Cheiracanthium punctorium in particular is large and can deliver a painful (medically minor but locally sore) bite if handled; the silk nursery sac guards the eggs. Often found by turning leaves or spotting the white silk retreat.

source.title: araneae - Spiders of Europe: Genus key for Cheiracanthiidae
source.authors: Nentwig W, Blick T, Bosmans R, Kropf C, Stäubli A

deeper_key.notes: The araneae.nmbe.ch genus key for this family is minimal: in Europe it is effectively a single lead keying out Cheiracanthium (by male pedipalp cymbium-with-spur / tibia length and by the broad-median epigyne with a dark sclerotised arc), while three further genera - Cheiraignotum, Cheiramiona, Eutichurus - are merely LISTED, not separated by diagnostic couplets, because they are not part of the European fauna (Afrotropical/Neotropical). For Portugal this is fully tractable: all 16 Portuguese species belong to the single genus Cheiracanthium, so genus identification is automatic once the family is confirmed. Going DEEPER to species is NOT possible in the field. Cheiracanthium species are notoriously similar in body colour and pattern, and reliable species identification almost always requires examining genitalia under a microscope - the shape of the male palpal tibial apophysis and embolus, and the female epigyne/vulva (e.g. C. punctorium males show diverging chelicerae and a tibial apophysis with equally long branches). Adult, intact specimens are needed; juveniles and damaged specimens often cannot be named to species at all. If no species key is workable, record it as Cheiracanthium sp. with a clear macro photo of the epigyne or palp and note size, colour and locality.

couplets.character: Male pedipalp and female epigyne structure (the only diagnostic lead given; the genus is the European endpoint)

options.text: Cymbium with spur; tibia of the male pedipalp about as long as the cymbium (measured without the spur) or longer; epigyne with a broad median part, often with a dark sclerotised arc
Further genera listed without diagnostic leads (not in the European/Portuguese fauna): Cheiraignotum, Cheiramiona, Eutichurus
options.leads_to: Cheiraignotum / Cheiramiona / Eutichurus (extralimital - not separable in this European key)

field_questions.ask: Is the spider pale and plain - straw-yellow, cream, pale green or beige - with no bold pattern?
field_questions.yes_means: Consistent with a yellow sac spider; rules out boldly patterned orb-weavers and crab spiders.

field_questions.ask: Are there long, dark, needle-like fangs that stick out forward, looking darker than the pale body?
field_questions.yes_means: Strong Cheiracanthium trait - the projecting dark chelicerae/fangs on a pale body are characteristic.

field_questions.ask: Is the front pair of legs clearly the longest, longer than the others?
field_questions.yes_means: Matches the family character (leg I considerably longer than leg II).

field_questions.ask: Did you find it in a small flattened white silk sac/tube (in a rolled leaf, grass head, or a ceiling/wall corner) rather than in a catching web?
field_questions.yes_means: Confirms the sac-spider lifestyle - a silk retreat, not a snare web.

field_questions.ask: Are the eight eyes all small and similar in size, in two rows, with no large forward-facing pair?
field_questions.yes_means: Rules out jumping spiders, wolf spiders and other big-eyed hunters.

records.found_check: Cheiracanthium punctorium fits the family characters well and was used as the test case. Per araneae.nmbe.ch it is large (female 10.0-15.0 mm, male 7.5-12.0 mm), lives in the herbaceous and bush layer of warm open habitats, and the male shows diverging chelicerae and a palpal tibial apophysis with equally long branches - consistent with the family's projecting jaws, pale body and long front legs. Its prosoma is greenish-brown to orange/red and the pale yellow-green abdomen may carry a faint dark cardiac streak, matching the pale-with-dark-fangs description above. It builds a silk nursery sac and is the species most often cited for a locally painful bite, consistent with the behaviour note.

lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: The closest look-alike - also pale, smooth, sac-living, eight similar eyes. In Clubionidae leg I and leg II are about equal and the posterior spinnerets are not conspicuously conical; in Cheiracanthiidae leg I is clearly the longest and the posterior spinnerets are two-segmented with a distinctly conical distal segment. The very long projecting dark fangs are far more pronounced in Cheiracanthium. Often needs a hand lens/microscope to be sure.

lookalikes.family: Eutichuridae / Miturgidae (prowling and related sac-like hunters)
lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Historically Cheiracanthium was placed in Miturgidae, so they are easily confused; both are pale nocturnal hunters. The exaggerated long forward-pointing chelicerae plus leg I being distinctly longest point to Cheiracanthiidae; many separations are genitalic and need a microscope.

Also pale and fast-running, but Anyphaenidae have the tracheal spiracle pushed well forward toward the middle of the underside of the abdomen (not just in front of the spinnerets) - a feature visible only under magnification but diagnostic.

Crab spiders also sit on vegetation and can be pale, but they hold their first two leg pairs sideways like a crab and walk sideways, and they spin no silk sac retreat; sac spiders run forward and shelter in a silk tube.

characters.web: Builds a silk-lined tube retreat deep in a crack, hole, or crevice (in walls, rock, bark, or mortar). From the tube mouth radiate roughly six or more straight silk trip-lines fanning across the surface; there is no flat or orb-shaped catching web. The spider sits at the tube entrance, front legs touching the lines, and rushes out when prey trips one. Look for a neat round or slit-like hole with a fan of threads around it, often on a warm south-facing wall.
characters.eyes: Six eyes (not eight), arranged in three pairs in a tight forward-facing cluster forming a short curved/semicircular row. Hard to count without a phone macro shot, but the absence of a wide-spread 8-eye pattern is the giveaway.
characters.body_shape: Elongate, somewhat cylindrical and tube-shaped body well suited to living head-out in a narrow burrow. Cephalothorax (front body) longish and slightly narrowed at the front; abdomen long and roughly oval. Overall sleek and stretched-looking rather than rounded or squat.
characters.legs: Eight legs, sturdy and not especially slender. The diagnostic feature: the first THREE pairs of legs point forwards (most spiders point only the first two pairs forward), so the spider looks like it is reaching ahead with six legs while only the back pair trails behind. In Segestria florentina the front legs (leg I) are notably darkened/black while the rear legs are more reddish-brown.
characters.colour: Dark and fairly plain: grey, dark brown to black, with little or no bold patterning on the front body. The big species Segestria florentina is blackish, often with a purplish sheen on the abdomen and a striking metallic green iridescence on the chelicerae (jaws/fangs) — best seen in good light or a flash photo. Smaller Segestria/Ariadna are duller grey-brown, sometimes with faint paler markings on the abdomen.
characters.behaviour_habitat: Sit-and-wait ambush hunter that almost never leaves its tube; mostly active at dusk and night. Lives in holes and crevices of stone walls, brickwork, mortar, rock faces, under bark and stones, and in and around buildings — common in towns and on old walls. Synanthropic species (especially S. florentina) are frequent on warm urban walls.

field_questions.ask: Does the spider live head-out in a silk-lined tube in a crack or hole, with several straight silk threads radiating out around the entrance (and no flat or orb web)?
field_questions.yes_means: Classic Segestriidae tube-and-trip-line setup; rules out web-builders that make sheets or orbs.

field_questions.ask: Do THREE pairs of legs (six legs) point forwards, with only the back pair trailing behind?
field_questions.yes_means: Strongly indicates Segestriidae — almost all other spiders point only the first two leg pairs forward (e.g. the similar six-eyed Dysderidae point only two pairs forward).

field_questions.ask: Are there six eyes (not eight) bunched together near the front of the head?
field_questions.yes_means: Narrows it to the small set of six-eyed families (Segestriidae, Dysderidae, Oonopidae, Sicariidae), which the leg posture and tube then separate.

field_questions.ask: Is the body dark grey/brown/black and plain, with the jaws (chelicerae) showing a metallic green shine in good light?
field_questions.yes_means: Points specifically to Segestria florentina, the large urban-wall tube spider in Portugal.

lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Also six-eyed and dark, but they are FREE-ROAMING ground hunters with no tube-and-radiating-threads web, and they hold only the first TWO pairs of legs forward, not three. Dysdera also has very large outward-projecting fangs and a reddish leg/orange-red carapace look.

Also live in wall/rock crevices with radiating silk lines, but they spin a lacy/woolly cribellate web mat (fuzzy bluish silk) around the hole rather than a few clean trip-lines, and they have eight eyes clustered on a central mound, not six eyes with three pairs of forward legs.
These also retreat into tubes/funnels in crevices, but they build a conspicuous flat sheet or lacy web at the tube mouth, have eight eyes, and hold legs in the normal way (only two pairs forward).

source.title: Araneae - Spiders of Europe: genus-level identification key for Segestriidae
source.authors: Nentwig W, Blick T, Bosmans R, Gloor D, Hänggi A, Kropf C, Stäubli A

deeper_key.notes: The araneae.nmbe.ch online key for Segestriidae goes only to GENUS (two genera in Europe: Segestria and Ariadna), separated by a single eye-arrangement character (see couplets). Genus is NOT reachable in the field with the naked eye — it needs a magnified, well-lit view of the posterior eye row, and even then a macro photo is borderline. Below genus, species-level ID is generally a microscope job: it relies on adult genitalia (male palp embolus shape, female epigyne/internal structures) plus leg-spination details, so most specimens cannot be named to species from a field photo. Practical exceptions in Portugal: the large size (female to ~22 mm) plus metallic green chelicerae and black front legs make Segestria florentina identifiable in the field with reasonable confidence; S. senoculata often shows a row of dark leaf-/diamond-shaped marks down the abdomen. For the other ~8 Portuguese species (further Segestria and Ariadna), expect to need a collected adult specimen under a microscope, or to leave the ID at family/genus. The araneae.nmbe.ch site also hosts a genus-level Segestria key (araneae.nmbe.ch/specieskey/298/Segestria) for finer work.

couplets.character: Arrangement of the median eyes and the posterior lateral eyes (rear eye row)

options.text: Median eyes and posterior lateral eyes set on a strongly recurved (backward-bowed) line
Median eyes and posterior lateral eyes set on a nearly straight line

records.found_check: Confirmed against the user's photographed species, Segestria florentina (araneae.nmbe.ch/data/420 and Wikipedia). It fits every family character: six eyes in a small forward cluster; first three leg pairs held forward; elongate tube-shaped dark body (uniform blackish with a purplish abdominal sheen); lives head-out in a silk tube in wall/rock/bark crevices with radiating silk trip-lines; sit-and-wait nocturnal hunter on warm walls and in buildings. Female body length 14-22 mm (the largest European segestriid) and male 10-15 mm sit at the top of the family size range, and its metallic green chelicerae and blackened front legs (leg I) are the standout field marks. Eye arrangement (strongly recurved rear row) places it in genus Segestria per the araneae.nmbe.ch genus key, consistent with the species name.

characters.web: Builds a small, flat, star-shaped or tent-like disc web (a silk sheet with radiating threads, often only a few cm across) pressed flat against a wall, stone, or bark, with a tubular retreat in a crevice. The spider sits under or beside it and rushes out to throw silk over passing prey (often ants). Not a tall airy snare like an orb or a sheet web in vegetation.
characters.eyes: Eight eyes bunched tightly together in a single compact oval cluster near the front of the head (not spread in two clear rows like most spiders). The two central rear eyes (posterior median eyes) are distinctively kidney/triangular-shaped and look flat or silvery. With the naked eye you mostly just see a small dark eye-patch.
characters.body_shape: Carapace (head/front body) almost circular or shield-like and somewhat flattened, roughly as wide as long. Abdomen rounded to oval. The whole spider looks squat and disc-like. A characteristic feature is a tube/anal tubercle between the rear spinnerets bearing a tuft of fine hairs (a star-like brush), visible only under magnification.
characters.legs: Legs short and spread evenly all around the body (radiating outward like spokes) rather than pointing clearly forward and back, giving a fast, scuttling, wheel-like dash. In Oecobius the rear spinnerets are noticeably long and project behind the body. Legs pale, sometimes faintly to distinctly ringed/annulated.
characters.colour: Oecobius (the common Portuguese wall spiders, incl. O. navus): pale brownish to grey, the rounded carapace pale with a dark margin and dark spots/marks in the eye region, abdomen yellowish-brown mottled with dark spots over pale flecks. Uroctea: larger, dark with a striking pattern of pale/yellow spots on a dark abdomen.
characters.behaviour_habitat: Free-living ambush hunters that specialise on ants. Oecobius is strongly synanthropic in Portugal: found on and inside buildings, on exterior walls, stones, fences and gravel, often in dry sunny Mediterranean spots (garrigue, oak woods, heathland) and indoors. Uroctea lives outdoors under stones with a flattened tent retreat. Active, fast runners when disturbed.

source.title: Spiders of Europe — genus key for Oecobiidae
source.authors: Nentwig W, Blick T, Bosmans R, Gloor D, Hänggi A, Kropf C (araneae.nmbe.ch)

couplets.character: Body size and posterior median eye shape (plus overall habitus / body pattern)

options.text: Body length < 3 mm; posterior median eyes kidney-shaped (triangular); habitus as figured. Small, pale wall-dwelling spiders.
Body length > 5 mm; eyes as figured; larger spider with a typical dark abdomen patterned with pale spots.

deeper_key.notes: The araneae.nmbe.ch genus key for Oecobiidae is tiny — a single couplet separating the two European genera Oecobius and Uroctea, on body size (<3 mm vs >5 mm) and PME shape. It does NOT go to species. In Portugal the family is overwhelmingly Oecobius (the ~8 PT species are mostly Oecobius, e.g. O. navus, with Uroctea limited/absent depending on source). For species-level ID within Oecobius there is a separate species key (https://araneae.nmbe.ch/specieskey/240/Oecobius), but reliable separation of Oecobius species generally requires a microscope and examination of genitalia (male palp / female epigyne) plus details of the carapace/eye-region markings; field photos alone usually only get you to genus Oecobius, and often O. navus by default given its dominance in synanthropic Portuguese settings.

field_questions.ask: Is it a small (2-3 mm) squat, almost circular-bodied spider sitting flat on a wall, stone, or building, under a tiny flat star-shaped silk patch?
field_questions.yes_means: Consistent with Oecobius (the common Portuguese wall spider, e.g. O. navus).

field_questions.ask: Are the legs short and spread evenly all around the body (like spokes of a wheel), and does it dash in a fast circular scuttle when disturbed?
field_questions.yes_means: Typical Oecobiidae locomotion — supports this family over most others.

field_questions.ask: Are all the eyes bunched together in one tight little cluster at the front (rather than in two clear spread-out rows)?
field_questions.yes_means: Matches the compact eye group of Oecobiidae.

field_questions.ask: Does it have two noticeably long spinnerets sticking out behind the rounded abdomen?
field_questions.yes_means: Supports Oecobius within Oecobiidae (long posterior spinnerets are a family hallmark).

records.found_check: Oecobius navus fits these family characters well. It is small (~2.2-2.5 mm body length), has the rounded, slightly flattened pale carapace with a dark margin and dark marks in the eye region, a mottled yellowish-brown abdomen, the compact eye cluster with triangular/kidney-shaped posterior median eyes, long posterior spinnerets, short evenly-radiating legs, and the synanthropic wall/building habitat with a small flat star-shaped disc web — all diagnostic of genus Oecobius in family Oecobiidae. It keys to Oecobius (body < 3 mm) at couplet 1 of the genus key, confirming the family characters above are correct for the user's photographed specimen.

lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: These also have a tight eye cluster, but lack the long projecting posterior spinnerets and the anal tubercle with a star-like hair tuft; they have 6 eyes (not 8) and do not build a flat star-shaped disc web on walls.

Also cribellate wall-dwellers with a clustered eye group, but Filistatids are more elongate/cylindrical with the legs not evenly radiating, and they spin a tangle of cribellate threads radiating from a tube rather than the flat disc/tent web; no star-brush anal tubercle.

Some small theridiids share rounded abdomens, but they have 8 eyes in two clear rows, longer legs not arranged spoke-like, and build 3-D tangle webs in corners rather than flat star webs pressed to a surface.

Wandering hunters can look superficially similar, but they have spread-out two-row eyes, longer forward/backward-oriented legs, no disc web, and no long paired projecting spinnerets with a hair-tuft tubercle.

characters.web: Snare web of bluish-grey, woolly cribellate silk laid down as radiating threads spreading out from a silk-lined tube retreat tucked deep in a crack — in a wall, between stones of a dry-stone wall, in tree bark, rock crevices or cave/building entrances. The spider sits in the tube and rushes out along the radiating lines onto prey.
characters.eyes: 8 eyes packed into one tight, compact cluster (a 'closed group') raised slightly on a small bump at the front of the head — all the eyes sit clearly less than one eye-diameter apart, so they look like a single dark spot rather than separated rows. Not the wide-spread two-row pattern of typical web spiders.
characters.body_shape: Body in two parts; the head-region (carapace) is rounded and fairly flat, the abdomen oval and softly hairy. Larger species (Filistata) are robust and broad; the small Pritha species are tiny and slighter. Males have spectacularly long, slender palps held out in front like horns (palp tibia at least 4x the length of the bulb in Filistata).
characters.legs: Eight legs, fairly long and the same colour as or slightly banded against the body; in the big Filistata they are brown with darker blotches on the thigh segment (femur). Front legs are angled to grip and hold prey at the retreat. Females carry a calamistrum — a comb of special hairs on the hind legs used to fluff out the cribellate silk (needs a lens to see).
characters.colour: Larger Filistata are uniformly light brown to dark brown, the carapace often with a thin dark edge and a triangular darker patch on the head region; abdomen plain light-to-mid brown with fine pale hairs. Pritha species are smaller and paler/yellowish-brown. Overall a plain, drab, hairy brown spider with no bold pattern.
characters.behaviour_habitat: Sedentary ambusher that lives for years in the same retreat. Strongly associated with vertical surfaces in warm dry places: old walls, dry-stone walls, rock faces, cork-oak bark, scrub, and the mouths of caves and buildings. A classic Mediterranean spider, common across Portugal; the radiating woolly web around a hole in a sunny wall is the giveaway.

field_questions.ask: Do all 8 eyes sit jammed together in one tight little cluster (like a single dark dot), rather than spread in two rows across the head?
field_questions.yes_means: Consistent with Filistatidae — the closed compact eye group is a key family trait.

field_questions.ask: Is the web a spray of woolly, bluish-grey radiating threads coming out of a hole or crack in a wall, rock or bark, with a silk tube the spider hides in?
field_questions.yes_means: Classic Filistatidae crevice web — radiating cribellate snare lines from a tube retreat.

field_questions.ask: Is the spider a fairly plain brown (or pale brown), hairy, two-part spider with no bold stripes or markings on the abdomen?
field_questions.yes_means: Fits the drab, unpatterned filistatid body, especially the common Filistata insidiatrix.

field_questions.ask: Is this a male with two very long, thin palps stuck out in front of the face like a pair of horns?
field_questions.yes_means: Strongly suggests a male Filistata — the long slender palps (tibia much longer than the bulb) are distinctive.

lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Oecobiids also make small radiating cribellate webs on walls, but they are tiny, flatter, with a broad squarish carapace and a large fan-shaped two-segmented anal tubercle bearing long hairs at the rear; their eyes are spread, not crammed into one tight group like Filistatidae.

Dysderids (e.g. woodlouse hunters) are reddish with a glossy long abdomen and only 6 eyes; Filistatidae have 8 eyes in a tight cluster, are browner and hairier, and build a radiating cribellate web rather than just a silk sac under stones.

Segestriids also live in a silk tube in a crevice with radiating trip-lines, but their threads are dry/plain (not woolly cribellate), they have 6 eyes, and their first three leg pairs point forward; Filistatidae have 8 clustered eyes and woolly bluish silk.
Goblin spiders are minute (often under 2 mm) with 6 eyes and usually a hard armoured abdomen, free-living with no snare web; Filistatidae have 8 eyes in a closed group and build a crevice snare web.

source.title: araneae - Spiders of Europe: Genus key for Filistatidae
source.authors: Nentwig W, Blick T, Bosmans R, Gloor D, Hänggi A, Kropf C, Stäubli A

couplets.character: Male palpal tibia shape and overall body length

options.text: Male palpal tibia strongly thickened; body length < 5 mm
Male palpal tibia cylindrical and long; body length > 8 mm

deeper_key.notes: The araneae.nmbe.ch online key for Filistatidae reaches GENUS level only, and is essentially a single couplet separating the small Pritha (palp tibia thickened, body <5 mm) from the large Filistata (palp tibia long/cylindrical, body >8 mm). Two further genera (Sahastata, Zaitunia) are listed on the site but are not part of the European/Portuguese fauna and are not keyed dichotomously. There is NO species-level dichotomous key on araneae.nmbe.ch for these genera: the Filistata species page only shows comparative figures of the male pedipalp and female internal genitalia (endogyne/receptacula), with the note that 'females [are] without externally visible epigynal structures.' So beyond genus, reliable species ID generally requires a microscope and examination of genitalia (male palp structure; cleared female internal endogyne), ideally with the 2018 revision of Filistata (Zonstein, Marusik & Grabolle, Arachnology 18(2)). In Portugal the common, easily-found species is Filistata insidiatrix; the tiny F. pygmaea was described from Portugal in 2018, and small Pritha species also occur — these small ones are hard to separate without a specialist and a scope. For field/photo work, getting to family or to the Filistata-vs-Pritha genus split (mainly by size) is the realistic ceiling.

records.found_check: Confirmed: the family characters fit the user's photographed Filistata insidiatrix. Per the araneae.nmbe.ch species data page it is a large brown to dark brown spider, females 9-14 mm and males 7-9 mm (matching the 'large Filistata, body >8 mm' branch of the genus key), with a light brown prosoma edged in a thin dark line and a triangular brown patch on the head, a plain light-brown hairy abdomen, and brown legs blotched on the femur. It builds an irregular cribellate web with a retreat tube, lives in cork-oak forest, scrub, dry-stone walls and cave entrances across the Mediterranean — exactly the 'radiating woolly web from a crevice in a sunny wall' described in the field characters. The 8-eyes-in-a-tight-cluster and female calamistrum (cribellate) traits also hold for this species. The male's very long slender palps (tibia at least 4x the bulb) are the standout feature noted in the field questions."

characters.web: Free-living hunter, no snare web. Sparassidae do not build a web to catch prey; they ambush and chase insects on foliage, bark or the ground. A silk retreat or egg sac may be present but there is no orb, sheet or tangle snare.
characters.eyes: Eight eyes arranged in two roughly horizontal rows of four (4 + 4). In the Portuguese green species Micrommata the eyes are fairly small, similar in size, and ringed with pale/white hairs; the back (posterior) row is gently procurved. Eyes do not form the high, wide-set turret shape of crab spiders.
characters.body_shape: Flattened, broad body held low. The carapace (head region) is about as wide as long and somewhat rounded; the abdomen is oval. The overall look is flat and crab-like, built for squeezing under bark and along surfaces rather than the dome-bodied profile of most web spiders.
characters.legs: Long and held laterigrade - twisted sideways so the legs splay out crab-fashion and the spider can run sideways and forwards. Crucially, leg pairs I and II are NOT obviously longer or thicker than pairs III and IV (all four pairs fairly even). Dense hair pads (scopulae) run along the underside of both the tarsi and metatarsi (the last two leg segments), helping it grip smooth surfaces. Each foot has two claws plus claw tufts.
characters.colour: Variable across the family from straw-brown and grey-mottled (Olios, Eusparassus - cryptic on bark/rock/sand) to vivid green. The one species the user photographed, Micrommata virescens, is the standout: females are bright leaf-green all over with a slightly darker green stripe down the abdomen; males are green with red sides and a red to red-brown central abdominal stripe bordered with yellow. Green individuals are unmistakable in Portugal.
characters.behaviour_habitat: Active free-roaming hunters, mostly nocturnal or crepuscular except the day-active green Micrommata. Found in low vegetation, grass, woodland edges, dry meadows and damp clearings (Micrommata virescens), or under bark, on rocks, walls and in dry/sandy Mediterranean scrub (Olios, Eusparassus). Several southern species are fast, flat-bodied and hide in crevices by day.

field_questions.ask: Are all the legs splayed out sideways (crab-like), and can the spider move sideways/forwards rather than holding legs straight down?
field_questions.yes_means: Laterigrade leg posture - typical of Sparassidae (also crab spiders and running crabs, so check the next questions).

field_questions.ask: Are the front two pairs of legs roughly the SAME length and thickness as the back two pairs (not dramatically longer/stouter)?
field_questions.yes_means: Points to Sparassidae and away from Thomisidae crab spiders, whose front legs are conspicuously longer and beefier.

field_questions.ask: Is there NO catching web - is the spider just sitting on or running over leaves, grass, bark or a wall?
field_questions.yes_means: Consistent with Sparassidae, which are free-living hunters that never spin a snare web.

field_questions.ask: Is the spider bright leaf-green (or green with a red/yellow abdominal stripe)?
field_questions.yes_means: Almost certainly Micrommata (the green huntsman), the most common Sparassidae a person will see in Portugal.

lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Crab spiders also sit with legs splayed sideways, but their FIRST TWO leg pairs are clearly much longer and stouter than the back two; in Sparassidae all four pairs are fairly even. Crab spiders also tend to have a more triangular/wedge abdomen and often sit motionless on flowers.

Also laterigrade and flat, but in Philodromidae leg pair II is usually the longest and the dense foot hair (scopula) is only at the very tips of the tarsi. In Sparassidae the scopula runs along both the tarsi AND metatarsi (further up the foot), and legs are more even.

lookalikes.family: Sparassidae vs. large wandering wolf spiders (Lycosidae)
lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Wolf spiders hold their legs in the normal downward-jointed way (not twisted sideways/laterigrade) and have a very different eye pattern with two large eyes on top; Sparassidae have eight smallish, more even eyes in two rows of four and a flat, sideways stance.

source.title: Araneae - Spiders of Europe: Determination key, genus level, Sparassidae
source.authors: Nentwig W, Blick T, Bosmans R, Gloor D, Hanggi A, Kropf C (key for Sparassidae contributed by Peter Jager)

couplets.character: Cheliceral teeth (the small teeth on the fang base)

options.text: Chelicerae with 3 anterior and 4 posterior teeth plus intermarginal denticles
Chelicerae with 2 anterior teeth; female palpal claw with short teeth

couplets.character: Posterior eye row shape and posterior cheliceral teeth

options.text: Posterior eye row distinctly recurved; posterior cheliceral teeth fused
Posterior eye row straight or procurved; posterior cheliceral teeth distinctly separated

couplets.character: Number of retromarginal bristles on the distal/basal cheliceral segment

options.text: 1 to 2 retromarginal bristles
4 to 11 retromarginal bristles

couplets.character: Body size and colour

options.text: Body length below 16 mm; colouration greenish
Larger, brownish; Mediterranean/tropical

deeper_key.notes: The araneae.nmbe.ch online key (by P. Jager) keys Sparassidae to GENUS, not to species. For Portugal the relevant genera it resolves are Micrommata, Olios and Eusparassus (Heteropoda and Cebrennus key out too but are not the Portuguese fauna). Note the key's couplets lean on microscope characters - counting cheliceral teeth and retromarginal bristles - so reaching genus reliably usually needs a hand lens or scope, though the green Micrommata is identifiable on colour/size alone in the field. Getting to SPECIES is harder: the six Portuguese species (e.g. Micrommata virescens vs M. formosa, and the Eusparassus dufouri group) are separated by genitalia (epigyne in females, palp in males) examined under a microscope - colour and size alone are NOT diagnostic to species. For species-level work consult Jager's Micrommata revision (Zootaxa 5352, 2023) and the Olios revision (Jager 2020); a single green field photo from Portugal can usually be called Micrommata sp., and most often M. virescens, but confirming the species needs a specimen and a scope.

records.found_check: Confirmed: the family characters fit Micrommata virescens, the species the user photographed. It is a free-living hunter with no snare web; it has eight eyes in two rows ringed with white hairs; legs are held laterigrade (crab-like) with the front pairs not noticeably longer/stouter than the rear; females are 12-16 mm and bright green, males 7-10 mm and green with a red/yellow-bordered abdominal stripe (within the under-16 mm greenish couplet that keys to Micrommata). It lives in grass, low branches and woodland edges - all consistent. No mismatch found.

characters.web: Cribellate snare web. Most build a horizontal or gently sloping orb web of woolly, bluish, NON-sticky 'hackled' silk that catches prey by tangling rather than glue; a silk stabilimentum (zig-zag or straight line across the hub) is often present. The genus Hyptiotes is the exception: it builds a tiny triangular sector web (a wedge of just a few radii) and holds it taut like a slingshot, collapsing it onto prey.
characters.eyes: Eight small eyes in two rows. They are hard to see in the field because the spider is small, hairy and dull. In some genera (e.g. Hyptiotes) the posterior lateral eyes sit on little raised tubercles. Eye detail is not a useful naked-eye character here - the web and posture tell you far more.
characters.body_shape: Small, drab, shaggy spider. The abdomen is often humped or knobbly on top, with one or two pairs of bumps/tubercles rather than a smooth oval; Hyptiotes has an especially plump, angular, humped abdomen. The whole animal is easily mistaken for a scrap of debris in the web.
characters.legs: Three tarsal claws. The hindmost (4th) leg carries a calamistrum - a single comb-like row of bristles on the metatarsus, used to comb out the woolly cribellate silk (visible only with a hand lens/macro). The front legs (leg I) are usually longest and held stretched straight forward along the web. In the very common Uloborus plumipes the female's first legs bear conspicuous long blackish feathery (plumose) hair tufts on the tibia - the 'feather-legged' look. Legs often faintly banded.
characters.colour: Dull and cryptic: browns, greys, buff, sometimes with pale/white flecks or a darker midline, densely hairy. U. plumipes is light brown with a darker median line, a pair of small white spots, and white-bluish leg bands. The colours are camouflage against bark, dead leaves and accumulated web debris.
characters.behaviour_habitat: Sit-and-wait web spiders, often hanging at or near the hub with legs outstretched. UNIQUE point: uloborids have NO venom glands - the only venomless spiders you will meet here. They kill prey purely by wrapping it in vast amounts of silk, then liquefy it with digestive juices. Found on low vegetation, scrub, walls, rock faces and tree trunks; U. plumipes is strongly associated with greenhouses, garden centres and warm indoor spaces and is the species most people in Portugal encounter.

field_questions.ask: Is the web a horizontal (flat / lie-flat) orb rather than a vertical wheel hanging like a wall?
field_questions.yes_means: Consistent with Uloboridae - true orb-weavers (Araneidae) almost always hang a vertical orb; uloborid orbs typically lie flat or sloping.

field_questions.ask: Does the silk look woolly, fuzzy, bluish-white and lint-like rather than beaded with tiny glue droplets?
field_questions.yes_means: Cribellate 'hackled' silk - strong evidence for Uloboridae; sticky-droplet silk points to Araneidae instead.

field_questions.ask: Is there a small zig-zag or straight silk band (stabilimentum) across the centre, OR is it a tiny triangular wedge web held taut?
field_questions.yes_means: Hub stabilimentum fits Uloborus/Octonoba; a triangular slingshot web indicates the uloborid genus Hyptiotes.

field_questions.ask: Is it a small (2-6 mm) drab, hairy spider with a lumpy/humped back, indoors in a greenhouse or garden centre, sometimes with feathery front-leg tufts?
field_questions.yes_means: Very likely Uloborus plumipes, the common indoor uloborid in Portugal.

lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Araneidae spin a vertical orb with sticky glue droplets on the spiral; Uloboridae spin a usually horizontal orb of woolly non-sticky cribellate silk and have a calamistrum (silk-combing brush) on leg IV. Under a lens, Uloboridae also lack venom glands/fangs hooked for biting - they wrap prey instead.

Tetragnathids build sticky, often horizontal orbs too, but are typically elongate with very long jaws and long slim legs, and their silk is sticky. Uloborids are stockier, hairier, often hump-backed and use woolly hackled silk.

lookalikes.family: Other cribellate web-builders e.g. Dictynidae / Filistatidae
lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: These also produce woolly cribellate silk but make irregular mesh/lace sheets or tube retreats, not a recognisable orb or triangular sector web. The orb/triangle geometry plus humped abdomen separates Uloboridae.

Theridiids build 3-D tangle cobwebs (often with a gumfoot) and have a comb of serrated bristles on tarsus IV plus a rounded glossy abdomen; Uloboridae build a 2-D orb/triangle of fuzzy silk and have a single-row calamistrum on metatarsus IV.

source.title: Araneae - Spiders of Europe: Identification key, family Uloboridae (genus key)
source.authors: Nentwig W, Blick T, Bosmans R, Gloor D, Hanggi A, Kropf C, Stauubli A

couplets.character: Posterior lateral eyes (PLE) and overall web type

options.text: PLE on tubercles; prosoma as figured; opisthosoma plump, dorsally with humps
PLE not on prominent tubercles; builds orb webs

couplets.character: Width of the anterior eye row relative to maximal prosoma width

options.text: Width of anterior eye row less than half of maximal prosoma width
Width of anterior eye row half of maximal prosoma width or more

couplets.character: Spacing of lateral vs median posterior eyes; cymbial macrosetae

options.text: Distance ALE-PLE about equal to distance PLE-PME
Distance ALE-PLE about double the distance PLE-PME; cymbium with two stout distal macrosetae

deeper_key.notes: The araneae.nmbe.ch online key resolves Uloboridae to GENUS only (4 European genera: Hyptiotes, Uloborus, Octonoba, Polenecia), via the 3 couplets transcribed above - this is the full key, not truncated. Several couplet characters (eye-row width ratios, eyes on tubercles, cymbial macrosetae) really need a hand lens or macro photo to score reliably. Getting to SPECIES is harder. For Uloborus, a separate species key (araneae.nmbe.ch/specieskey/352/Uloborus) separates U. walckenaerius (prosoma with distinct dark longitudinal bands) from U. plumipes (no longitudinal stripes; female tibia I with strikingly long black plumose/feathery hairs) - these two are doable from good photos. Beyond that, confident species-level identification of uloborids (and of females vs juveniles, and of the Hyptiotes species) generally requires examining genitalia (epigyne / male palp) under a microscope. Honest field ceiling for a non-expert: genus, and often species for the distinctive greenhouse U. plumipes; otherwise treat species as 'microscope needed'.

records.found_check: Test case Uloborus plumipes fits all family characters: it is cribellate (has cribellum + calamistrum on leg IV), small (female ~3-6 mm, male ~3-4 mm), drab light-brown with a darker median line and a humped/lumpy abdomen (2 dorsal humps with 2 white spots), builds a horizontal hackled orb often with a stabilimentum, and the female's tibia I bears the diagnostic long black plumose 'feather-leg' hairs. It is venomless and famously common in greenhouses/garden centres - matching the family's behaviour_habitat. In the genus key it goes: couplet 1 (PLE not on tubercles, builds orb web) to couplet 2 (anterior eye row less than half prosoma width) = Uloborus, then to U. plumipes in the species key by the lack of dark longitudinal prosomal bands plus the plumose leg-I hairs. The other 5 Portuguese species sit in the same European genera covered by the key (Hyptiotes, Octonoba, Polenecia, Uloborus) and are consistent with the family characters above."

characters.web: Builds a lace web: an untidy patch of bluish, woolly-looking hackled (cribellate) silk radiating out from a crack or hole, with a silk-lined tube retreat going back into the crevice. The fluffy combed silk often shows a faint blue tinge when fresh. It is a snare around a retreat, not a neat orb or sheet.
characters.eyes: Eight eyes in two rows, all small and roughly equal, clustered in a compact group at the front of the head. With the naked eye you mostly see a small dark eye-patch; you do not need to count them to place the family. (In the genus key the spacing of the front-middle eyes separates genera, but that needs magnification.)
characters.body_shape: Stout, fairly flat-bodied spider. Oval, somewhat plump abdomen and a solid rounded head/thorax (cephalothorax). Not long-legged or spindly; looks compact and a bit chunky overall.
characters.legs: Eight robust legs of moderate length, fairly thick and often faintly banded (vague darker annulations). On the underside of the fourth (rearmost) leg there is a comb of bristles called the calamistrum used to fluff the silk, but this needs a hand lens or microscope to see.
characters.colour: Generally dark and drab. Amaurobius ferox is very dark: reddish-brown to almost black head/thorax with a near-black eye region, reddish-brown legs, and a dark grey-to-black abdomen marked with pale yellowish spots that often form a faint mask- or skull-like / skeletal pattern toward the front. Other Portuguese Amaurobius can be a bit paler grey-brown with a dark anterior abdominal mark.
characters.behaviour_habitat: Lives hidden in dark, damp crevices: cracks in walls, under stones and logs, in cellars, caves, basements and old masonry, often around buildings. The spider hides in its tube and rushes out onto the lace web when prey vibrates it, mostly at night. A. ferox in particular is strongly tied to caves, cellars and humid wall cracks (troglophile).

records.common_group: lace-weavers / lace web spiders (tangled-web cribellate spiders)

field_questions.ask: Is there a patch of messy, woolly, faintly bluish silk spreading out from a crack, hole, or crevice (rather than a neat round orb web or a flat sheet with a funnel)?
field_questions.yes_means: Consistent with a lace web spider (Amaurobiidae) - cribellate hackled silk built around a retreat in a crevice.

field_questions.ask: Is the spider a stout, dark, fairly flat-bodied spider living in a damp wall crack, cellar, cave, or under a stone/log near buildings?
field_questions.yes_means: Fits the typical Amaurobius lifestyle, especially A. ferox in cellars and humid masonry.

field_questions.ask: Does the dark abdomen have pale yellowish spots that form a faint mask- or skull-/skeleton-like pattern at the front?
field_questions.yes_means: Strongly suggests Amaurobius ferox specifically among the Portuguese species.

field_questions.ask: Is the head region compact with a small tight cluster of eight small eyes, and the legs fairly thick and only vaguely banded (not long, thin, and strongly striped)?
field_questions.yes_means: Body form matches Amaurobiidae rather than long-legged house or cellar spiders.

lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Agelenids spin a flat sheet web with an obvious funnel retreat and have very long, conspicuous spinnerets sticking out the back; their silk is plain (not cribellate/woolly). Amaurobius makes a tangled woolly bluish lace web around a crack and has short spinnerets.

Also cribellate (woolly silk), but dictynids are tiny (mostly 2-4 mm) and build small mesh webs on plants and walls; Amaurobiidae are bigger, stouter, and crevice-dwelling. Reliable separation is by genitalia under a scope.

These relatives are not cribellate - no woolly hackled silk and no calamistrum comb on leg 4. If the silk is plain and there is no calamistrum, it is not Amaurobiidae. This usually needs a hand lens or microscope.

lookalikes.family: Segestriidae (tube/six-eyed spiders) and other crevice dwellers
lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Segestriids have only six eyes and build a tube with radiating trip-lines but no woolly cribellate sheet; Amaurobius has eight eyes and combed bluish lace silk.

source.title: araneae - Spiders of Europe: Genus-level key to Amaurobiidae
source.authors: Nentwig, W., Blick, T., Bosmans, R., Gloor, D., Hänggi, A., Kropf, C. (Spiders of Europe / araneae.nmbe.ch)

deeper_key.notes: araneae.nmbe.ch provides a tractable online GENUS-level key for Amaurobiidae (5 European genera: Ovtchinnikovia, Arctobius, Callobius, Amaurobius, Cybaeopsis), transcribed in full below. The realistic ceiling for a non-expert is family, and at best genus, in the field. In Portugal the family is represented essentially by the genus Amaurobius (e.g. A. ferox, A. similis, A. fenestralis, A. occidentalis and relatives). Getting to SPECIES is NOT a field task: the species-level key at araneae.nmbe.ch/specieskey/25/Amaurobius keys ~31 Amaurobius species and relies almost entirely on adult genitalia - the shape of the male palp tibial apophyses and the female epigyne/vulva - examined under a microscope from a mature, ideally collected specimen. Note even couplet 1 of the genus key uses the cribellum, which needs magnification. So: photo/naked eye -> family (lace web + stout dark crevice spider); hand lens -> genus Amaurobius; species -> microscope + genitalia, often best confirmed by an arachnologist. A. ferox is one of the more recognisable on habitus (very dark, pale skull-like abdominal spots, cellar/cave habitat) but should still be genitalia-checked for certainty.

couplets.character: Cribellum (silk plate in front of spinnerets) and cheliceral groove

options.text: Cribellum lacking; serrated keel on the inner margin of the cheliceral groove
Cribellum present

couplets.character: Position of anterior median eyes and opisthosomal median band

options.text: Anterior median eyes equidistant from each other and from the anterior lateral eyes; opisthosoma with a dark median band
Anterior median eyes closer to each other than to the lateral eyes; median band on opisthosoma not developed or visible only in the anterior half

couplets.character: Male tibial apophysis of palp and female epigyne (genitalia)

options.text: Dorsal tibial apophysis not subdivided (shallow rounded bulge); epigyne divided by a longitudinal slit
Retrolateral tibial apophysis large; epigyne with a transverse lobe or fovea

records.found_check: Amaurobius ferox fits the family characters well and was used as the test case. It is cribellate (builds the woolly, faintly bluish lace web around a crevice retreat), is a stout, flat-bodied dark spider, and lives in exactly the damp crevice / cellar / cave / wall-crack habitat described - araneae.nmbe.ch gives its habitat as 'almost only in caves, basements and humid mural chinks' (troglophile). Size matches the family range: male 8-10 mm, female 11-16 mm. Colour matches: reddish-brown to near-black prosoma, dark grey-black opisthosoma with pale yellowish spots forming the characteristic mask/skull-like anterior pattern. In the genus key it keys out to Amaurobius (couplet 3: large retrolateral tibial apophysis / epigyne with transverse lobe), and in the species key to A. ferox via a shallow roundish dorsal tibial apophysis bulge and a semicircular median epigyne - i.e. species confirmation needs the genitalia. All family-level characters above are consistent with this photographed species.

characters.web: Free-living hunter, no snare web. Active, sharp-eyed ambush hunters on low vegetation; they stalk and pounce on prey rather than trap it. Silk is used only for the egg sac (often guarded by the female on a leaf), not for catching food.
characters.eyes: Eight eyes, but the most reliable family clue is that six of them sit in a near-equilateral hexagon on a raised hump at the front of the head; the remaining two small eyes sit low at the front. Combined with a tall, vertical 'flat face', this hexagon is the give-away for lynx spiders.
characters.body_shape: Small to medium spider with a fairly high, rounded head region (carapace) and an abdomen that tapers to a point at the rear, giving a teardrop or torpedo outline. The face is steep and vertical.
characters.legs: Long, slender legs studded with conspicuous long, erect spines that stick out at angles, forming a sort of 'basket' the spider uses to trap struggling insects. The bold spininess of the legs is very noticeable even in a phone photo.
characters.colour: Usually pale: yellowish, straw, light brown or (in Peucetia) bright green, often with darker longitudinal stripes or rows of white/dark marks down the head and abdomen, and dark spots or streaks on the legs.
characters.behaviour_habitat: Daytime sun-lovers found on bushes, grasses, flowers and other low plants in warm, open, sunny places (scrub, meadows, garden vegetation). They run fast, leap, and are alert to movement, often sitting head-down on a stem watching for prey.

field_questions.ask: Are six of the eyes set in a roughly hexagonal ring on a raised bump at the front of the head, with the face steep and vertical?
field_questions.yes_means: Strong sign of Oxyopidae (lynx spider) - this hexagonal eye block is their signature.

field_questions.ask: Do the long legs carry obvious long spines sticking out sideways, almost like a bristly cage?
field_questions.yes_means: Consistent with lynx spiders, which use these spines to corral prey.

field_questions.ask: Does the abdomen taper to a point at the back (teardrop shape) rather than being round or stubby?
field_questions.yes_means: Fits Oxyopidae body outline.

field_questions.ask: Is the spider sitting in the open on a sunny plant/flower/grass stem with no web around it?
field_questions.yes_means: Matches lynx spider behaviour - they are web-less daytime hunters on vegetation.

lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Jumping spiders have two huge forward-facing front eyes dominating a square 'face'; lynx spiders instead have six eyes in a hexagon with no oversized pair, and much longer, spinier legs.

Wolf spiders have a very different eye layout (a row of four small eyes with two large eyes above) and are mostly ground-runners; lynx spiders have the hexagonal eye block, live up on plants, and have far spinier legs.
Crab spiders sit on flowers too but hold their stout front legs out sideways crab-fashion, have small eyes (no hexagon) and lack the long erect leg spines of lynx spiders.
Nursery-web spiders are larger, longer-bodied and lack the raised hexagonal eye hump and the strongly spined, basket-forming legs of lynx spiders.

source.title: Oxyopidae - Identification key to genera (Spiders of Europe)
source.authors: Nentwig W, Blick T, Bosmans R, Gloor D, Hanggi A, Kropf C, Staubli A

couplets.character: Male pedipalp: presence/absence of a pronounced paracymbium process; female epigyne structure; overall body length

options.text: Male palp WITHOUT a pronounced paracymbium process; female epigyne with a scapus-like / anteriorly pointed median projection. Smaller: males 4-7 mm, females 5-10 mm body length.

Male palp WITH a pronounced paracymbium process about as wide as the palp; female epigyne clearly divided into separate left and right halves, no central scapus. Larger: males 6-9 mm, females 9-15 mm body length.

deeper_key.notes: araneae.nmbe.ch has a genus-level key for Oxyopidae, and it is tiny: a single couplet splitting the family into its two European genera, Oxyopes and Peucetia (transcribed in full above; not truncated). The split leans mainly on genitalia (male paracymbium process, female epigyne form) plus the size difference - Peucetia is notably larger and is typically bright green. In Portugal the realistic genus answer is almost always Oxyopes (Peucetia viridis is a rare/peripheral green species). Below genus there is NO comparably simple online couplet key: the five Portuguese species (Oxyopes lineatus, O. heterophthalmus, O. mediterraneus, O. nigripalpis and Peucetia viridis) are separated reliably only by examining adult genitalia (male palp and female epigyne) under a microscope, so confident species-level ID from a field photo is generally not possible - treat species names as tentative unless an adult is examined under magnification or vetted by an expert. Compare against the species accounts and figures on araneae.nmbe.ch / wsc.nmbe.ch for the final step.

records.found_check: The user's photographed species, Oxyopes lineatus, fits the family characters well: it shows the hexagonal six-eye block on a vertical face, long spiny legs in the classic basket arrangement, a tapering abdomen, and a pale yellowish-to-light-brown body with longitudinal markings; it is a web-less daytime hunter on bushes and grasses in sunny places, and it occurs in Portugal. Its body size (males approx 4-5 mm, females approx 6-8 mm) sits inside the Oxyopes range from the genus key (males 4-7 mm, females 5-10 mm), confirming both the family and genus characters apply to it correctly."

characters.web: No snare/catching web. Free-living hunters. Female builds a temporary silk 'nursery' (a loose bell- or tent-shaped tangle among grass/leaves) only to hold her egg sac and newly hatched young; this is a nest, not a prey-catching web. Dolomedes lives at the water's edge and uses the water surface itself, not silk, as its hunting 'web'.
characters.eyes: Eight eyes in two rows, fairly even in size and laid out in a wide gentle curve, not a big-eyed pawn-like pattern. The rear four eyes form a flat trapezoid. Diagnostic (vs wolf spiders): looking at the head from above, the line joining the rear side eye to the rear middle eye meets the centre of the head AT or just behind the front edge of the carapace, not well in front of it. No huge pair of forward 'headlight' eyes like a wolf spider, and the rear eyes are not lifted into a tall hexagon like a lynx spider.
characters.body_shape: Elongate, fairly flat-bodied hunter with a longish, gently tapering abdomen (narrowing toward the front in Pisaura). Carapace longer than wide, usually with a clear pale lengthwise stripe down the middle. Legs long and laterigrade-ish, held out sideways; overall look is leggy and athletic, between a wolf spider and a huntsman. Dolomedes is much bulkier and broader.
characters.legs: Long, slender, spiny legs roughly equal in length (4th pair longest in Pisaura); held splayed. Two claws (not three). In Pisaura the legs often show fine dark speckling/annulation. Dolomedes has stout legs fringed with short hairs that help it run on and grip the water surface.
characters.colour: Very variable: yellowish, light to red-brown, grey or near-black. Almost always a bright, distinct pale median lengthwise stripe on the carapace; abdomen with a wide jagged (folium) band edged darker. Dolomedes is typically dark brown to blackish with a bold pale (cream/yellow) stripe running down each side of the whole body. Pisaura mirabilis often shows a pale dart and a pair of pale tear-drop marks below the eyes.
characters.behaviour_habitat: Ground/vegetation-dwelling ambush hunters that do not build catching webs. Pisaura mirabilis is very common in open sunny habitats - humid meadows, grassland, scrub, hedgerows, garden edges - up to ~900 m, often sunbathing on a leaf with the long front legs pressed together pointing forward. Dolomedes (raft/fishing spiders) live at the margins of ponds, bogs, slow streams and marshes, sitting at the waterline and running across the surface to catch insects and even tiny fish/tadpoles. Hallmark behaviour: the female carries her large round egg sac in her jaws (chelicerae) under her body, then slings it in a silk nursery and guards the spiderlings. In Pisaura the male offers the female a silk-wrapped 'nuptial gift' (a wrapped insect) before mating.

field_questions.ask: Is the spider a free-running hunter with NO flat or orb catching web (you only ever see it sitting on vegetation or at a water's edge)?
field_questions.yes_means: Consistent with Pisauridae (and several other hunting families) - rules out web-builders like orb-weavers and cobweb spiders.

field_questions.ask: Does it have a clear pale stripe running lengthwise down the middle of the head/back, on a body that looks long-legged and slender rather than fat?
field_questions.yes_means: Strongly fits Pisaura mirabilis, the common Portuguese nursery-web spider.

field_questions.ask: Is a female carrying a big round pea-like egg sac held in her JAWS under the front of her body (not stuck to the rear spinnerets)?
field_questions.yes_means: Pisauridae - the jaw-carried egg sac is the single best behavioural giveaway versus wolf spiders, which tow the sac from the spinnerets.

field_questions.ask: Is it a large, bulky, dark spider with a bold pale cream/yellow stripe down each side, sitting at the edge of water or running on the surface?
field_questions.yes_means: A Dolomedes raft/fishing spider (traditionally placed in Pisauridae; now often Dolomedidae).

lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Wolf spiders have two big forward-facing 'headlight' eyes in a distinct 3-row pattern and the female drags her egg sac attached to the spinnerets at the rear. Pisauridae eyes are more even and the female carries the egg sac in her jaws. Technical clincher: the rear-eye connecting line meets the carapace midline AT/just behind the front edge in Pisauridae, but well IN FRONT of it in Lycosidae.

Lynx spiders have a tall, near-hexagonal arrangement of the six rear eyes and very long erect spines on the legs, and a sharply tapering abdomen; Pisauridae eyes sit in a low curve and leg spines are less dramatic.

Huntsmen are flat, crab-like, with legs twisted forward (laterigrade) for sideways scuttling and no strong pale median stripe; Pisauridae are not as flattened and usually show the pale carapace stripe and a long tapering abdomen.

lookalikes.family: Dolomedidae (raft spiders, the new home of Dolomedes)
lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Not really a 'lookalike' to tell apart so much as a recent split: Dolomedes was historically inside Pisauridae and many field guides and the Spiders of Europe checklist still treat it as such, so a water's-edge raft spider keys out under this record even though the World Spider Catalog now files it under Dolomedidae.

source.title: araneae - Spiders of Europe, identification key to the genera of family Pisauridae
source.authors: Nentwig W, Blick T, Bosmans R, Kropf C, Staeubli A

deeper_key.notes: The online araneae.nmbe.ch genus 'key' for Pisauridae is NOT a multi-step dichotomous key - in the European fauna the family is effectively monogeneric for routine purposes, so the page simply gives the diagnostic characters of the single keyed genus Pisaura and then lists two further (Mediterranean/non-Central-European) genera, Nilus and Rothus, without couplets. Crucially, Dolomedes is no longer in this Pisauridae key because Spiders of Europe / the World Spider Catalog moved it to its own family Dolomedidae (Yu et al. 2024; reclassification credited to Morris, Hazzi & Hormiga 2025). So for Portugal: a leggy land nursery-web spider with a pale median carapace stripe = Pisaura (almost certainly P. mirabilis, the only common Portuguese species); a bulky dark water's-edge spider with a pale lateral stripe = Dolomedes (D. fimbriatus, or the rarer wetland-restricted D. plantarius). Telling D. fimbriatus from D. plantarius reliably needs a microscope: they are separated on genital structure (epigyne of the female, palp of the male) plus subtle pattern/setal differences, so naked-eye/photo ID typically stops at 'a Dolomedes'. Likewise Pisaura is confidently called to species only because P. mirabilis is the sole widespread Iberian representative - a positive species-level genital check is still the gold standard. Bottom line: family and genus are achievable in the field; species-level certainty for the Dolomedes pair requires genitalia under magnification.

couplets.character: Lifestyle, build and body markings of an adult Pisauridae (s.l.) in Portugal - this captures the practical genus split since araneae gives no formal couplets

options.text: Slender, long-legged, terrestrial hunter of grassland/scrub/hedgerow; carapace with a bright distinct pale median longitudinal stripe; abdomen long, tapering toward the front, with a wide jagged dark-edged band; posterior margin of the cheliceral furrow with 2-3 teeth; female carries egg sac in chelicerae and builds a bell-shaped nursery web in vegetation

Large, bulky, dark spider living at the margins of still/slow fresh water, hunting on the water surface; body usually with a conspicuous pale (cream/yellow) longitudinal stripe along each side; legs stout
options.leads_to: Dolomedes (now family Dolomedidae; in Portugal: D. fimbriatus and the scarcer D. plantarius - separate to species only by genitalia under a microscope)

records.found_check: Confirmed for the user's photographed species, Pisaura mirabilis. The family characters fit it exactly: it is a free-living hunter with no catching web (only the female's bell-shaped nursery web among grass), carapace has the bright pale median longitudinal stripe, the abdomen is long and tapers forward, the legs are long and slender, the eye row sits in a low curve, and the female famously carries the round egg sac in her chelicerae - matching the araneae.nmbe.ch family/genus diagnosis. Size also fits (araneae lists male 10-13 mm, female 12-15 mm; humid meadows and open habitats up to 900 m), which sits within the family size range given. The genus-level lead places it cleanly in Pisaura, the only common Portuguese member of the genus."

characters.web: Free-living nocturnal hunter, no snare web at all. It does not spin a web to catch prey; instead it stalks slowly and immobilises prey by spitting a sticky, venomous gum over it from up to ~20 mm away. You will find the spider itself, never a catching web.
characters.eyes: Six eyes, not the usual eight. They sit in three well-separated pairs (one front pair plus a pair on each side), forming a wide, broken arc rather than the tight 4+4 block of most spiders. With a phone macro you can often count three little clusters of two.
characters.body_shape: Cephalothorax (front body) strongly domed and humped, sloping UP towards the rear so it looks taller at the back; this big dome houses the enlarged venom/glue glands. The front body is about as large as, or larger than, the rounded abdomen, which slopes down at the back, giving a distinctive teardrop/dome silhouette in side view.
characters.legs: Eight long, very slender, delicate legs, pale straw to yellow-brown, conspicuously ringed (annulated) with darker bands. Legs look fragile and thread-like relative to the body.
characters.colour: Overall pale: straw-coloured, yellowish or light reddish-brown ground colour, boldly marked with a net-like pattern of black to brown-black flecks, spots and lines on both the domed front body and the abdomen. The matching dotted pattern on carapace and abdomen is very characteristic.
characters.behaviour_habitat: Slow, skulking, mostly nocturnal. In Portugal and southern Europe found both indoors (houses, sheds, cellars, on walls at night) and outdoors under stones, in scrub, heathland, gardens and rocky coastal areas. Moves deliberately, not a fast runner. Females carry the egg sac under the body.

field_questions.ask: Can you count six eyes arranged as three separated pairs, instead of eight?
field_questions.yes_means: Strong sign of Scytodidae (also fits a few other six-eyed families, so check the dome next).

field_questions.ask: Is the front body (carapace) high and domed, looking taller towards the rear, roughly as big as the abdomen?
field_questions.yes_means: Classic spitting-spider profile; combined with six eyes this nails Scytodidae.

field_questions.ask: Is the spider pale straw/yellow with a matching pattern of black dots and flecks on BOTH the front body and the abdomen, with thin ringed legs?
field_questions.yes_means: Typical Scytodes colouring, fits Scytodes thoracica and its relatives.

field_questions.ask: Is there no catching web, with the spider walking slowly on a wall or under a stone at night?
field_questions.yes_means: Consistent with a free-living spitting spider rather than a web-builder.

lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Pholcids also have very long thin legs and live indoors, but they build a loose tangle web and hang upside-down in it; most have eight eyes (six-eyed pholcids lack the high domed carapace). Scytodidae build NO web and have the humped, dome-backed front body.

Recluses are the other classic six-eyed (three pairs) house spider, but they are uniform tan/brown with little or no spotted pattern and have a FLAT carapace; Scytodes has a tall domed carapace and a bold black-on-straw dotted pattern on body and legs.
Six-eyed and indoor too, but dark grey/blackish, unmarked, with a low body and the front three pairs of legs held forward into a silk tube/web. Scytodes is pale-patterned, domed, and webless.

Also six-eyed, but the eyes sit in a tight compact group, the carapace is flat to gently arched (not domed), and colour is plain (reddish front + pale abdomen, or tiny orange goblins). They lack the bold dotted straw pattern and the high humped dome of Scytodes.

source.title: Spiders of Europe - Family Scytodidae, key to genera (genkey 40)
source.authors: Nentwig W, Blick T, Bosmans R, Kropf C, Stäubli A

deeper_key.notes: The araneae.nmbe.ch genus key for Scytodidae is effectively a single trivial step: it states there is only ONE genus in Europe, Scytodes Latreille, 1804 (Dictis is listed only under 'Further genera' and is not part of the European fauna). So any European/Portuguese spitting spider keys directly to Scytodes with no real branching. There is NO online araneae.nmbe.ch species-level key for Scytodidae - the species pages give descriptions and genitalia figures only. Honest ceiling: from a field photo you can confidently reach FAMILY (Scytodidae) and GENUS (Scytodes), and the common, widespread one in Portugal that you photographed is almost certainly Scytodes thoracica. But Portugal has ~3 Scytodes species (incl. S. thoracica plus Mediterranean species such as S. fusca / S. velutina), and the genus has 13 European species that overlap in size and the straw-with-black-dots pattern. Reliable separation of these species requires examining the adult genitalia under a microscope - male palp (embolus shape) and female epigyne/vulva - exactly the figures the araneae datasheets provide. Juveniles and photos generally cannot be taken past genus. To go below family, compare the live spider's genitalia against the figures on the per-species datasheets at araneae.nmbe.ch (e.g. https://araneae.nmbe.ch/data/1224/Scytodes_thoracica) or in the Iberian literature (Branco, Morano & Cardoso 2019, Iberian spider checklist update, Zootaxa 4614).

couplets.character: Genus-level placement within Scytodidae in Europe

options.text: Only one genus present in Europe (and in Portugal): the six-eyed spitting spiders with a high domed carapace and pale, black-spotted body and ringed legs
Further (non-European) genus listed for reference only, not part of the European/Portuguese fauna

records.found_check: The family characters fit the user's photographed Scytodes thoracica exactly. The araneae.nmbe.ch datasheet for S. thoracica gives: six eyes in three pairs; prosoma high/domed and 'dorsal line ascending posteriorly' (the carapace rising toward the rear); prosoma equal to or larger than the abdomen; pale yellowish ground colour with brown-black/black pattern on both prosoma and opisthosoma; legs yellow-brown to black and annulated; body length female 4.0-5.5 mm, male 3.5-4.0 mm; webless, slow nocturnal hunter that spits glue at prey up to 20 mm away; found indoors and outdoors under stones and in scrub. It is confirmed from Portugal (Branco et al., 2019). All six recorded family characters above (six eyes, domed carapace, pale black-flecked body, slender ringed legs, no web, slow nocturnal indoor/outdoor habit) match this species.

characters.web: Free-living nocturnal hunter, no snare web. Builds only a silk retreat and an egg-sac that the mother guards; does not spin a catching web. Being cribellate, it can lay down fine woolly (hackled) silk, but you will not see a typical orb or sheet web.
characters.eyes: Eight eyes set in two rows across the front third of the head. Crucially, the eyes are all fairly small and similar in size and spread out fairly evenly, NOT the big-headlight pattern of a wolf spider. There is no pair of greatly enlarged forward-facing eyes. The eye field itself often looks distinctly pale/whitish in Zoropsis spinimana.
characters.body_shape: Large, robust, flattish hunting spider that at first glance looks like a big wolf spider (hence 'false wolf spider'). Heavy oval abdomen, broad sturdy cephalothorax (carapace), fairly long legs. Body length clearly bigger than most house spiders.
characters.legs: Long, sturdy, and clearly banded/annulated with dark rings on a yellowish-grey to reddish-yellow ground; overall speckled brownish appearance. Robust and spiny. No comb of long curved bristles visible on the hind feet to the naked eye (the cribellate spinning comb, the calamistrum, is microscopic).
characters.colour: Mottled brown and pale. In Z. spinimana the carapace is yellow-whitish with a bold black pattern that famously resembles a vampire/Nosferatu face, the abdomen is yellow-whitish at the front, dark/black in the middle and greyish at the rear. Z. media is more reddish-orange with two brown dorsal bands; Z. rufipes (Madeira) has reddish legs. Overall impression: a large drab brown-and-cream spider with a dark patterned back.
