characters.behaviour_habitat: Ground- and surface-dwelling nocturnal ambush hunter of open woodland, found under stones and bark. Strongly synanthropic: very often comes into houses, garages and on walls, especially in autumn, which is when people most notice the big 'Nosferatu spider'. Mediterranean climate species; in Portugal on the mainland (Z. spinimana, Z. media) and on Madeira (Z. rufipes).

source.title: Spiders of Europe — Genus key: Zoropsidae
source.authors: Nentwig W, Blick T, Bosmans R, Gloor D, Hänggi A, Kropf C (araneae.nmbe.ch)

couplets.character: Form of the male pedipalp (conductor, embolar lamella, tegular process and median apophysis) and of the female epigyne; geographic range

options.text: Male pedipalp WITH a conductor, an embolar lamella and a membranous tegular process; median apophysis with an unsclerotised base; female epigyne with a U- to V-shaped scape; southern Europe (the only genus reaching Portugal)

Male pedipalp WITHOUT a conductor, embolar lamella or membranous tegular process; median apophysis fully sclerotised with a simple tip; female epigyne with a short, mushroom-shaped scape; endemic to Cyprus

deeper_key.notes: The online genus key covers only the 2 European genera (Zoropsis and Akamasia) and is small enough to transcribe in full. In Portugal this collapses to a single genus, Zoropsis — Akamasia is endemic to Cyprus and does not occur here, so any Portuguese zoropsid is Zoropsis. Note the genus key relies on genitalia (male palp, female epigyne) seen under a microscope, NOT on field-visible characters. Below genus there is NO clean dichotomous species key on araneae.nmbe.ch (the Zoropsis species page is a side-by-side comparison of palps and epigynes, not a couplet key). Reliable species separation of the 3 Portuguese species (Z. spinimana, Z. media, Z. rufipes) requires an adult and examination of the genitalia under magnification — Z. spinimana: male cymbium dorsally with a field of short dense bristles and a hook-shaped median apophysis, female epigyne with a long, narrow, finger-shaped scape. In practice, a large patterned 'Nosferatu' spider found in a house on the Portuguese mainland is overwhelmingly Z. spinimana; Z. media is more reddish with two dark bands; Z. rufipes is the Madeiran species with reddish legs. Juveniles and females often cannot be named to species from a photo alone.

field_questions.ask: Is it a big robust spider (body well over 1 cm, females up to ~2 cm) that looks like a hairy wolf spider but with eyes that are all roughly the same small size, NOT two big forward eyes?
field_questions.yes_means: Consistent with Zoropsidae (false wolf spider) rather than a true wolf spider, which has two large dominant eyes.

field_questions.ask: Did you find it walking on a wall, floor or under bark, with NO catching web around it?
field_questions.yes_means: Matches a free-living zoropsid hunter; they do not build snare webs.

field_questions.ask: Does the carapace have a bold dark-on-pale pattern that looks a bit like a face/mask (the 'Nosferatu' look), and is the abdomen pale at the front with a dark patch in the middle?
field_questions.yes_means: Strongly suggests Zoropsis spinimana, the common Portuguese mainland house-entering species.

field_questions.ask: Are the legs long, sturdy and clearly marked with darker bands/rings on a lighter ground?
field_questions.yes_means: Consistent with Zoropsis; banded legs plus the mask pattern together are a good Zoropsidae signal.

records.found_check: Zoropsis spinimana (the species the user photographed) fits every family character above: it is a large robust 'false wolf spider' (males 10-13 mm, females 10-19 mm), free-living with no snare web, with eyes in two rows that are more equal in size than a wolf spider's, banded legs, and the diagnostic dark-on-pale 'Nosferatu' carapace pattern with a pale-front/black-middle abdomen. It is strongly synanthropic and the species people most often find indoors in Portugal in autumn — confirming the family characters are correct for the user's animal.

lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Wolf spiders have a dramatic eye pattern with two very large forward-facing eyes (plus a row of small ones); Zoropsidae eyes are all small and more equal in size and evenly spread. Also, wolf spiders are ecribellate and the female carries her egg-sac attached to her spinnerets, whereas a zoropsid mother guards a sac in a retreat.

Huntsman spiders hold their legs crab-like (laterigrade, turned sideways) and are very flat and fast; Zoropsidae hold their legs in the normal forward/backward (prograde) wolf-spider posture and look bulkier.

Nursery-web spiders are also large prograde hunters, but again show the wolf-spider-like eye arrangement with some enlarged eyes and the female carries the egg-sac in her jaws; Zoropsidae have the smaller, more even eyes and the dark mask pattern, and do not carry the sac in the chelicerae.
Yellow sac spiders (e.g. Cheiracanthium) are far smaller, pale/uniform yellowish-green without the bold dark carapace mask, and lack the heavy banded legs and large size of Zoropsis.

characters.web: Free-living nocturnal hunter, no snare web for catching prey. Spins only an irregular, loose, sticky tangle of sheet-like silk as a daytime retreat — typically a flattened messy mat in a crack, behind furniture, under bark or stones, or in undisturbed corners of buildings. If you see a loose untidy silk patch tucked in a dry crevice with no neat geometric structure, that fits.
characters.eyes: SIX eyes, not the usual eight. They are arranged as three well-separated PAIRS (dyads) in a wide arc across the front of the head — one pair in the middle and one pair on each side. This 6-eyes-in-3-pairs layout is the single most reliable naked-eye/phone-macro giveaway for this family in Portugal.
characters.body_shape: Small to medium, flat and low-slung. Body in two parts: a rounded, somewhat pear-shaped or oval head region (cephalothorax/prosoma, often with a faint dark central groove) and a plain oval abdomen with no bold pattern. Often a darker violin- or fiddle-shaped mark on top of the head region with the neck of the violin pointing backwards (clearest in L. rufescens; can be faint or worn).
characters.legs: Long, thin, fairly uniform legs, noticeably longer than the body, held in a low sprawling posture. Tarsi (leg tips) bear only TWO claws (a haplogyne feature; not visible without magnification). Legs are uniformly pale, the same yellow/orange/reddish tone as the body, without rings or banding.
characters.colour: Uniform light tan, yellow-brown, orange-brown to reddish-brown, with little contrast; head region usually slightly darker toward the front. No bright colours, no spots, no striking stripes. The only marking of note is the darker violin shape on the head region.
characters.behaviour_habitat: Strongly synanthropic and warmth-loving (thermophilic): found in and around houses and buildings, cellars, garages, behind skirting boards, in stored boxes; outdoors under stones, in dry rock crevices and caves (troglophile). Nocturnal, reclusive and shy — flees from light and disturbance, rarely seen in the open by day. Bite is medically significant (can cause a slow necrotic lesion) but the spider is non-aggressive.

field_questions.ask: Count the eyes in a clear close-up: are there SIX (not eight)?
field_questions.yes_means: Six eyes alone already points strongly to a recluse (Sicariidae) in Portugal — very few PT spiders have six eyes.

field_questions.ask: Are those six eyes set as three separate PAIRS spread in a curved row across the front?
field_questions.yes_means: Three pairs (dyads) confirms the recluse eye pattern and rules out most six-eyed lookalikes that clump their eyes differently.

field_questions.ask: Is the whole spider a plain, uniform tan/yellow-brown to reddish-brown with no bold pattern, and the legs the same colour with no rings?
field_questions.yes_means: Consistent with a recluse; rules out boldly patterned or banded-legged spiders.

field_questions.ask: Is there a darker violin/fiddle-shaped mark on top of the head region, neck pointing toward the abdomen?
field_questions.yes_means: Supports Loxosceles (especially L. rufescens), the recluse genus; helps separate from unmarked six-eyed families.

field_questions.ask: Was it found indoors or in a dry crevice/under a stone, with at most a loose messy silk retreat and no neat snare web?
field_questions.yes_means: Fits the free-living, synanthropic, retreat-only lifestyle of recluses rather than a web-builder.

lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Also have six eyes in three pairs, but Scytodes has a high, strongly domed head region carrying conspicuous black/brown spots or a leopard-like dark pattern on a pale body, and ringed/banded legs — versus the flat head and plain uniform colour of a recluse. Scytodes walks slowly and lacks the violin mark.

Also six-eyed, but the eyes are clustered close together in a tight oval group (not three widely spaced pairs), the body is reddish with a distinctly dark/greyish abdomen, and the jaws (chelicerae) are large and forward-projecting. Recluses have widely separated eye pairs and small inconspicuous jaws.

Share indoor habitats and pale colour, but pholcids have extremely long thread-like legs, usually eight eyes (two side triads plus a small middle pair), and build a loose tangled aerial web they hang in upside down. Recluses are stockier, six-eyed, ground-hugging hunters with no aerial web.

Plain brown free-living nocturnal hunters that share crevices and retreats, but they have the normal eight eyes (the rear-middle pair often oval/flattened) and prominent cylindrical, widely-spaced spinnerets at the rear. Counting eight eyes rules out a recluse.

source.title: araneae — Spiders of Europe, genus key for Sicariidae
source.authors: Nentwig W, Blick T, Bosmans R, Kropf C, Stäubli A (2026) Spiders of Europe. Version 06.2026

deeper_key.notes: The araneae.nmbe.ch genus key for Sicariidae is not a branching key: it states there is ONLY ONE genus in Europe, Loxosceles. So once a Portuguese spider is keyed to family Sicariidae (six eyes in three pairs, two tarsal claws, plain build, retreat-only silk), it is automatically genus Loxosceles — no further couplets exist at the online genus level. Below genus there is NO reliable field key. The two Portuguese species, L. rufescens (native Mediterranean) and L. laeta (introduced South American, established at Porto), look essentially identical externally; the L. laeta records were confirmed only by examining the genitalia of captured males under a microscope (Gil & Grosso-Silva). Reliable species identification therefore requires a microscope and adult genitalia (male palp / female internal genitalia), and ideally locality context (L. laeta currently very localised around Porto). Treat naked-eye/photo IDs as 'Loxosceles sp.' and default to L. rufescens only as the far more widespread species, never as a certainty.

couplets.character: Number of genera of Sicariidae in Europe

options.text: Only one genus present in Europe — six eyes in three diads, haplogyne, two tarsal claws, plain tan to reddish-brown body, retreat-only silk

records.found_check: The user's photographed Loxosceles rufescens fits every family character: araneae.nmbe.ch gives it body length ~7.0-7.5 mm (within the 6-12 mm family range), prosoma orange-brown / reddish-yellow and uniformly reddish-yellow legs (plain uniform colour, unringed legs as described), the genus-diagnostic six eyes in three pairs, the violin mark on the head region, a free-living retreat-only habit, and a strongly synanthropic/troglophile habitat (in and around houses, under stones, rock crevices, caves) — all matching the characters above. The second Portuguese species, L. laeta, is the same genus and matches the family characters identically; it differs from L. rufescens only in genitalia (microscope), which is exactly why the deeper key stops at genus.

characters.web: Builds a silk-lined funnel-and-tube retreat: a sheet of silk among stones, roots, leaf litter or in crevices, running back into a tubular funnel-shaped retreat, often with loose trip-threads radiating from the entrance. The spider waits at the mouth of the tube and rushes out when prey hits the threads. Not an aerial orb web and not a free-roaming hunter.
characters.eyes: Eight small eyes set close together in one tight compact group on a slight mound near the front of the head — they look like a little clustered patch rather than spread out in two clear rows. Hard to count without a magnifier; the key point is they are bunched, not in wide-set rows.
characters.body_shape: Large, heavy-bodied mygalomorph (a 'primitive' spider, like a small funnel-web relative of tarantulas). Low, flat, glossy carapace; chunky oval abdomen. The fangs strike straight down (point downward, roughly parallel) rather than pinching sideways. The single most eye-catching feature is a pair of very long finger-like spinnerets sticking out conspicuously from the rear tip of the abdomen.
characters.legs: Eight stout, fairly long legs, uniformly dark and glossy. Males have relatively longer, thinner legs than the bulkier females. No bright bands or rings. Front legs of the female carry distinctive paddle-shaped spines on the first leg segment (coxa), but this needs a close look or photo to see.
characters.colour: Overall satiny black to very dark brown in life — body, legs and carapace all dark and shiny with no pattern. The abdomen can show a purplish-violet sheen in fresh living animals; preserved specimens fade to dark grey/red-brown. Effectively a uniformly black, glossy spider.
characters.behaviour_habitat: Ground-dwelling and sedentary, living in its silk tube among rocks, roots, banks and leaf litter, classically in cork-oak and oak woodland but also in scrub, gardens, dumps and other disturbed sites. Native to the southwest Iberian Peninsula (southern Portugal, southern Spain, Gibraltar) and northwest Africa; in Portugal it is the single representative of the family. Occasionally turns up elsewhere in Europe transported in the root-balls of imported potted olive trees. It is the only spider species protected under EU law (Habitats Directive).

field_questions.ask: Is it a large (2 cm or more body), uniformly glossy black or very dark spider with no pattern?
field_questions.yes_means: Consistent with Macrothele calpeiana, the only member of this family in Portugal.

field_questions.ask: Are there two long, finger-like spinnerets sticking out conspicuously from the back tip of the abdomen?
field_questions.yes_means: Strongly points to Macrothelidae — long projecting posterior spinnerets are a hallmark of this family (separates it from tarantulas, whose spinnerets are short).

field_questions.ask: Do the fangs point straight down (so the spider would bite by striking downward), rather than pinching together sideways?
field_questions.yes_means: It is a mygalomorph (primitive group containing Macrothelidae, tarantulas, trapdoor spiders), not a typical 'modern' spider.

field_questions.ask: Is it living in a silk-lined tube/funnel retreat among stones, roots or leaf litter (not in an aerial web and not freely wandering)?
field_questions.yes_means: Fits the funnel-and-tube lifestyle of Macrothele.

field_questions.ask: Are the eight eyes bunched together in one tight little group near the front of the head?
field_questions.yes_means: Matches the compact mygalomorph eye cluster of Macrothelidae.

lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Tarantulas are hairier and bulkier with dense claw tufts on the foot tips and SHORT spinnerets; Macrothelidae has comparatively sparse hair and very LONG, conspicuous finger-like posterior spinnerets projecting from the abdomen. In Portugal true tarantulas are essentially absent, so a big black mygalomorph with long spinnerets is Macrothele.

These mygalomorphs have short spinnerets and dense scopula brushes on the front feet, and typically live in burrows or trapdoor tubes in soil; Macrothelidae has the very long projecting posterior spinnerets and a broad open pit-shaped fovea, and spins an obvious silk funnel/sheet retreat rather than a soil burrow with a lid.
Atypids have SIX spinnerets and build a closed silk purse-tube up against a stone or tree base; Macrothelidae has only FOUR spinnerets (the rear pair very long) and an open funnel retreat.

Agelenids are 'modern' spiders: fangs pinch sideways, eyes spread in two clear rows, body usually brownish and patterned, and they are far less bulky. Macrothele's downward-striking parallel fangs, clustered eyes, glossy black colour and long projecting spinnerets separate it instantly even though both make funnel sheets.

source.title: Spiders of Europe — Key to genera of Macrothelidae (genkey 60) and family-level Key to families
source.authors: Nentwig, W., Blick, T., Bosmans, R., Gloor, D., Hänggi, A. & Kropf, C.

deeper_key.notes: The araneae.nmbe.ch genus key for Macrothelidae terminates immediately: in Europe the family contains only ONE genus, Macrothele, so reaching the family automatically gives you the genus. There is no online dichotomous couplet structure WITHIN the family on this site — the family is reached at couplet 6 of the site's family-level 'Key to families' (orthognath/mygalomorph; posterior lateral spinnerets very long; posterior median spinnerets wide apart; fovea an open broad pit; labium with numerous cuspules; no dense spinefields on anterior tarsi/metatarsi). For SPECIES the site presents three Macrothele in Europe (calpeiana, cretica from Crete, drolshageni from Turkey) only as comparative image/distribution pages, NOT as a couplet key. In practice species separation is by geography (in Portugal the only species is M. calpeiana) and, for certainty, by microscopic examination of genitalia — the male palpal bulb/embolus and the female's paired long coiled spermathecae. So in Portugal a naked-eye/photo ID confidently reaches genus Macrothele and, by range, species M. calpeiana; truly nailing species elsewhere needs a microscope.

couplets.character: Number of genera in the family Macrothelidae as treated for Europe

options.text: Single genus in Europe — large mygalomorph with very long projecting posterior lateral spinnerets, open broad pit-shaped fovea, labium with numerous cuspules
options.leads_to: Macrothele (only genus; in Portugal = Macrothele calpeiana)

records.found_check: Confirmed. The family characters fit the user's photographed Macrothele calpeiana: araneae.nmbe.ch lists it as body length male 20.3-26.8 mm and female 28.2-34.7 mm (matching the 20-35 mm range), glossy black in life with a purple-violet abdomen, four spinnerets with very long three-segmented posterior laterals, a circular pit-shaped fovea, and a funnel/tube silk retreat in oakwoods and secondary habitats — and it is the sole Portuguese species of the family. The 'paddle-shaped spines on coxa I' and clustered eyes noted in the characters above are diagnostic features recorded for this exact species.

characters.web: Sheet web — a horizontal or domed hammock of fine, non-sticky silk. Larger species (Linyphiinae) build a distinct 'bowl and doily', dome, or flat sheet slung between grass, twigs or in low vegetation, often with a tangle of threads above that knocks insects down onto the sheet; the spider hangs upside-down beneath the sheet. The tiny dwarf species (Erigoninae) build minute, often almost invisible sheets in leaf litter, moss and at ground level.
characters.eyes: Eight eyes arranged in two horizontal rows of four. The anterior median (front-centre) eyes are usually the smallest and the rear/lateral eyes sit close together. Far too small to assess by naked eye in most species; a phone macro photo may show the two-row-of-four layout in larger individuals. A subtle field cue used by experts is a tall 'forehead' (clypeus) — the face below the eyes is noticeably high, more than twice the width of the front-centre eyes.
characters.body_shape: Compact, rounded cephalothorax with a relatively small, often oval to slightly elongated abdomen. Larger sheet-web species look like dainty, leggy little spiders hanging under a web; the dwarf 'money spiders' look like minute dark dots. In some males the head region is raised into bumps, lobes or turrets bearing the eyes — an odd but useful sign when present.
characters.legs: Generally long and slender relative to the small body, especially in the sheet-web builders; not robust or spiny-looking to the eye. Legs carry fine bristles and spines whose exact position is a major microscope-level ID character. A diagnostic family trait (microscope only) is a comb of teeth on the outer edge of the chelicerae (the tarsal claw comb).
characters.colour: Most are uniformly dark — black, blackish-brown or grey-brown — particularly the dwarf species. Sheet-web species can be paler with brown, yellowish or banded abdomens and sometimes faint chevrons or mottling. A few are brightly coloured (e.g. red-bodied forms exist in the family elsewhere), but in Portugal expect mostly drab dark to brown tones.
characters.behaviour_habitat: Extremely abundant and found almost everywhere — grassland, heath, leaf litter, moss, low vegetation, gardens, farmland and indoors. Famous for 'ballooning': they release silk threads and drift on the wind, even as adults, so numbers in a spot can change overnight. The classic sign in the field is dewy sheet webs glistening on grass and shrubs on damp mornings.

records.common_group: money spiders and sheet-web weavers (dwarf spiders)

field_questions.ask: Is the spider very small — a dark dot roughly 1-3 mm, or at most a dainty leggy spider up to about 6-8 mm?
field_questions.yes_means: Consistent with Linyphiidae; the family is dominated by tiny dwarf 'money spiders' and small sheet-web weavers.

field_questions.ask: Is there a horizontal sheet, hammock, dome or 'bowl-and-doily' web (not a sticky orb and not a messy cobweb), with the spider hanging upside-down underneath it?
field_questions.yes_means: Strongly points to Linyphiidae — they build non-sticky sheet webs and hang beneath them.

field_questions.ask: Is the body mostly plain dark — black or blackish-brown — with long thin legs and no bold pattern?
field_questions.yes_means: Typical of most Portuguese Linyphiidae, especially the dwarf spiders.

field_questions.ask: Did you find lots of tiny dewy sheet webs on grass or low plants on a damp morning, or see tiny spiders drifting/landing on silk threads (ballooning)?
field_questions.yes_means: Classic Linyphiidae behaviour — abundant sheet-web builders and habitual balloonists.

lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Theridiidae build irregular 3-D cobwebs (tangles) rather than a flat or domed sheet, and many have a rounded, almost spherical abdomen. The web shape is the easiest field tell: messy tangle = Theridiidae, organised sheet/hammock = Linyphiidae.

Araneidae build classic sticky wheel-shaped orb webs and are usually larger and more robust. Linyphiidae webs are non-sticky sheets, never a spoked orb. Under magnification the clypeus (face below the eyes) is tall in Linyphiidae and short in Araneidae.
Tetragnathidae spin (often tilted) orb webs and many have a very elongated body and large jaws. Linyphiidae do not build orbs and have short jaws and a compact body.

Hahniidae are also tiny and build delicate sheet webs at ground level, so they are easily confused. Their spinnerets sit in a single transverse row (visible only under magnification); separating them reliably needs a microscope, so this pair often cannot be split in the field.

source.title: Araneae - Spiders of Europe: Determination key for the genera of Linyphiidae
source.authors: Nentwig W, Blick T, Bosmans R, Gloor D, Hänggi A, Kropf C

deeper_key.notes: Araneae (Spiders of Europe) provides an online dichotomous key that reaches GENUS level for European Linyphiidae. It is very large — roughly 126 numbered couplets leading to ~140 genus endpoints (including a 'further genera' section) — and it is overwhelmingly genitalia- and microscopy-dependent: leads turn on male pedipalp (cymbium, palp) structure, female epigyne details, and the precise number and position of leg bristles, spines and trichobothria (e.g. trichobothrium position on metatarsus I expressed as a fraction like 0.35-0.6). None of this is observable in the field or from a phone photo. Below genus, species ID in this family almost always requires examining the mature male palp or female epigyne under a microscope (often after clearing), and immatures frequently cannot be named at all. Honest field ceiling for a non-expert: FAMILY only (recognise it as a money/sheet-web spider). To go further you need a stereomicroscope, the specimen in hand (ideally a mature adult), and the Araneae genus key plus species-level revisions; expect to stop at genus for many specimens even then.

characters.web: Free-living ground hunter, no snare web. Builds only a flat silken sac-like retreat under stones, bark or in leaf litter to rest and lay eggs in; it does not spin a web to catch prey.
characters.eyes: Eight eyes in two rows of four. The hallmark is the two rear-central eyes (posterior median eyes): instead of being round and shiny they are flattened, often oval, kidney-shaped or angular, and look dull, pale or silvery-grey rather than dark and beady. Hard to see without a phone macro shot, but distinctive once spotted.
characters.legs: Eight legs, fairly short and sturdy (not long and spindly), built for fast running on the ground. Tip of each leg has two claws hidden in dense tufts of hair (claw tufts) that let them grip smooth surfaces; legs often look slightly hairy.
characters.colour: Usually plain and drab: matte black, dark grey, slate, chocolate or sandy brown, frequently with a faint silky or velvety sheen. Many are uniform with no markings; some have a paler or bicoloured (black-and-white spotted/banded) abdomen. Ant-mimicking Micaria are shiny with white or metallic scaly hairs.
characters.body_shape: Smooth, low, somewhat flattened oval body in two parts. The abdomen is plump and rounded to slightly elongate; the most reliable field clue is at the rear tip, where two finger-like spinnerets stick out conspicuously, are cylindrical (parallel-sided, like two short tubes), and are clearly set apart from each other. Often a slight saddle-shaped pinch on top of the abdomen.
characters.behaviour_habitat: Nocturnal ground-dwellers. Found by day hiding in silk retreats under rocks, logs, bark, debris and leaf litter, in dry sunny places, gardens, walls and sometimes indoors. When uncovered they run fast in quick bursts then freeze. Active hunters that chase down prey (insects and other spiders) at night rather than waiting in a web.

records.common_group: ground spiders (stealthy ground spiders / mouse spiders)

field_questions.ask: Are there two short finger-like spinnerets sticking out at the very tip of the abdomen, clearly separated from each other (not touching)?
field_questions.yes_means: Strong sign of Gnaphosidae — the widely-spaced cylindrical anterior spinnerets are the single best field clue and separate them from look-alike sac spiders.

field_questions.ask: Is the spider plain and drab (black, grey or brown), with a smooth low body and rather short legs, and no obvious web?
field_questions.yes_means: Consistent with a ground spider that hunts on foot rather than catching prey in a web.

field_questions.ask: When you uncovered it (under a stone, log or bark), did it dash off in a fast burst and then go still?
field_questions.yes_means: Typical gnaphosid behaviour — fast nocturnal ground hunters that shelter in silk retreats by day.

field_questions.ask: In a sharp phone macro, do the two rear-central eyes look flattened, oval/angular and dull or silvery rather than round and dark?
field_questions.yes_means: The modified flat posterior median eyes are a defining gnaphosid trait (though easy to miss without a good close-up).

lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Sac spiders have their anterior spinnerets close together, touching and conical (tapering), whereas gnaphosids have them cylindrical and clearly set apart. Clubionids also have normal round shiny rear eyes, often a paler straw/yellow body, and longer legs.

Similar drab ground-living build, but they lack the gnaphosid's flattened oval posterior median eyes and the prominent widely-separated cylindrical spinnerets; their legs often carry conspicuous paired spines beneath, and spinnerets are short and conical.

Very close relatives that also have flattened posterior median eyes; separated mainly by chelicerae angled forward/sideways and other microscope-level features. Rare in Portugal, so an unfamiliar gnaphosid-looking spider is far more likely to be a true gnaphosid.

lookalikes.family: Corinnidae / Trachelidae (ant-like or hard-bodied sac spiders)
lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Can resemble ant-mimicking Micaria, but corinnids/trachelids have round shiny eyes and spinnerets that are short and not the parallel cylindrical pair; Micaria show distinctive white or metallic scaly hairs on body and legs.

source.title: Araneae - Spiders of Europe: Genus determination key for Gnaphosidae
source.authors: Nentwig W, Blick T, Bosmans R, Gloor D, Hänggi A, Kropf C; key adapted from Wunderlich (2011)

deeper_key.notes: A real, online, illustrated dichotomous GENUS-level key exists and covers the European genera (the main key runs to 36 couplets, with ~16 further genera listed separately). It gets you to genus only — NOT to species. Crucially, almost every couplet relies on characters you CANNOT see in the field or in a phone photo: the shape of the male pedipalp (tibial/median apophysis, embolus, conductor, intercalary sclerites), the female epigyne, cheliceral retromargin teeth/keels, and fine leg-bristle counts (e.g. preening combs on metatarsi III-IV, retrolateral bristles on patella IV, ventral tibial bristles). You therefore need an adult specimen, a stereo microscope, and usually genitalia (often cleared) to run this key. A few leads use grosser features visible at low magnification — ant-shaped body with white/metallic scales (Micaria), bicoloured black-and-white abdomen (Aphantaulax, Poecilochroa, Cesonia and allies), or a serrated cheliceral keel with the eye pattern of Gnaphosa. Below genus, species identification is even harder: Portugal's ~139 species sit in large, taxonomically difficult genera (notably Zelotes, Drassyllus, Haplodrassus, Gnaphosa, Micaria, Trachyzelotes), and most species can only be separated by examining the genitalia under a microscope, ideally against the species pages and figures on araneae.nmbe.ch and the World Spider Catalog. Realistic field ceiling for a non-expert: FAMILY (confident from spinnerets + eyes + behaviour) and occasionally a recognisable genus group; species-level naming is generally not possible from a photo. If no usable species key is available for a given genus, the honest answer is to record it as 'Gnaphosidae, genus X' and, where collection is permitted, retain a voucher for an arachnologist.
