records.found_check: No species were supplied by the user (they have not yet photographed any Gnaphosidae). The family characters above were checked for internal consistency against the araneae.nmbe.ch family key and genus key and against independent descriptive sources (DELTA Spiders of Britain & Ireland; EOL); they hold across the family. Note the one built-in caveat captured in the key itself: the ant-mimicking genus Micaria has short (not long cylindrical) spinnerets and is instead recognised by its white/metallic scaly hairs and ant-like habitus, so the 'protruding cylindrical spinnerets' field clue can fail for that one genus.

characters.web: Free-living hunters, no snare web. They do not build a catching web; they sit on plant stems, leaves, bark, walls or low vegetation and dash after prey, then ambush it. Females spin only a flat silk sac to guard eggs.
characters.eyes: Eight small eyes set in two gently curved rows across the front of a fairly flat head, the back row wider than the front row. The eyes are roughly similar in size and sit on a low head (not raised on bumps or turrets), so on a phone photo you mostly just see two arched rows of tiny dots.
characters.body_shape: Crab-like and noticeably flat and low-slung, looking a bit like a flattened, faded crab spider but lankier. The abdomen is oval to (in the slender grass species) long and cigar-shaped. The whole spider looks built to lie pressed flat against bark, a leaf or a wall.
characters.legs: Eight long legs held out sideways crab-fashion (laterigrade), letting it scuttle sideways and run fast. Unlike true crab spiders, all four pairs are fairly similar in length and slenderness rather than the front two pairs being hugely thicker; often the second pair is the longest. In the beach/sand genus Halodromus the second leg is dramatically longer (1.4x or more than leg I). A magnifier shows dense hairy pads (scopulae) only at the very tips of the feet.
characters.colour: Usually drab and camouflaged: cream, grey, sandy, or light-to-mid brown, often with faint paler/darker lengthwise stripes or a dappled, lichen-like or bark-like mottling. Many have a darker central band or, in the genus Thanatus, a small dark spear/lance-shaped mark on the front third of the abdomen. The plain, dusty look helps them vanish against bark, dead grass or stone.
characters.behaviour_habitat: Fast, alert daytime runners found on tree trunks and bark, shrubs, tall grass and reeds, heathland, walls and fences, and (some species) sand and beaches; a few enter buildings and catch flies on walls. When disturbed they sprint sideways and freeze flattened against the surface. Common across Portugal in gardens, scrub, grassland and woodland edges.

field_questions.ask: Does the spider hold its legs out sideways like a crab and run/scuttle sideways fast, rather than walking forward like a normal spider?
field_questions.yes_means: Consistent with a crab-type spider (Philodromidae, Thomisidae or huntsman) rather than the many forward-walking families.

field_questions.ask: Are all the legs fairly similar in length and thickness (NOT a pair of hugely thicker, much longer front legs)?
field_questions.yes_means: Points to Philodromidae (running crab spider) rather than a true crab spider (Thomisidae), which usually has obviously enlarged, beefier front legs.

field_questions.ask: Is the spider drab and flattened - cream, grey, sandy or brown, often with faint lengthwise stripes or bark-like mottling - and pressed flat on bark, a wall, grass or a leaf?
field_questions.yes_means: Typical of Philodromidae body and lifestyle.

field_questions.ask: Is there NO catching web - the spider is just sitting out in the open or running, not hanging in a snare?
field_questions.yes_means: Fits a free-living hunter like Philodromidae rather than a web-building family.

lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Thomisidae have the front two leg pairs distinctly longer AND much thicker/stouter than the back two, giving a strongly lopsided crab look; their bodies are often shorter, wider and sometimes brightly coloured (white, yellow, pink) sitting on flowers. Philodromidae have legs of more even length/thickness (often leg II longest), a flatter drabber body, and live on bark, grass and walls and run fast rather than ambushing in flowers.

Huntsmen are usually much larger and flatter with dense hairy pads (scopulae) along the whole underside of the feet and lower legs; Philodromidae are smaller and have scopulae only at the very tips of the feet.

Jumping spiders have two huge forward-facing front eyes and a short, blunt, upright body and stalk-and-jump; Philodromidae have eight small similar-sized eyes in two arched rows, a flat sideways-crab posture, and run rather than jump.

source.title: Araneae - Spiders of Europe: Genus key for Philodromidae (provided by Christoph Muster)
source.authors: Nentwig W, Blick T, Bosmans R, Gloor D, Hänggi A, Kropf C; key by Christoph Muster

couplets.character: Length of leg II relative to leg I, size of anterior median eyes, and male palp patella

options.text: Leg II strongly elongated (at least 1.4 times as long as leg I); anterior median eyes distinctly enlarged; patella of male palp with an apophysis
Not so (leg II not so strongly elongated)

couplets.character: Position of the posterior median eyes; presence of dense scopula on tarsi/metatarsi

options.text: Posterior median eyes closer to the posterior lateral eyes than to each other; tarsi and metatarsi without dense scopula in males
Posterior median eyes closer to each other than to the posterior lateral eyes (or equidistant); tarsi and metatarsi with dense scopula in both sexes

couplets.character: Shape of the opisthosoma (abdomen) and presence of a dark lanceolate mark

options.text: Opisthosoma strongly elongated (more than 1.8 times longer than wide), without a dark lanceolate mark
Opisthosoma less than 1.8 times longer than wide, in the distal third usually with a dark lanceolate (spear-shaped) mark

deeper_key.notes: The araneae.nmbe.ch online genus key (by Christoph Muster) is short - only 3 couplets - and reliably resolves the main European/Portuguese genera Halodromus, Philodromus, Tibellus and Thanatus. It is NOT exhaustive at genus level: four further genera relevant to the European/Portuguese fauna (Celerrimus, Emargidromus, Pulchellodromus, Rhysodromus) are listed separately under 'Further genera' and are NOT keyed out by the dichotomous couplets - several of these were recently split out of Philodromus, so older specimens keyed to 'Philodromus' may actually belong to them. Two couplets (1 and 2) already lean on microscope-only characters (enlarged anterior median eyes, eye spacing, scopula density, male palp apophysis), so confident genus placement usually needs a hand lens or low-power microscope. Species-level identification in this family is genuinely hard: most species can only be named by examining the genitalia (male palp / female epigyne) under a microscope, and the species-level keys for Philodromus in particular are notoriously difficult with many similar, variable species. There is no reliable naked-eye/photo route to species for most of this family - a clear photo will usually only get you to genus, or to a species group at best. For species, consult the per-species pages and determination keys on araneae.nmbe.ch, or regional revisions of Philodromus, Thanatus and Tibellus, and expect to need a scope.

characters.web: Most are snare-web builders: small, irregular criss-cross mesh ('lacework' or hackled cribellate web) spun over the tips of leaves, dead flower/seed heads, twigs, or in cracks of walls and rock. The silk is fuzzy/woolly (cribellate) so it traps prey by tangle rather than glue, and often looks dusty and grey with caught debris. Wall-living Brigittea spins palm-sized dirty-looking sheets around a crack. A few small ground/litter genera (e.g. Lathys, Argenna) make only sparse retreat silk.
characters.eyes: Eight small eyes set close together in two rows on the front of the head; in plant-dwelling Dictyna/Nigma they sit in a compact group. No striking eye pattern visible to the naked eye — they just look like a tiny clump of dots. (Eye detail is hard to use in the field; better cues are the woolly web and the dense furry body.)
characters.body_shape: Tiny, compact spider. Carapace (front body) fairly flat and rounded; abdomen oval to slightly elongate. The whole body is clothed in a dense coat of short fine hairs, giving a soft, furry, sometimes velvety look. In males of some genera (e.g. Dictyna) the head region is noticeably raised/swollen.
characters.legs: Short to moderate legs, not long and spindly. Legs roughly equal in length, fairly stout, often faintly ringed (annulated) with darker bands. Females carry a calamistrum — a single comb-row of curved bristles on the top of the hind leg (4th leg) metatarsus — used to comb out the woolly cribellate silk, but this needs a loupe to see.
characters.colour: Variable by genus. Many Dictyna are dark brown to black on the carapace with a paler grey abdomen marked by a brown heart-stripe and chevrons, and a frosting of white/pale hairs. Nigma stands out: bright leaf-green or yellow-green (sometimes reddish in males), often with a pale margin — a distinctive green dictynid on foliage. Brigittea (walls) is dark brown with a patterned greyish abdomen.
characters.behaviour_habitat: Mostly small ambush dwellers that sit in a silk retreat at the edge of their mesh web and rush out onto trapped insects. Found on the tips of low plants, bushes, grass and flower heads (Dictyna, Argenna), on leaves of trees and shrubs (Nigma, often green and camouflaged on a leaf), in leaf litter and under bark (Lathys), or synanthropically on sunny walls, rocks and house facades (Brigittea civica, often in colonies). Active spring to autumn; widespread in Portugal in gardens, scrub, meadows and woodland edges.

source.title: Araneae - Spiders of Europe: Genus-level identification key for Dictynidae
source.authors: Nentwig W, Blick T, Bosmans R, Gloor D, Hänggi A, Kropf C, Stäubli A (Spiders of Europe)

couplets.character: Cribellum (silk plate in front of spinnerets)

options.text: Cribellum absent
Cribellum present
options.leads_to: 2 (cribellate branch; key continues toward couplet 3 / spined-leg and conductor characters)

couplets.character: Retrotibial apophysis / habitat

options.text: Retrotibial apophysis absent, tip of conductor straight, embolus short, epigyne with septum (intertidal species)
8 eyes (general dictynid condition)

couplets.character: Cribellum shape

options.text: Cribellum divided (bipartite)
Cribellum undivided (entire)

couplets.character: Colour and palp

options.text: Dark, almost black, no white (guanine) pigment, palp with ctenidia
Bright green or red with white guanine pigment, male palp with small patellar apophysis

couplets.character: Leg spines

options.text: Legs with spines
Legs without spines

couplets.character: Size / cymbium

options.text: Minute (<2 mm), cymbium unmodified
Medium (>2 mm), normal spines on all legs, cymbium with basal fold

couplets.character: Male palp ctenidia / epigyne ridges

options.text: Male palp with ctenidia, epigyne with ridges
Male palp without ctenidia, epigyne without ridges

couplets.character: Male cephalic region / epigyne

options.text: Cephalic part strongly raised in males; epigyne with two dark receptacula anterior to epigastric furrow
Cephalic part not strongly raised; epigynal ridges present

couplets.character: Male chelicera / embolic base

options.text: Male chelicera with lateral boss, embolic base central, loops >360°
No lateral boss, embolic base prolateral-basal or central-basal

couplets.character: Embolus form

options.text: Embolus thick, short (starts 8-9 o'clock)
Embolus thin, short to long (starts 6-9)

couplets.character: Male palpal patella outgrowth (truncated — key continues to Lathys, Arctella, Argenna, Hackmania, Chaerea)

options.text: Patella with conical outgrowth/apophysis, receptacles round
Patella without outgrowth

deeper_key.notes: The araneae.nmbe.ch genus key for Dictynidae is genuine and was fetched; it runs ~15 couplets and covers ~14 keyed genera (plus several extra genera listed outside the dichotomy). I have transcribed the leading couplets above; later steps (11-15, splitting Lathys, Argenna, Arctella, Hackmania, Chaerea) are truncated here. CRITICAL field honesty: this key is genus-level and leans almost entirely on microscope characters — the cribellum, the calamistrum comb on the hind leg, leg spination, male palp structure (ctenidia, emboli, apophyses) and female epigyne. A naked-eye or phone-photo observer realistically cannot run it. In the field you can usually only place a spider as 'a dictynid' (small, furry, sitting in a woolly mesh web) and at best guess the easy genera by gestalt: bright green on a leaf = Nigma; dirty palm-sized web on a sunny wall = Brigittea; small dark spider in lacework on a flower/seed head = Dictyna. Confident species ID in Portugal's ~19 species almost always requires a mature adult examined under a microscope (epigyne for females, expanded palp for males), and several species pairs need genitalic dissection. There is no reliable species-level key usable from photographs for most of this family.

field_questions.ask: Is the spider very small (under about 5 mm body) and clearly fuzzy/furry, with a dense coat of fine hairs?
field_questions.yes_means: Consistent with Dictynidae (their bodies are notably hairy/velvety).

field_questions.ask: Is it sitting in a small, irregular, woolly-looking mesh web that traps dust and debris (on a leaf tip, flower/seed head, twig, or on a wall crack) rather than a neat orb or a clean sheet/funnel?
field_questions.yes_means: Strongly suggests a cribellate mesh-web spider such as Dictynidae.

field_questions.ask: If it is on green foliage, is the spider itself bright leaf-green or yellow-green?
field_questions.yes_means: Points to the genus Nigma within Dictynidae.

field_questions.ask: Is it a small dark patterned spider living in a colony of dirty palm-sized webs on a sunny building wall, rock, or fence?
field_questions.yes_means: Points to Brigittea civica (a wall-dwelling dictynid).

records.found_check: No species were supplied by the user (no personal photos yet). The family characters above (tiny size, dense body hairs, cribellate woolly mesh web, calamistrum comb on the hind leg, converging gnathocoxae) match the documented Portuguese/Iberian Dictynidae fauna, which is dominated by Dictyna, Nigma, Lathys, Argenna, Brigittea, Altella and a few others, and are consistent with araneae.nmbe.ch species pages for Dictyna arundinacea, Nigma flavescens and Brigittea civica that I fetched.

lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Also small, dark and cribellate, so the woolly web looks similar. Distinguish under magnification: Dictynidae have converging gnathocoxae (jaw plates angling together at their tips) and a dense furry hair coat; Titanoecidae have nearly parallel gnathocoxae and males show short lateral spurs on the chelicerae. Titanoecids are generally larger and live more under stones/bark.
Also cribellate and make lacy bluish webs in crevices, but amaurobiids are clearly larger and stockier with longer legs; dictynids are tiny (mostly under 4-5 mm) and daintier.

Tiny theridiids in tangled webs can be confused, but theridiid silk is sticky-gluey and not woolly, the spiders are typically smooth and globular (less furry), and they have a comb of serrated bristles on the hind tarsus rather than a calamistrum comb on the metatarsus. Their web is not cribellate so it does not trap dust the same way.

lookalikes.family: Hahniidae / Cybaeidae and other tiny sheet-weavers
lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: These small spiders are not cribellate, so they make thin clean sheet webs with no woolly hackled silk and no dust-trapping fuzz; they also lack the dense furry body coat typical of dictynids.

characters.web: Free-living hunters, no snare web. They do not build a capture web at all. Some (genus Agroeca) make a very distinctive small white egg sac shaped like an upside-down lantern or fairy lamp on a thin stalk, coated with soil so it looks like a tiny dirty pendant hanging under a stone, on a stem or on heather.
characters.eyes: Eight small eyes in two rows. The front (anterior) row is more or less straight, with the eyes set fairly close together. The back row is roughly equidistant or slightly curved. No single huge pair, no widely spreading pattern. To the naked eye the eye region just looks like a small even cluster up front. Eye details usually need a hand lens.
characters.body_shape: Small, fairly flat, oval-bodied spider with a smooth, somewhat shiny carapace and no obvious humps or projections. Looks like a typical fast little ground spider rather than anything ornate.
characters.legs: Moderately long, slightly built running legs ending in three claws. Look closely (hand lens or zoomed phone photo) for rows of stiff downward-pointing spines (bristle pairs) on the underside of the front tibiae and metatarsi, which become darker toward the leg tips. No dense brush or pad of hair at the tips in most species (no conspicuous claw tufts), separating them from true sac spiders.
characters.colour: Mostly drab earth tones: yellow-brown, red-brown to greyish brown. Carapace often with darker radiating lines and dark margins; abdomen grey to brown, frequently with a paler central pattern or a row of dark chevrons (angle marks) toward the rear. Agroeca species have shiny golden hairs and chevron marks; Liocranum is browner with a dark radial pattern and black-edged carapace.
characters.behaviour_habitat: Ground-living nocturnal hunters of leaf litter, moss, grass tussocks, under stones and bark, in dry steppe and cliff habitats, woodland, bogs, heathland and dry meadows; some (Liocranum) also come into buildings and onto walls. Females tend to hide in a silk retreat or burrow; wandering males are what people most often catch. Run fast when disturbed.

records.common_group: liocranid sac/ground spiders (the family with Agroeca's distinctive 'fairy-lamp' egg sacs)

source.title: araneae – Spiders of Europe, genus-level identification key for Liocranidae
source.authors: Nentwig W, Blick T, Bosmans R, Gloor D, Hänggi A, Kropf C (Spiders of Europe / araneae.nmbe.ch)

deeper_key.notes: araneae.nmbe.ch provides a working online dichotomous genus-level key for Liocranidae, transcribed in full below (8 main genera reached: Apostenus, Scotina, Drapeta, Liocranum, Mesiotelus, Agroeca, Agraecina, Liocranoeca; the European fauna also includes Cybaeodes, Liocranoeca and a few minor/peripheral genera such as Arabelia, Sestakovaia, Turkocranum, Vankeeria, Oedignatha). HONEST CEILING: the key gets you to GENUS, not species. It relies on characters that need a microscope or at least a good stereo-zoom: counting paired ventral bristles on tibia/metatarsus I-II, seeing whether sclerotized 'praecoxal triangles' are present, whether the female's median spinnerets are flattened, presence of feathery hairs on leg bases, and claw tufts/scopulae. Almost none of this is reliable from a naked-eye field view or an ordinary phone photo. To go from genus to species you essentially always need an adult and must examine the genitalia under a microscope: the male palp (embolus, tegular/median apophysis, tibial apophysis) or the female epigyne, compared against species descriptions. Mesiotelus in particular was substantially revised in 2024 (Zootaxa 5519) with many new West Palaearctic species, so species-level names should be checked against current literature. For a field/photo tool, stop at family or at best a tentative genus (e.g. Agroeca if you find the lantern egg sac) and treat species as 'needs a specialist with a scope'.

couplets.character: Posterior eye row curvature; feathery hairs on leg bases; body colour; male belly bristles

options.text: Posterior eye row strongly recurved; feathery hairs absent; body unicoloured brown; male opisthosoma (abdomen) with short ventral bristles
Posterior eye row only slightly procurved to recurved; feathery hairs usually present on basal leg articles

couplets.character: Body length and spacing of the posterior eyes

options.text: Body length 2.0–3.5 mm; distance between the two posterior median eyes wider than the distance to the lateral eyes
Body length 3.5–12 mm; eyes of the posterior row about equidistant

couplets.character: Claw tufts and scopulae on tarsi/metatarsi

options.text: Tarsi with dense spatulate claw tufts; dense scopulae (brushes) on tarsi and metatarsi
Claw tufts absent or indistinct; no tarsal/metatarsal scopulae

couplets.character: Sclerotized praecoxal triangles; shape of female median spinnerets

options.text: Sclerotized praecoxal triangles present; median spinnerets of female compressed/flattened
Praecoxal triangles absent; female median spinnerets not compressed

couplets.character: Number of ventral bristle pairs on tibia I; degree of spinneret flattening

options.text: Tibia I usually with 4–11 ventral bristle pairs; female median spinnerets strongly flattened
Tibia I usually with 2 ventral bristle pairs; median spinnerets not or only slightly flattened

couplets.character: Ventral bristle pairs on metatarsi I-II; male belly bristles; tegulum folds

options.text: Metatarsi I-II usually with 3 ventral bristle pairs; male opisthosoma may bear ventral bristles; tegulum with longitudinal folds
Metatarsi I-II usually with 2 ventral bristle pairs; male opisthosoma lacks spines/bristles; tegulum without folds

couplets.character: Genital structures (male median apophysis; female epigyne)

options.text: Median apophysis very long; bulbus with an additional long tegular apophysis; epigyne with a medium septum
Median apophysis stout; no additional apophysis; epigyne with a shallow furrow and a helm-shaped anterior structure

field_questions.ask: Is there NO capture web - just a small fast spider running on the ground, under a stone, in litter or on a wall?
field_questions.yes_means: Consistent with Liocranidae (free-living hunters that do not spin a snare web). Rules out orb-weavers, cobweb and funnel-web spiders.

field_questions.ask: Is the body small (roughly 2-9 mm, mostly under about 8 mm) and plain earth-coloured - yellow-brown, red-brown or greyish - with maybe a darker pattern or a row of chevron 'angle' marks on the abdomen, rather than brightly coloured or boldly striped?
field_questions.yes_means: Fits Liocranidae's drab, modestly patterned look; argues against colourful jumping spiders or boldly marked wolf spiders.

field_questions.ask: Are the eight eyes all small and roughly the same size, set in a fairly even cluster at the front (front row more or less straight), with no single very large pair?
field_questions.yes_means: Matches the liocranid eye layout and rules out jumping spiders (two huge front eyes) and wolf/nursery-web spiders (large rear eyes in a distinctive 3-row trapezoid).

field_questions.ask: Did you find a small whitish egg sac shaped like an upside-down lantern on a short stalk, coated in soil so it looks dirty, stuck under a stone or on a plant stem?
field_questions.yes_means: Strong sign of the genus Agroeca within Liocranidae - this 'fairy lamp' egg sac is essentially diagnostic for that genus in the field.

records.found_check: No species in this family were photographed or supplied by the user yet, so there is nothing to cross-check. The family characters above are written generically for the whole family and were validated against the Spiders of Europe accounts for representative Portuguese genera (Liocranum rupicola: 4.9-8.5 mm, brownish carapace with dark radial pattern and black margin, grey patterned abdrenomen, free-living under stones/bark and in buildings; Agroeca brunnea: 5.1-9.4 mm, light red-brown with golden hairs and dark abdominal chevrons, ground hunter with the lantern egg sac), which all fit the description.

lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Clubionids look very similar (small, plain, fast ground/foliage hunters) but their leg tips bear conspicuous dense claw tufts and the legs are usually almost spineless, whereas liocranids lack obvious claw tufts and show paired ventral spines/bristles on the front tibiae and metatarsae. Often needs a hand lens or scope.

Closely related and historically lumped together; corinnids tend to have the anterior eye row procurved (curved forward) and some are shiny/ant-mimicking, while Liocranidae have a more or less straight anterior eye row. A genuinely tricky pair - confirmation is a specialist job.

Gnaphosids have cylindrical, clearly separated front spinnerets that stick out at the rear tip (visible with a lens) and flattened, often parallel-sided posterior median eyes; liocranids have ordinary, more conical/closer-set spinnerets and a normal eye cluster.

Wolf spiders have a distinctive eye pattern - four small eyes in a bottom row and two very large eyes above plus two on top in a trapezoid - and are usually larger and hairier; liocranid eyes are all small and even, and the body is smaller and flatter.

characters.web: Free-living hunter, no snare web. Builds a silk retreat (a curled/spun leaf tied shut with silk threads) used as a daytime hideout, then leaves it at night to hunt on foot.
characters.eyes: Eight eyes in two rows (typical for the group); no single eye character is enough to separate this family from several lookalikes by naked eye - the family is really told apart by where the breathing-pore (tracheal spiracle) sits on the underside of the abdomen, which needs a hand lens or macro photo of the belly.
characters.body_shape: Small to medium, fairly slender, flattish spider with a somewhat elongated abdomen - not chunky, not flat-and-wide like a crab spider.
characters.legs: Eight legs of normal (not sideways-splayed) posture, each leg ending in two claws with tufts of fine hairs underneath that help it grip and climb on leaves and bark; legs often faintly mottled/marbled rather than plain.
characters.colour: Pale, washed-out colouring overall - yellowish to light brown body, abdomen often pale yellow to pinkish-brown with small dark spots or a couple of dark angled marks down the middle, legs yellow-brown sometimes with faint dark marbling. This pale 'washed out' look is why the family is nicknamed 'ghost spiders'.
characters.behaviour_habitat: Lives on tree trunks, in the crowns/foliage of deciduous trees and shrubs, and sometimes on conifers; hides by day in a retreat of spun-together leaves and comes out at night to actively hunt insects on foliage and bark rather than sitting in a web.

field_questions.ask: Is the spider found at night, actively walking on a tree trunk, leaf, or shrub, with no web visible around it?
field_questions.yes_means: Consistent with Anyphaenidae - they are free-running night hunters, not web-builders.

field_questions.ask: During the day, did you find it tucked inside a curled leaf or a small pouch of leaves held together with a few silk strands?
field_questions.yes_means: Consistent with Anyphaenidae - this leaf retreat is a classic daytime hiding habit for this family.

field_questions.ask: Is the body pale and washed-out (light yellow/tan/pinkish) rather than dark, glossy, or strongly patterned, with legs that look faintly marbled rather than boldly banded?
field_questions.yes_means: Consistent with the typical 'ghost spider' pale colouring, though this alone is not conclusive.

field_questions.ask: Are the front two pairs of legs held forward/normal (not splayed out sideways like a crab)?
field_questions.yes_means: Rules out crab spiders (Philodromidae/Thomisidae), which hold legs I-II sideways.

lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Very similar pale, slender body plan and similar leaf/bark-dwelling habits. The only reliable difference is internal: the breathing-pore (tracheal spiracle) sits about midway between the abdomen's underside fold and the spinnerets in Anyphaenidae, but right next to the spinnerets in Clubionidae - this needs a macro photo of the spider's underside or a microscope, not something visible casually in the field.

Similar build and colouring; separated mainly by the front eye row being straight in Liocranidae versus the spiracle-position character above for Anyphaenidae - again a magnification-level character, not a naked-eye one.
Philodromidae hold their front two leg pairs out sideways (crab-like, laterigrade stance) and are flatter; Anyphaenidae legs point forward/normal and the body is less flattened.

Corinnidae tend to have a more strongly procurved front eye row and often a more contrastingly patterned, harder-looking body; Anyphaenidae looks softer and paler overall, but definitive separation again relies on the spiracle position.

source.title: araneae - Anyphaenidae (genus-level key page)
source.authors: Nentwig, W., Blick, T., Bosmans, R., Gloor, D., Hänggi, A. & Kropf, C.

deeper_key.notes: There is no real dichotomous genus key to transcribe for this family on araneae.nmbe.ch: the page explicitly states that in Central Europe (and effectively in the whole Western Mediterranean/Portugal area) there is only one genus, Anyphaena, so the 'key' is a single statement rather than branching couplets. At the species level, araneae.nmbe.ch's species key for Anyphaena (https://araneae.nmbe.ch/specieskey/29/Anyphaena) only separates two species (A. accentuata vs A. furva) by male pedipalp spine arrangement, and does not cover the other Portuguese species (alboirrorata, numida, sabina) in a couplet form. A separate taxonomic paper (Vieira & Lo-Man-Hung style regional revision; see sources_consulted) confirms that within Portugal the four Anyphaena species (accentuata, alboirrorata, numida, sabina) are reliably told apart ONLY by adult genitalia under magnification: males by the shape of the tibial retrolateral apophysis on the pedipalp, and females by the shape and width of the epigyne's anterior hood (absent in alboirrorata, narrow in numida, very wide in sabina). There is no naked-eye or photo-only character that separates the four Portuguese species from each other. A field photo can confidently place a spider in the genus Anyphaena (pale colouring, leaf retreat habit, no web) but species-level ID realistically requires a specimen examined under a microscope, ideally an adult male or female with genitalia visible.

records.common_group: water spiders and dictynid-like ground spiders (genus Argenna)

characters.web: Two very different lifestyles in this family. Argyroneta aquatica builds a unique underwater "diving bell": a dome of silk filled with air, anchored to submerged pondweed, used as a permanent retreat for resting, eating, mating and egg-laying - no spider in any other family does this. Argenna subnigra is a small ground-dwelling species that spins an irregular silk retreat/sheet under stones or in leaf litter, not a snare web for catching prey in the air.
characters.eyes: Eight eyes in two rows, the standard araneomorph ("true spider") arrangement - not a useful field character to separate this family from most others.
characters.body_shape: Argyroneta aquatica: large, robust, funnel-web-spider-like body shape with a noticeably hairy, velvety abdomen. Argenna subnigra: small, compact ground spider, unremarkable shape, similar to a tiny Dictynidae.
characters.legs: Argyroneta aquatica: legs (especially the 4th pair) carry unusually long, dense hairs that sometimes form visible tufts - an adaptation for swimming and holding the air bell; males have longer front legs than females. Argenna subnigra: legs pale brown with distinct dark bands (annulations), nothing unusual in shape.
characters.colour: Argyroneta aquatica: dark brown carapace and mouthparts, dark grey-brown to black abdomen; underwater the body looks bright silver because of a film of trapped air clinging to its hairs. Argenna subnigra: brown carapace with a black eye region, central pit (fovea) and dark margins; abdomen dark brown to almost black; legs light brown with dark rings.
characters.behaviour_habitat: Argyroneta aquatica lives almost its entire life submerged in clean, plant-choked still or slow water (ponds, lakes, canals, marshes) and only rarely surfaces - if you see a spider swimming underwater belly-up inside a silvery silk bubble, this is almost certainly it. Argenna subnigra is terrestrial, found in sunny spots on the ground, under stones, in lichens and leaf litter - the complete opposite habitat.

field_questions.ask: Did you find this spider actually living underwater, inside or near a silvery silk bubble attached to pondweed?
field_questions.yes_means: Almost certainly Argyroneta aquatica - no other European spider does this.

field_questions.ask: Is the spider tiny (a few mm), brown with dark-ringed legs, found on dry ground under a stone or in leaf litter in a sunny spot - definitely not near water?
field_questions.yes_means: Consistent with Argenna subnigra, but only a specialist can confirm against similar small ground spiders (e.g. Dictynidae) under a microscope.

field_questions.ask: Does the abdomen look hairy and turn bright silver/mirror-like when the spider is underwater?
field_questions.yes_means: Strong support for Argyroneta aquatica - the silvering is trapped air on specialised hairs.

field_questions.ask: Is the body length under 5 mm?
field_questions.yes_means: Rules out Argyroneta aquatica (8-15 mm) and points toward Argenna subnigra or an unrelated tiny family.

lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Argyroneta aquatica looks like a large funnel-weaver but is found permanently submerged in water with a visible silk diving bell; Agelenidae/Cybaeidae build dry funnel webs on land or at water margins and do not live underwater.

Argenna subnigra is virtually indistinguishable from small Dictynidae by eye - both are tiny brown ground/low-vegetation spiders with a cribellum; in fact Argenna sat inside Dictynidae until 2025. Separation requires microscopic examination of the cribellum/calamistrum and genitalia, not a field character.
Both can be seen on or near water, but Dolomedes is much larger, hunts on the water surface and does not build an underwater silk bell - it never goes fully submerged the way Argyroneta does.

source.title: World Spider Catalog - Family Argyronetidae
source.authors: Natural History Museum Bern (WSC team); family redefined by Montana, S. et al. 2025

deeper_key.notes: No published dichotomous key exists below family level for this family in Portugal/Iberia. araneae.nmbe.ch's online family key (https://araneae.nmbe.ch/key) does not even list Argyronetidae, because the site predates the 2025 reclassification (Montana et al., Zoological Journal of the Linnean Society, 2025) that pulled Argyroneta, Argenna and ten other genera out of Dictynidae into a redefined Argyronetidae. That paper is a phylogenetic reappraisal, not an identification key, and gives no genus- or species-level couplets. In practice the two Portuguese species are easy to separate from each other just by size, colour and habitat (see characters/field_questions above), but going further - confirming Argenna subnigra against similar small Dictynoidea, or confirming any Argyroneta aquatica record - requires expert examination of genitalia (epigyne/vulva in females, palp structure in males) under a microscope, or at minimum a clear macro photo reviewed by a specialist (e.g. via iNaturalist community ID). There is no shortcut to species level from naked-eye or phone-photo characters for Argenna; Argyroneta aquatica is the one exception, being unmistakable by its underwater diving-bell lifestyle alone.

characters.web: No snare web visible above ground. Builds a closed silk tube ("purse-web"): part of the tube runs underground as a burrow, and a finger-like extension lies along the soil surface or up the base of a rock/tree trunk, camouflaged with soil, moss and debris so it looks like a root or twig, not a web at all. The spider never leaves the tube to hunt; it waits inside and bites prey through the silk wall from the inside.
characters.eyes: Eight eyes grouped tightly together on a small raised mound at the front of the head (typical of mygalomorphs) - not a feature usefully checked without magnification in the field; not a practical naked-eye distinguishing mark.
characters.body_shape: Stocky, robust, almost tank-like body with a notably large, rounded, shiny carapace (head-body region) relative to a plumper abdomen. Fangs point straight down and strike downward/parallel to each other (orthognathous), unlike the crossing, pincer-like fangs of almost all other spiders - visible if you look at the front of the spider head-on.
characters.legs: Legs relatively short and thick compared to body size, not long and spindly. No obvious spines or tufts that stand out to the naked eye. Adult males have a noticeably bright/pale-coloured tip on the front leg tarsus, contrasting with the otherwise dark leg.
characters.colour: Overall black to dark brown; females often have a paler yellow-brown to black carapace with a glossy abdomen that can show a faint bluish or purplish sheen. Males are mostly black overall with that pale leg-tip marking.
characters.behaviour_habitat: Sedentary, almost never seen in the open. Lives in a self-built silk tube in dry, sun-exposed habitats: open heathland, dry grassland, open pine woodland, sunny meadows, and the base of rocky slopes/outcrops, from sea level up to about 500 m. Forms loose colonies of many burrows close together. Adults (especially males in autumn) are the only life stage likely to be seen wandering, when males leave their tube to search for a mate; otherwise found only by spotting and carefully excavating the camouflaged surface tube.

field_questions.ask: Did you find the spider out in the open, walking on soil or low vegetation (not in a typical web)?
field_questions.yes_means: Consistent with Atypidae - these spiders are almost only seen wandering (usually males in autumn) rather than sitting in a visible web.

field_questions.ask: Is there a finger-like or root-like silk tube lying along the ground surface or up the base of a rock, covered in soil and moss so it barely looks like silk?
field_questions.yes_means: Strongly supports Atypidae - this is the diagnostic purse-web, very different from any orb, sheet, or funnel web.

field_questions.ask: Looking at the front of the spider, do the fangs point straight down and stay parallel, rather than crossing like pincers?
field_questions.yes_means: Confirms a mygalomorph (orthognathous) layout, which in Portugal at this body size and habitat points to Atypidae rather than any 'true' (labidognath) spider family.

field_questions.ask: Is the body stocky and the colour plain black or dark brown, with short thick legs (no bright patterns)?
field_questions.yes_means: Matches Atypidae's plain, robust look as opposed to patterned web-builders.

lookalikes.family: Nemesiidae / Ctenizidae (trapdoor spiders, not currently confirmed established in mainland Portugal's standard checklist but mygalomorphs in general)
lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Trapdoor-spider relatives build a hinged soil 'lid' over a vertical burrow entrance with no above-ground silk extension, whereas Atypus builds a closed sock-like tube that runs along the surface - look for the camouflaged finger-like tube rather than a round hinged door.

Segestriidae also build a silk tube with trip-lines radiating from the entrance, but they have normal crossing (labidognath) fangs and are slimmer-bodied; Atypus has thicker, parallel downward-pointing fangs and no radiating trip threads around the tube mouth.

Eresidae build a silk web with a tubular retreat plus an irregular capture web with debris around the entrance, often on shrubs; their fangs cross normally and many species (e.g. Eresus) are brightly coloured/patterned, unlike the plain dark, fully closed tube of Atypus.

couplets.character: Posterior spinnerets and sternum colour

options.text: Posterior spinnerets with 3 distinct segments (visible in both dorsal and ventral view); sternum with a bright (pale) median area
Posterior spinnerets with 4 segments, or appearing 4-segmented through pigmentation, and/or sternum with dark median area

couplets.character: Spinneret segmentation and male metatarsus IV spination

options.text: Posterior spinnerets with 4 distinct segments in both dorsal and ventral view; sternum with dark median part; male metatarsus IV with about 4 spines
Posterior spinnerets appear 4-segmented only due to a pigmentation band but are actually 3-segmented ventrally; male metatarsus IV with 2 or more spines

source.title: araneae.nmbe.ch - Key to species of genus Atypus (Atypidae)
source.authors: Nentwig W, Blick T, Bosmans R, Gloor D, Hanggi A, Kropf C, Staubli A (Araneae - Spiders of Europe)

deeper_key.notes: The family-level genus key (https://araneae.nmbe.ch/genkey/13/Atypidae) has no couplets because only one genus, Atypus, occurs in Europe - it states this directly rather than branching. Below genus, the araneae.nmbe.ch species key for Atypus reliably separates the 3 European species (A. affinis, A. muralis, A. piceus) using posterior-spinneret segmentation, sternum colour, and male metatarsus IV spine count - characters that mostly require a hand lens or microscope on a preserved/photographed specimen, not naked-eye field ID. Since only A. affinis is confirmed for Portugal, in practice any Portuguese Atypus encountered is presumptively that species, but a fully rigorous species-level confirmation still requires checking the spinneret/sternum/leg-spine characters above; truly certain sexing and some closely related species elsewhere in the genus worldwide may need genitalia examination under a scope. This key is fully transcribed above (not truncated) since it has only 2 couplets for 3 species.

records.found_check: No specific found species list was provided for this record. The family characters above (closed purse-web tube, orthognathous downward-parallel fangs, stocky dark body, sedentary fossorial habit in dry sunny habitats) are standard and consistent with how Atypidae/Atypus is described on araneae.nmbe.ch and corroborating sources; Atypus affinis is the only Atypidae species confirmed for mainland Portugal per the World Spider Catalog distribution data (Britain and Portugal to Ukraine, North Africa).

characters.behaviour_habitat: Lives under stones and in leaf litter in damp woodland, and also in caves, cellars and drains (a troglophile - equally at home underground or in dark damp surface spots). Builds a small, irregular sheet web rather than an obvious orb or funnel. Adult males wander in search of mates mostly October-December.
characters.body_shape: Small, compact spider, 5-7 mm body length, with a smooth shiny carapace and a softly hairy abdomen - overall a plain, unremarkable little brown spider with no bold pattern.
characters.colour: Carapace reddish-yellow to yellow-brown and glossy; sternum pale yellowish; legs yellowish, often darkening toward the leg tips; abdomen light grey to yellowish, finely covered in short hairs (no bright colours or strong markings).
characters.eyes: Eight eyes in the typical two slightly curved rows (this is the character that separates Cicurina, the Portuguese genus, from six-eyed relatives like Chorizomma within the same family) - eyes are small and not strikingly arranged.
characters.legs: Legs are moderately long and slender relative to the body, yellowish with darker tips, without spines or colour bands that stand out; nothing about the legs is dramatic - they look like generic ground-spider legs.
characters.web: No snare web in the usual sense - makes a small, loose, irregular silk sheet/retreat under stones or in crevices rather than a geometric trap; not cribellate (no woolly blue silk band).

records.common_group: cave meshweavers / mesh-web spiders (formerly placed in Hahniidae/Dictynidae)

deeper_key.notes: The araneae.nmbe.ch genus key (genkey/92) only separates the family's three European genera (Cicurina, Chorizomma, Brommella) - and since only Cicurina cicur occurs in Portugal, genus-level placement is effectively automatic for any Portuguese specimen of this family. Going from genus to confirmed species (and especially confirming it really is C. cicur rather than a similar Cicurina) requires examining the male pedipalp (retrotibial apophysis shape, coiled conductor tip) or the female epigyne/vulva under a microscope - these are NOT visible by eye or in a phone photo. In practice, for Portugal, family = genus = species (Cicurina cicur) by elimination, but a rigorous species-level confirmation still needs genitalia examination by a specialist.

source.title: araneae.nmbe.ch genus-level key to Cicurinidae
source.authors: Nentwig, W., Blick, T., Bosmans, R., Gloor, D., Hanggi, A., Kropf, C. & Stäubli, A.

couplets.character: Number of eyes

options.text: Six eyes
Eight eyes

couplets.character: Male pedipalp: retrotibial apophysis length and conductor tip shape

options.text: Retrotibial apophysis as long as the tibia, tip of conductor coiled
Retrotibial apophysis small or invisible, insemination ducts long and thin

couplets.character: Male pedipalp: retrotibial apophysis and epigynal fovea

options.text: Retrotibial apophysis present and wide, female with a distinct epigynal fovea
options.leads_to: Cicurina (includes Cicurina cicur, the only Portuguese species)

options.text: Retrotibial apophysis long, almost as long as the cymbium

field_questions.ask: Did you find it under a stone, in leaf litter, or in a cave/cellar/drain rather than out in the open or on a web in vegetation?
field_questions.yes_means: Consistent with Cicurinidae habitat preference (damp, dark, ground-level or underground spots).

field_questions.ask: Is the spider small (roughly 5-7 mm body, not counting legs) and plain reddish-yellow to grey-brown with no bold pattern or bright colours?
field_questions.yes_means: Matches the plain, small-bodied look of Cicurina cicur.

field_questions.ask: Is the web (if you can see one) just a loose, messy little silk sheet or patch rather than a neat funnel, orb, or sheet with a tube retreat?
field_questions.yes_means: Consistent with Cicurinidae's simple irregular sheet web, as opposed to Agelenidae (funnel) or larger sheet-web families.

field_questions.ask: Does it have eight eyes in two short, slightly curved rows, with no eyes obviously enlarged?
field_questions.yes_means: Matches the genus Cicurina (the only Cicurinidae genus confirmed in Portugal); six eyes would point to Chorizomma instead, which is not recorded in Portugal.

lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Hahniidae (which Cicurinidae was split out of) have their spinnerets arranged in a single transverse row at the rear of the abdomen, visible from above; Cicurinidae spinnerets are not aligned in that single straight row - this is a microscope-level character, so in the field the families look almost identical (small, plain, ground-dwelling sheet-web spiders).

Dictynidae are cribellate - they have a distinctive band of fine blue-grey woolly silk (the cribellum/calamistrum combination) on the rear legs and a patch of fuzzy silk in the web; Cicurinidae lack this woolly cribellate silk, so a close look at the web texture (smooth vs fuzzy/woolly) is the practical clue.

Agelenidae (house/funnel-web spiders) build a clear funnel-shaped retreat leading off a sheet web and have noticeably long posterior spinnerets (about twice the length of the anterior pair); Cicurinidae make only a small irregular sheet with no funnel and have posterior spinnerets only slightly longer than the anterior ones.

characters.web: free-living hunter, no snare web; builds a simple silken retreat/sac under stones or bark by day rather than a capture web
characters.eyes: eight eyes in two rows, fairly small; not a distinguishing field character - body shape and habitat are more useful
characters.body_shape: small, slender, somewhat flattened spider; legs noticeably long and thin relative to body, giving a fast, running spider look similar to a ground spider (Gnaphosidae)
characters.legs: long, thin legs; tarsi are pseudo-segmented (look like they have extra false joints, especially noticeable bent in dried/preserved specimens) - a key microscopic character but hard to see in the field; legs often pale and semi-translucent
characters.colour: pale yellowish to light orange-brown overall, legs often paler and almost translucent; no bold pattern
characters.behaviour_habitat: fast-moving nocturnal hunter; hides by day under stones, bark, or in cracks/crevices in hot, dry, stony habitats; also known as a synanthropic species that turns up in and around buildings and has been spread worldwide by human transport (found in warehouses, ports, homes)

field_questions.ask: Is the spider small (under 6 mm body length), pale yellowish/orange with long, thin, almost see-through legs?
field_questions.yes_means: consistent with Cithaeron - but many small pale ground-dwelling spiders look similar, so this alone is not conclusive

field_questions.ask: Was it found hiding under a stone, piece of bark, or in a crevice in a hot, dry, stony spot (or alternatively indoors/in a building)?
field_questions.yes_means: matches the known habitat and synanthropic habits of Cithaeron praedonius

field_questions.ask: Does it lack any kind of web for catching prey, just a thin silk retreat/sac where it hides?
field_questions.yes_means: consistent with this family's free-living hunting lifestyle

field_questions.ask: Were you able to see, with magnification, that the leg tarsi look like they have odd extra false segments or bends?
field_questions.yes_means: this is the actual diagnostic character used by specialists (pseudo-segmented tarsi) - a strong supporting clue, though confirmation still needs expert/microscope work

lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Gnaphosidae are usually similarly fast pale-to-dark ground hunters but have cylindrical (not conical) anterior spinnerets without a sclerotised ring, and normal (not pseudo-segmented) tarsi; Cithaeronidae was historically grouped near Gnaphosidae and the two are very easily confused without examining spinnerets and tarsi closely
these are generally larger and flatter with a more sprawled leg posture; Cithaeron is much smaller (under 6 mm) and slimmer-bodied
similar pale, slender, fast ground/crevice hunters, but lack the conical spinnerets with sclerotised ring and pseudo-segmented tarsi that define Cithaeronidae

source.title: araneae.nmbe.ch genus-level key entry for Cithaeronidae
source.authors: Nentwig, Blick, Bosmans, Kropf & Stäubli (Spiders of Europe)

deeper_key.notes: There is no real dichotomous couplet to transcribe: the araneae.nmbe.ch genus-key page for this family states plainly that there is only one genus in Europe, Cithaeron, so the key terminates immediately at the family level with no further branching needed to reach genus. The separate family-level key (https://araneae.nmbe.ch/key, step 59) is the only real couplet involved, distinguishing Cithaeronidae from neighbouring families (notably Gnaphosidae) by conical anterior spinnerets with a sclerotised ring and pseudo-segmented tarsi. Species-level identification within Cithaeron (the global genus has 8 species per the World Spider Catalog) requires examining male palp/bulbus shape (retrolateral tibial apophysis, median apophysis) or female epigyne/vulva structure under a microscope - this cannot be done from a field photo. The species most plausibly recorded in/near Portugal, Cithaeron praedonius, is a known synanthropic species spread worldwide by human transport (confirmed from North Africa, Greece, Cyprus, Turkey, the Middle East to India and Malaysia, with introductions across the Americas and Australia); araneae.nmbe.ch and the World Spider Catalog text do not explicitly list Portugal in its distribution, though it does appear in at least one Portugal species-list aggregator. Treat the Portuguese record as plausible but not strongly confirmed by the primary European source - flag this honestly rather than asserting certainty.

characters.web: free-living hunter, no snare web — they do not build a web to catch prey; instead they actively hunt at night and build only a silk retreat sac to rest or molt in during the day
characters.eyes: eight eyes in two rows; the back row of eyes is noticeably longer (wider) than the front row — this 'eyes spread wide at the back' look is one of the better field clues for Clubiona
characters.body_shape: elongated, somewhat flattened, sac-like body; legs comparatively short and stout for the body size, with the second pair of legs (leg II) often slightly longer than the first pair (leg I); top of the front body section (prosoma) has a visible median groove/furrow running lengthwise
characters.legs: short and stout compared to many other hunting spiders; no strong spines or obvious bristle combs; leg II typically a bit longer than leg I
characters.colour: generally plain and understated — straw-yellow, sandy-brown, olive, or greyish-brown overall, sometimes with a darker brown patch on the front body section; opisthosoma (abdomen) often pale with a faint darker central band or no strong pattern at all; no bright colours, no bold geometric markings
characters.behaviour_habitat: nocturnal ground- and foliage-dwelling hunters found in low vegetation, leaf litter, bark crevices, under stones, in grass tussocks and shrubs; by day they hide inside a distinctive silken sac/tube retreat, often a curled or folded leaf bound with silk, or a tubular sac under bark or stones; at night they leave the retreat to hunt prey on foot rather than trapping it in a web

field_questions.ask: Did you find it hiding by day inside a tightly rolled or folded leaf bound shut with silk, or inside a silk tube/sac under bark or a stone, rather than sitting in an open web?
field_questions.yes_means: Consistent with Clubionidae (and several other ground/foliage hunters) — supports this family over web-building families

field_questions.ask: Is the spider a plain, unpatterned straw-yellow, sandy-brown, or greyish colour with no bold stripes, spots, or bright colours?
field_questions.yes_means: Consistent with Clubionidae's typically plain, understated colouring

field_questions.ask: Looking at the body from above, does it look elongated and somewhat flattened with short, stout legs rather than long spindly legs?
field_questions.yes_means: Matches the Clubionidae body plan rather than families with long thin legs (e.g. some sac-spider lookalikes)

field_questions.ask: Was it active at night, found walking/hunting on foliage, bark, or ground litter rather than sitting motionless in a web waiting for prey?
field_questions.yes_means: Matches the free-living nocturnal hunting behaviour typical of this family

lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Liocranid sac spiders look extremely similar in body shape, plain colouring, and leaf-retreat behaviour; they were in fact split out of Clubionidae taxonomically. In practice the two cannot be reliably told apart by naked-eye field characters — separation usually requires examining eye-row proportions and genital structures under magnification.

Gnaphosids (ground spiders) also hunt at night without webs and are often plainly coloured, but they have two cylindrical, clearly separated spinnerets visible at the rear tip of the abdomen, and a flatter, more compact body; Clubionidae's spinnerets are less prominently separated and the body is more elongated/sac-like.

Anyphaenids (ghost spiders) look similar and were once grouped with Clubionidae, but they tend to be found higher in foliage/trees, often have a small sclerotized plate near the spinnerets (visible under magnification), and are generally more slender; field separation by eye is unreliable.

source.title: araneae - Clubionidae (genus-level key)
source.authors: Nentwig, W., Blick, T., Bosmans, R., Gloor, D., Hänggi, A., Kropf, C.

deeper_key.notes: Honest ceiling: this online key only goes to GENUS, not species, and even at genus level it is very shallow. It presents a single couplet (one set of characters: short stout legs, leg II longer than leg I, posterior eye row clearly longer than anterior, median furrow on prosoma) that identifies Clubiona, then simply lists Elaver and Porrhoclubiona as 'further genera' without contrasting diagnostic characters to separate them from Clubiona or from each other. Of the genera mentioned, Elaver is a New World/Philippines genus and does NOT occur in Portugal or Europe at all (confirmed via Wikipedia/WSC-sourced search) — for Portuguese purposes effectively only Clubiona and Porrhoclubiona are relevant, and Porrhoclubiona is itself debated by some authors as possibly a subgenus of Clubiona rather than a fully distinct genus. Beyond genus, species-level identification within Clubiona is genuinely difficult: species are separated mainly by the fine structure of the male pedipalp (embolus shape, tegular apophyses) and the female epigyne/internal seminal receptacles, visible only under a microscope after dissection or careful clearing — not from a live spider or a phone photo. For the 15 Portuguese Clubionidae species, expect family-to-genus confidence from field/photo characters, and treat species names as provisional pending expert genitalia examination.

couplets.character: Legs short and stout; leg II distinctly longer than leg I; posterior eye row clearly longer (wider) than anterior eye row; prosoma with a median furrow on the dorsal surface (see figured eye and spinneret diagrams on source page)

options.text: All characters present as described
Does not match (key does not provide contrasting characters on this page)
options.leads_to: Elaver or Porrhoclubiona (listed by the source only as 'further genera', without a worked dichotomous contrast — see notes)

characters.web: Most Corinnidae do not build a snare web at all — they are free-living ground/bark hunters that wander and pounce on prey rather than trapping it in silk. Both species recorded from Portugal in this family are active hunters found in leaf litter, moss, and low vegetation, not web-builders. Females spin a simple silken retreat/egg sac under bark, stones, or litter, but this is a resting sac, not a prey-catching web.
characters.eyes: Eight eyes in two rows. The front (anterior) row is straight to slightly curved, and the eyes are often clustered close together relative to the width of the head — described on araneae.nmbe.ch as an eye field that is narrow (less than about half the width of the head shield). To the naked eye this looks like a small, tight cluster of eyes at the front of the face rather than two evenly spaced wide rows.
characters.body_shape: Small spiders (in Portugal, in the roughly 2-4 mm range): a rounded, often shiny and somewhat domed carapace (head/thorax shield), smooth or slightly wrinkled cuticle, and powerful, robust chelicerae (jaws) for the spider's size. The abdomen is oval and can show a hardened shield (scutum) on top in males of some genera. Legs are moderate in length, not unusually long or spindly.
characters.legs: The single most useful hands-lens character for this family: legs lack true stout spines, but the male's first two pairs of legs (tibia and metatarsus I-II) often carry rows of short, blunt, peg-like bristles or thickened hairs ('cusps') on the underside, arranged in characteristic rows/pairs used by the online key to separate genera. To the naked eye this just looks like faint short stubble on the underside of the front legs in males; females usually lack it.
characters.colour: Variable but typically understated: shades of orange-brown, reddish-brown, or grey-brown on the body, sometimes with darker mottling, leg-ring banding, or pale/dark chevron markings on the abdomen. Not metallic or brightly patterned in the Portuguese species. Some non-Portuguese relatives in this broad family are ant-mimics with white spots on a dark body, but that pattern is not expected in our local species.
characters.behaviour_habitat: Ground- and litter-dwelling active hunters rather than web-sitters: found wandering by night or day among leaf litter, moss, low vegetation, under stones, bark, and similar ground cover in warm, often dry habitats. They do not sit in a web waiting for prey; they actively stalk and catch it. Egg sacs are tucked into a simple silk retreat in litter or under bark rather than in an open web.

records.common_group: corinnid sac spiders / ground sac spiders

source.title: Corinnidae — genus key (araneae.nmbe.ch online identification key)
source.authors: Nentwig, W., Blick, T., Bosmans, R., Gloor, D., Hänggi, A. & Kropf, C.

deeper_key.notes: This online key only reaches GENUS, not species. It explicitly relies on characters (leg cusps vs spines, pedipalpal apophyses, epigyne shape, cheliceral teeth) that in practice require a microscope and often an adult male's palp or adult female's epigyne examined under magnification — not something visible by eye or in a phone photo. Past genus, species-level identification within Corinnidae genera such as Phrurolithus or Trachelas depends on fine genital morphology (shape of the tibial apophysis in males, shape and depth of the epigynal groove in females) that is only reliably assessed by a specialist with a microscope, ideally cross-checked against original species descriptions or a regional revision (e.g. Bosmans' revision of Iberian Trachelinae). For a field photo alone, family or at best genus is the honest ceiling; do not expect species-level ID from appearance photos.
