field_questions.ask: Is the spider small (a few millimetres, smaller than a grain of rice to about the size of a watermelon seed) and found on the ground, in leaf litter, moss, or under bark/stones rather than sitting in a web?
field_questions.yes_means: Consistent with Corinnidae — they are ground/bark hunters, not web-builders.

field_questions.ask: Looking at the front of the head with a hand lens or zoomed phone photo, do the eyes look bunched tightly together in a narrow cluster rather than spread evenly across the width of the head?
field_questions.yes_means: Supports Corinnidae's narrow, close-set anterior eye row.

field_questions.ask: Is the body smooth, rounded, and fairly uniformly orange-brown, reddish-brown, or grey-brown, without bright colours, long spiny legs, or a flattened crab-like shape?
field_questions.yes_means: Consistent with the plain, robust-bodied look typical of this family (rules out flashier or flatter lookalike families).

field_questions.ask: If you can see a male's front legs closely (tibia/metatarsus I-II), is there a faint row of short stubble or peg-like bristles on the underside rather than true long spines?
field_questions.yes_means: A strong, family-diagnostic character used directly in the genus key, though hard to see without magnification.

records.found_check: No species list was provided for verification against found specimens. Note for the record-keeper: araneae.nmbe.ch's own per-species distribution data lists THREE Corinnidae species with Portugal in their range — Phrurolithus nigrinus, Phrurolithus festivus, and Trachelas minor — whereas this brief specifies 2 Portuguese species. All three fit the family-level characters described above equally well (small ground/litter hunters, narrow eye cluster, robust smooth body), so the family-level appearance description and field questions in this record hold regardless of which 2 (or 3) species are ultimately confirmed for the national list. This discrepancy should be checked against the authoritative current Portuguese checklist (e.g. Cardoso et al. or the most recent Iberian checklist update) before finalizing species-level entries.

lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Gnaphosidae have two clearly separated, cylindrical spinnerets sticking out visibly at the rear tip of the abdomen (easy to spot from above/behind), and a flatter, more matte/velvety grey-brown body; Corinnidae spinnerets are not prominently protruding and the body tends to look smoother/shinier.

Liocranidae legs carry true, well-developed spines along the tibiae and metatarsi; Corinnidae legs lack true spines (at most short blunt cusps on the male's front legs), giving Corinnidae legs a comparatively smooth, unarmed look.

Clubionidae have a similarly plain, soft-bodied look and also build silk retreats, but their eye field is wider/more evenly spaced and they lack the leg cusps; genitalia under magnification are needed for a sure separation, but overall body proportions in Clubionidae tend to look slightly more elongated and pale/uniform.

records.common_group: soft spiders (small relatives of funnel-web and dictynid spiders)

characters.web: No conspicuous snare web in the confirmed Portuguese species. The Portuguese genus Mastigusa lives under bark or inside ant nests and dead wood, spinning only a thin silk lining/retreat rather than a sheet or funnel web. (Other European Cybaeidae genera, e.g. Cybaeus and Cryphoeca, do build small irregular sheet webs in leaf litter or under stones, but those genera are not confirmed from Portugal.)
characters.eyes: Eight eyes in two rows, fairly small, not strikingly enlarged or reduced - nothing diagnostic to the naked eye; eye pattern alone will not separate this family from close relatives.
characters.body_shape: Small, soft-bodied spider with an oval, unarmoured abdomen (no hard plates, humps or spines) and an unremarkable, moderately long cephalothorax - a generic small "house-spider-like" build, much smaller and slimmer than Tegenaria/Eratigena.
characters.legs: Legs yellow-brown and plain or only faintly marked. In females the front (first) leg tibiae carry a double row of tiny spines on the underside, visible with a hand lens, and the leg segments nearest the body (femurs) can show a faint metallic blue sheen in good light - a useful but close-up clue, not something seen at a glance.
characters.colour: Light yellow-brown prosoma and sternum, yellow-brown legs, pale yellowish abdomen that is plain or only vaguely smudged with faint brownish/blackish marks - an overall washed-out, pale tan spider with no bold pattern.
characters.behaviour_habitat: Lives under bark and inside the hollows of dead or ancient trees, very often closely associated with ant colonies (myrmecophilous); active year-round rather than seasonally. Small, secretive and rarely noticed out in the open.

field_questions.ask: Is the spider small (under about 1 cm body length, not counting legs) and uniformly pale yellow-brown to tan, with no bold black-and-white or banded pattern?
field_questions.yes_means: Consistent with Cybaeidae (genus Mastigusa) rather than a larger, more strikingly patterned house- or funnel-web spider.

field_questions.ask: Was it found under loose bark, in a rotten/hollow tree trunk, or near or inside an ant nest, rather than out in open vegetation or in a house?
field_questions.yes_means: Strongly supports Cybaeidae/Mastigusa, which favours dead wood and ant colonies over webs in open habitats or buildings.

field_questions.ask: Is there any visible web at all, or just bare silk lining a crack or gallery (no sheet, funnel, or orb)?
field_questions.yes_means: Matches Mastigusa's habit of not building a conspicuous snare web - an obvious sheet or funnel web points to a different family instead.

field_questions.ask: Under a hand lens, can you see a double row of tiny spines on the underside of the front leg segment nearest the body joint, or a faint blue-green sheen on the upper leg segments?
field_questions.yes_means: A specific (if subtle) clue toward Mastigusa, though confirming the exact species still needs a microscope.

lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Dictynidae are cribellate (they have a cribellum spinning plate and calamistrum comb on the hind legs) and often build small irregular mesh webs on vegetation in the open; Cybaeidae (Mastigusa) lack the cribellum/calamistrum and hide under bark or with ants rather than weaving an open mesh. Historically Mastigusa was even classified inside Dictynidae, so the two are easy to confuse without checking for the cribellum.

Funnel-web spiders are distinctly larger (often 1-2 cm+), more robustly built, with long spinnerets and a obvious funnel-shaped sheet web in corners or crevices; Cybaeidae/Mastigusa are smaller, softer-bodied, and build no funnel web at all.

Amaurobiidae are also cribellate (fluffy blue-grey "lace" silk from a cribellum) and build a tangled lace-like capture web at the entrance of a retreat under bark or stones; Cybaeidae are ecribellate (no cribellum, no lacy blue silk) and leave no obvious capture web.

Hahniidae have their spinnerets arranged in a single transverse row at the rear of the abdomen and spin small flat sheet webs low in grass or moss; Cybaeidae spinnerets are not arranged in one simple transverse row, and the Portuguese genus does not build a sheet web at all.

source.title: araneae - Cybaeidae (genus-level key) and araneae - Mastigusa diversa / arietina / macrophthalma data sheets
source.authors: Nentwig, W., Blick, T., Bosmans, R., Gloor, D., Hänggi, A. & Kropf, C. (eds.) - araneae.nmbe.ch, Spiders of Europe

deeper_key.notes: araneae.nmbe.ch DOES have an online genus-level key for Cybaeidae, and it was fetched (couplets transcribed below, genus level only, covering all European genera including Mastigusa). However, for the actual Portuguese fauna the genus-level key is mostly moot: current evidence (World Spider Catalog; Castellucci, Luchetti & Scharff 2024, 'Enigmatic and extravagant genitalia in the spider genus Mastigusa', Invertebrate Systematics 38) indicates only ONE genus (Mastigusa) and, after recent revision, only ONE valid species - Mastigusa diversa - is confirmed from Portugal. Two species names appear in the classic 2000 Portuguese checklist (Cardoso, under family Dictynidae as then classified) - Mastigusa arietina and a doubtful 'Mastigusa macrophthalma ?' - but the 2024 taxonomic revision shows the Iberian Peninsula material attributed to both those names actually belongs to the revalidated species M. diversa; M. arietina has since been formally removed from the Portuguese list (Branco et al. 2019; Castellucci et al. 2024), and the M. macrophthalma record was already flagged as doubtful in 2000. Species-level separation within Mastigusa rests entirely on male palp structure (embolus/conductor shape, tibial apophysis) and female vulval anatomy, illustrated only in the primary literature (Castellucci et al. 2024) and requiring a microscope and a mounted/cleared specimen - this is NOT achievable from a field photo. Below this family/genus level there is no naked-eye or phone-photo path to species; treat any Portuguese Cybaeidae sighting as 'Mastigusa diversa (cf.)' in a citizen-science context and flag for expert/genitalic confirmation if precision matters.

couplets.character: Habitat and general build

options.text: Aquatic species; 10-12 mm; dark brown to black; tracheal stigma behind epigastric furrow; legs III-IV with notably long hairs
options.leads_to: Argyroneta (note: this genus is treated under Dictynidae by some current classifications, not confirmed from Portugal)

options.text: Terrestrial species with other characters

couplets.character: Size and colour pattern

options.text: Females 6-10 mm, males 4.5-7 mm; prosoma yellow-brown often with fine black pattern; opisthosoma grey with bright pattern
Different combination of characters

couplets.character: Male pedipalp patella and epigyne shape

options.text: Patella with spoon-like apophysis; epigyne with square plate; grey opisthosoma with indistinct pattern
Patella with 2 pointed apophyses; epigyne with rounded plate; grey opisthosoma with 2 bright dot rows
Other characters

couplets.character: Embolus length and epigyne ducts

options.text: Long, bent embolus reaching tibia; epigyne with long irregular entrance ducts
Other characters

couplets.character: Anterior median eyes

options.text: Strongly reduced, spider appears six-eyed
Smaller but clearly visible

couplets.character: Prosoma pattern and male pedipalp apophysis

options.text: Indistinct bands; small tapering median apophysis
options.leads_to: Cryphoeca (includes the widespread C. silvicola, explicitly recorded as absent from Portugal)

options.text: Broad central and lateral bands; reduced median apophysis

couplets.character: Conductor and tibial apophysis (this is the genus relevant to Portugal)

options.text: Conductor with a very long upper arm, longer than the cymbium; retrotibial apophysis sharply pointed - small (3-3.5 mm), pale yellow-brown spider living under bark or with ants
options.leads_to: Mastigusa - the only genus confirmed from Portugal (M. diversa); species-level separation from its close relatives M. arietina/M. macrophthalma requires examining male palp and female vulval microstructure under magnification, per Castellucci et al. 2024

sources_consulted: https://araneae.nmbe.ch/pdfs/03208_Cardoso_2000_EkologiaBratislava_19_Suppl3_p19-29_Araneae_checklist_Portugal.pdf (Cardoso 2000, preliminary Portuguese spider checklist - primary source for the historical Mastigusa arietina / M. macrophthalma '?' Portugal records under Dictynidae)
https://www.mapress.com/zt/article/view/zootaxa.4614.2.1 (Branco, Morano & Cardoso 2019, update to Iberian spider checklist)

characters.web: An extremely delicate, flat sheet web spun low down over a shallow depression in damp moss, leaf litter, or soil, with no closed retreat or tube. The silk is so fine it is usually only visible when beaded with dew or mist; otherwise the web is easy to miss entirely.
characters.eyes: Eight eyes in two horizontal rows of four (in Hahnia and Antistea), but in the genus Iberina the eyes can be strongly reduced or completely absent (cave/leaf-litter adapted species) - so 'no visible eyes' does not rule out this family.
characters.body_shape: Tiny, compact, medium-built to plump-bodied spiders with notably short legs relative to body size.
characters.legs: Short legs (this is one of the most useful field cues - the legs look disproportionately stubby compared to similarly tiny money spiders/Linyphiidae); legs and body often bear long fine hairs and, in some species, distinct spines on the femora.
characters.colour: Generally drab and inconspicuous: prosoma (head/thorax region) yellowish-brown to dark grey, sometimes with darker mottling; opisthosoma (abdomen) dark grey-brown often with faint pale chevron (zig-zag) markings down the back; some cave/litter-dwelling species (e.g. Iberina) are pale yellowish-white to whitish with little or no pigment.
characters.behaviour_habitat: Ground-dwelling, found in damp, shaded microhabitats - moss, leaf litter, pine needle litter, marshland and bog vegetation, woodland soil, and pool/stream margins. They sit beneath their flat sheet web waiting for small prey walking over the depression below. Family-defining trait (needs a hand lens or macro photo, not visible to the naked eye in the field): the spinnerets at the tip of the abdomen are arranged in a single transverse row, with the outer pair distinctly the longest - this is what separates Hahniidae from the otherwise very similar-looking money spiders.

field_questions.ask: Is the spider tiny (a few millimetres at most) with notably short, stubby-looking legs for its body size, found low down in moss, leaf litter, or damp soil rather than up in vegetation?
field_questions.yes_means: Consistent with Hahniidae - this family is ground-hugging and short-legged compared to similarly small Linyphiidae.

field_questions.ask: Can you see (with a hand lens or a sharp macro photo of the abdomen tip) the spinnerets lined up side-by-side in one straight row across the rear of the abdomen, rather than clustered/stacked?
field_questions.yes_means: This is the definitive family character for Hahniidae - a single transverse row of spinnerets with the outer pair clearly the longest.

field_questions.ask: Is there a very thin, flat sheet of silk spread directly over a small dip in the moss or litter, with no obvious funnel, tube, or retreat the spider can hide inside?
field_questions.yes_means: Matches the typical Hahniidae sheet web; many only become visible when covered in dew.

field_questions.ask: Does the spider appear to have very small or no visible eyes at all under magnification?
field_questions.yes_means: Points toward the genus Iberina within this family (some species are cave-adapted with reduced/absent eyes) rather than ruling out a spider entirely.

lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Money spiders are extremely similar in size, habitat, and general sheet-web behaviour, but their spinnerets are not arranged in one straight transverse row, and their legs are proportionally longer/more slender than the short-legged Hahniidae. The spinneret arrangement is the only fully reliable distinction and needs a hand lens or macro photo of the abdomen tip.

Some small funnel-web spiders can superficially resemble Hahniidae in damp ground habitats, but they build a funnel or tube retreat leading off the sheet, whereas Hahniidae webs have no retreat at all and their spinnerets are not in a single transverse row.

source.title: araneae.nmbe.ch online genus key for Hahniidae (key entry point: 'Spiders of Europe')
source.authors: Nentwig, W., Blick, T., Bosmans, R., Gloor, D., Hanggi, A., Kropf, C. & Staubli, A.

couplets.character: Eye and clypeus development; presence of a stridulatory organ on the opisthosoma anterior to the epigastric furrow

options.text: Anterior median eyes larger than or equal to posterior median eyes; posterior median eyes normally developed; clypeus smaller than or equal to the diameter of the anterior median eyes; opisthosoma with a stridulatory organ anterior to the epigastric furrow
Eyes or clypeus significantly reduced, or stridulatory organ absent

couplets.character: Femur spination, male pedipalp structure, epigyne fovea, and eye reduction

options.text: Femora with one spine; male pedipalp with a median apophysis; epigyne with a central fovea (sometimes inconspicuous); most species with 8 normally developed eyes
Femora without a spine; male pedipalp without a median apophysis; epigyne without a central fovea; eyes completely reduced (cave-adapted species, e.g. in the Pyrenees)

deeper_key.notes: This online key is genus-level only and very short: just two couplets covering the three European genera (Antistea, Hahnia, Iberina). It does NOT key out to species - araneae.nmbe.ch genus pages instead direct users to compare illustrated male pedipalp and female epigyne/vulva structures species-by-species, which effectively requires a microscope and genitalia preparation (or at minimum very high-magnification macro photos matched carefully against published illustrations). Genus-level field/photo ID is realistic for this family (especially separating Antistea from Hahnia/Iberina by eye size and the stridulatory organ), but reliable species-level identification within Hahnia or Iberina genuinely requires genitalia examination under magnification. That ceiling should be stated plainly to users rather than implying a photo alone will reach species.

records.found_check: No specific found-species list was provided in this task to verify against the family characters above.

characters.web: Free-living hunter, no snare web. They do not build a capture web; instead they sit motionless flush against tree bark or rock, relying on camouflage, and dash a short distance to ambush passing prey.
characters.eyes: Eight eyes in two rows on a low, barely raised head region; eyes are small relative to body and arranged in a compact group rather than spread across a raised carapace hump.
characters.body_shape: Flattened, oval, bark-coloured body that presses tightly against the substrate; legs are held out flat and somewhat radially so the whole animal looks like a lichen-covered patch on bark - this flattened, splayed silhouette is the single most recognisable family trait.
characters.legs: Long, thin legs, often faintly banded; the genus-defining feature is the pair of posterior (rear) spinnerets, which are dramatically elongated - in some genera as long as or longer than the abdomen itself, giving a 'two-tailed' look when the spider runs.
characters.colour: Mottled grey, brown and cream in an intricate pattern that mimics lichen or bark texture - excellent camouflage; undersides are usually paler.
characters.behaviour_habitat: Lives pressed flat against tree trunks, rock faces, or walls in warm, dry, sun-exposed Mediterranean habitats; sedentary by day, ambushing insects that walk past, and very hard to spot until it moves. Restricted in Iberia to southern/Mediterranean-influenced areas.

source.title: araneae.nmbe.ch genus-level key to Hersiliidae
source.authors: Nentwig, W., Blick, T., Bosmans, R., Gloor, D., Hänggi, A., Kropf, C. (Spiders of Europe project, NMBE)

deeper_key.notes: The online key only resolves to GENUS (Tama vs Hersiliola, separated by relative spinneret/eye/leg proportions); it does not provide species-level dichotomous couplets. Species-level determination within Hersiliola, or confirmation of Tama edwardsi, requires comparison against published redescriptions and illustrations - and in many spider families, examination of genitalia under magnification. Naked-eye or phone-photo identification should realistically stop at family, with genus only a tentative call if spinneret/leg proportions are clearly photographed from below. IMPORTANT CAVEAT found while sourcing: cross-checking the World Spider Catalog (wsc.nmbe.ch/family/39/Hersiliidae and species records), the Cardoso (2000) Portuguese spider checklist (which does not list any Hersiliidae at all), and the Portuguese Wikipedia Hersiliidae article, the only Hersiliidae species with a confirmed Portugal distribution record across every source I could fetch is Tama edwardsi (Lucas, 1846), listed as 'Portugal, Spain, Morocco/Algeria'. I could not find any source confirming a second or third Portuguese species (e.g. within Hersiliola, whose listed species - H. macullulata, H. simoni - are recorded for Spain/North Africa but not Portugal in the sources I reached). The brief states 3 Portuguese species; I was unable to verify that count against the literature available to me, so it should be re-confirmed against a current regional faunistic source (e.g. a recent national checklist or GBIF/iNaturalist occurrence review) before being treated as settled. The appearance/field characters above are written at family level so they remain valid regardless of the exact species tally.

couplets.character: Relative length of posterior spinnerets vs. opisthosoma (abdomen); relative size of anterior vs posterior median eyes; ratio of leg III length to leg I length; presence/absence of a flexible zone near the distal end of metatarsus III

options.text: Posterior spinnerets much LONGER than the opisthosoma; anterior and posterior median eyes about EQUAL in size; leg III less than 0.4x the length of leg I; metatarsus III has a flexible zone near its tip
Posterior spinnerets much SHORTER than the opisthosoma; anterior median eyes LARGER than posterior median eyes; leg III more than 0.5x the length of leg I; metatarsus III has NO flexible zone

field_questions.ask: Does the spider have a pair of unusually long, thin 'tail-like' appendages at the back end of the abdomen (not legs)?
field_questions.yes_means: Likely Hersiliidae - this is the elongated posterior spinneret pair, unique among Iberian spider families.

field_questions.ask: Is the spider flattened and pressed tightly against tree bark, a rock, or a wall, with legs splayed out flat rather than raised up?
field_questions.yes_means: Consistent with Hersiliidae's characteristic bark-hugging ambush posture.

field_questions.ask: Is there no web, sheet, or funnel near the spider - just the animal sitting still on a hard surface?
field_questions.yes_means: Consistent with Hersiliidae, which builds no snare web at all.

field_questions.ask: Does the body and leg pattern look like mottled lichen or bark rather than a solid colour or bold contrasting bands/spots?
field_questions.yes_means: Supports Hersiliidae's camouflage-pattern appearance.

records.found_check: No specific found species list was provided in the brief, so no per-species fit check was performed; the family characters above are general to Hersiliidae as described on araneae.nmbe.ch and the World Spider Catalog and would apply to any Hersiliidae individual photographed in Portugal (most likely Tama edwardsi based on confirmed distribution records).

lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Huntsmen are also flat, fast, bark/wall-dwelling hunters, but lack the elongated posterior spinnerets - their abdomen tip looks ordinary with no tail-like projections.
Philodromids are flattened bark-runners too, but are smaller, hold their front legs crab-like and sideways, and never show elongated spinnerets at the rear.
Selenopids are extremely flat wall-dwellers with eyes spread in a wide arc across the front of the head, and they lack the long posterior spinneret 'tails' of Hersiliidae.

characters.web: No snare web at all. This is a burrowing trapdoor spider: it digs a vertical silk-lined tunnel in the ground and seals the entrance with a hinged 'cork' or 'wafer' lid made of silk, soil and debris that blends into the surroundings. Ummidia algarve is unusual even among trapdoor spiders in sometimes having a SECOND, upside-down trapdoor partway down the burrow ('double-trapdoor' spider). You will essentially never see a web in the open — finding the animal usually means spotting the disc-shaped lid on a slope or bank.
characters.eyes: Eight eyes grouped tightly together on a small raised area at the front of the carapace, arranged in two or three closely packed rows — not spread out or easy to make out without magnification; not diagnostic for the naked eye in the field.
characters.body_shape: Large, heavy-bodied, robust mygalomorph (the 'tarantula' lineage): chunky prosoma, big strong chelicerae (fangs) held pointing down, short stocky legs relative to body size, no obvious waist constriction. Carapace mostly smooth/hairless, without bold patterns.
characters.legs: 8 legs, thick and relatively short, dark brown to almost black, often noticeably paler/yellow-brown toward the tips of the front legs in males. Females have curved, thorn-like spines on the sides of legs I and II (visible with a hand lens, not obvious to the naked eye). No leaf-shaped or feathery hairs.
characters.colour: Black with dark reddish tones on the body; chelicerae black on top, orange-brown underneath; legs dark brown, lightening toward the tips of the front pairs, with the hindmost leg pair distinctly paler than the rest. Overall a dark, unmarked, almost glossy spider.
characters.behaviour_habitat: Lives below ground its whole life in a self-dug, silk-lined burrow closed with a trapdoor lid, typically on sloped or vertical earth banks, sandy/clay soil, often in coastal scrub or dune-adjacent habitat in the Algarve; can occur in dense colonies, sometimes alongside unrelated nemesiid trapdoor spiders. Ambushes passing prey from just inside the lid. Defensive when the lid is forced open: rears up showing raised chelicerae and front legs rather than fleeing. Wandering males are seen mostly in autumn searching for burrows of females.

source.title: Key to Ummidia (illustrated species comparison, not a textual dichotomous key) and family key, Spiders of Europe
source.authors: Nentwig, W., Blick, T., Bosmans, R., Gloor, D., Hänggi, A. & Kropf, C.

deeper_key.notes: araneae.nmbe.ch has no textual dichotomous key below family level for this group. There is a page at /specieskey/431/Ummidia that shows the four western-Mediterranean Ummidia species (algarve, picea, aedificatoria, algeriana) side by side with photos of male pedipalps, eyes and female genitalia for visual comparison, but it is an illustrated comparison chart, not a couplet-based key, and reliable separation of Ummidia species in general still rests on adult male pedipalp and female spermathecae morphology examined under magnification (Decae 2010, Journal of Arachnology 38: 328-340). In Portugal this is moot in practice: only one species, Ummidia algarve Decae, 2010, is currently confirmed from the country (World Spider Catalog, GBIF, araneae.nmbe.ch), so any adult Halonoproctidae found in mainland Portugal can be called Ummidia algarve with reasonable confidence based on locality alone — but confirming sex/species formally still needs a specialist or genitalia examination. If you are outside Portugal and suspect a different Ummidia species, you need genitalia under a scope and Decae (2010) — there is no field-usable key.

field_questions.ask: Did you find a small (2-4 cm), round, hinged 'lid' or 'door' flush with the soil on a bank or slope, rather than any kind of web?
field_questions.yes_means: Consistent with a trapdoor-building mygalomorph (Halonoproctidae or the similar Nemesiidae) rather than a web-building spider.

field_questions.ask: Is the spider itself large, dark (black to dark reddish-brown), heavy-bodied, with thick short legs and prominent fangs, more 'tarantula-like' than a typical garden spider?
field_questions.yes_means: Consistent with a mygalomorph (trapdoor/tarantula relative) rather than a true spider (Araneomorphae).

field_questions.ask: Was it found in the Algarve (or generally southern/coastal mainland Portugal), in sandy or clay soil on a slope, rather than a garden flowerpot or house?
field_questions.yes_means: Geographically consistent with Ummidia algarve, the only Halonoproctidae species confirmed from Portugal.

field_questions.ask: If you could see inside the burrow, was there a second, upside-down trapdoor partway down the tunnel?
field_questions.yes_means: A distinctive trait reported for Ummidia algarve ('double-trapdoor spider'); absence does not rule it out since not every burrow has been recorded with this feature.

records.found_check: No species names were supplied for this family in the prompt, so there is nothing to cross-check against the family characters above.

lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Nemesiidae (e.g. Nemesia, Iberesia) are also burrowing trapdoor-building mygalomorphs in Portugal and look very similar to the naked eye (dark, robust, fanged, lid-covered burrow). The reliable separating character used by araneae.nmbe.ch's family key is under magnification: Nemesiidae have a dense scopula (velvety hair pad) on the front leg tarsi/metatarsi and an ocular process, which Halonoproctidae lack. In the field there is no naked-eye-safe way to separate them — treat any Portuguese trapdoor spider as 'trapdoor spider, family uncertain' unless examined closely, and note that Portugal actually has more Nemesiidae species than Halonoproctidae species.

Atypidae (e.g. Atypus in Portugal) build a sock-like silk tube that runs along the ground or up the base of a plant/tree, not a flush hinged lid — if you see a finger-like silk tube above ground rather than a disc-shaped door, it is a purseweb spider, not Halonoproctidae.

Some large burrowing wolf spiders also dig unlidded or simple burrows, but they are araneomorphs with two large forward-facing eyes obviously bigger than the rest (distinct eye pattern visible even by eye/phone macro) and they do not build a hinged trapdoor lid — Halonoproctidae have small, evenly sized eyes clustered together and always a proper hinged door.

characters.web: A small, irregular, sheet-like meshweb of fine cribellate (woolly, non-sticky) silk, spun close to the substrate -- on bark, low bushes, herbs, the ground surface, or among lichen. Not an orb, not a funnel, not an open tangle web; it looks like a small patch of fuzzy gauze tucked into a crevice or pressed against a leaf or stem.
characters.eyes: Eight small eyes in two rows on a low to moderately domed carapace with a narrow clypeus (area below the front eyes). The anterior median pair is usually noticeably narrower or smaller than the other six eyes, sometimes nearly absent, so the eye group looks slightly uneven rather than a tidy symmetrical octet.
characters.body_shape: Tiny, soft-bodied spider with an oval abdomen and proportionate, unremarkable legs -- nothing spiny, armoured, or elongated. In silhouette it looks like a generic small dictynid-type spider; no single body-shape feature jumps out.
characters.legs: Legs pale grey to light brown with distinct dark rings (annulations) -- this banded-leg look is one of the more noticeable field clues. No spines or other ornamentation.
characters.colour: Prosoma light brown with a darker margin and radiating dark lines from the centre; sternum dark brown; abdomen greenish-brown with blackish or reddish-brown longitudinal lines and chevron (zig-zag) marks down the back. Overall a mottled brown/olive camouflage pattern, not bright or shiny.
characters.behaviour_habitat: Lives low down in sunny, often dry sites: on tree bark, bushes, herbaceous vegetation, sometimes on bare ground or lichen-covered surfaces, building its small cribellate sheet web there and rarely wandering far from it. In Portugal one species (Auximus affinis) also occurs on Madeira and in the Azores.

records.common_group: meshweb dwarf spiders (recently split out of the old "tailor's drawer" family Dictynidae)

source.title: araneae - Key to families (Spiders of Europe)
source.authors: Nentwig, W., Blick, T., Bosmans, R., Gloor, D., Hänggi, A., Kropf, C.

deeper_key.notes: araneae.nmbe.ch only provides an identification key down to family level (plus a standalone key for Linyphiidae); there is no published genus- or species-level key for Lathyidae as a whole, and I could not find one in World Spider Catalog literature links or general searches either. Lathyidae itself is brand-new (erected in 2025 by Cala-Riquelme, Montana, Crews & Esposito out of the old Dictynidae sensu lato), so the taxonomic dust has barely settled -- several Iberian species were only recombined into their current genera in 2025-2026. Species-level identification within this family realistically requires examining the epigyne (females) or palp/conductor shape (males) under a microscope against the original species descriptions (e.g. Wunderlich 2022 for Lathys mantarota, or Nentwig et al. on araneae.nmbe.ch for the widespread species) -- not something doable from a photo. For a field photo, your honest ceiling is family or genus (if a clear photo of the eye region/leg banding rules out lookalike families), not species. Use iNaturalist community ID or contact a specialist (e.g. via the Portuguese arachnology community) for a species-level call.

field_questions.ask: Is the spider very small (body under 3 mm) with legs that show clear dark bands/rings against a paler background?
field_questions.yes_means: Consistent with Lathyidae (and several similar small cribellate families) -- banded legs are a useful first clue.

field_questions.ask: Did you find it on tree bark, on a bush or low herb, or right on the ground/among lichen, sitting in or near a small fuzzy (not glossy/sticky-looking) sheet of silk pressed against the surface?
field_questions.yes_means: Matches the typical Lathyidae web and habitat; rules out orb-weavers, funnel-web spiders, and spiders that build no web at all.

field_questions.ask: Does the abdomen show a pattern of dark zig-zag (chevron) marks or longitudinal stripes on a greenish-brown background, rather than a single bold colour or bright pattern?
field_questions.yes_means: Matches the typical Lathyidae/Lathys-type camouflage pattern.

field_questions.ask: Looking closely at the front eye row (with a macro photo or loupe), does the middle pair of front eyes look distinctly smaller/narrower than the other eyes, rather than all eight eyes looking roughly equal?
field_questions.yes_means: Supports Lathyidae over look-alike cribellate families where all eyes are more even in size.

records.found_check: No species in this family have been personally photographed by the user yet, so there is nothing to check against found specimens.

lookalikes.family: Dictynidae (the family Lathyidae was carved out of, in 2025)
lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Essentially identical at a glance to the naked eye -- both are tiny cribellate meshweb spiders with banded legs and brownish chevron-marked abdomens. They were only split apart by a 2025 molecular study; reliably telling them apart from a photo alone is not realistic, you need genitalia/microscope characters or a specialist's call.

Hahniidae have their spinnerets arranged in a single transverse row near the tip of the abdomen (visible from below/behind with a strong macro shot) and tend to build a flat sheet on the ground rather than on bark/bushes; Lathyidae spinnerets are arranged normally (not in a single straight row).

Amaurobiids are generally larger (often 5-15 mm) and build a denser, more conspicuous bluish-white cribellate lace web in crevices, under bark, or in wall cracks; Lathyidae are much smaller (under 3 mm) with a sparser, less obvious web.

Many tiny linyphiids look superficially similar in size and colour, but they spin a non-cribellate (smooth, sticky-free but silk-line, not woolly) sheet web, usually slung horizontally between vegetation rather than pressed flat against bark or the ground; under magnification their silk lacks the fuzzy cribellate texture.

characters.web: Small, minimalistic, irregular sheet web; the spider hangs beneath a loose sheet of silk, sometimes using a few scattered threads for shelter rather than a recognisable snare
characters.eyes: Six eyes when present (the family lacks the usual 8), arranged with the back pair set further back from the rest; some cave-dwelling individuals/populations are reduced-eyed or effectively eyeless
characters.body_shape: Tiny, soft-bodied, elongated spider with a noticeably small, weakly sclerotised carapace and long thin legs relative to body size
characters.legs: Long, thin, fragile-looking legs; unusually, when a leg is lost it tends to break at the patella-tibia joint rather than at the base (coxa-trochanter) as in most spiders
characters.colour: Pale, often translucent or whitish-yellow, sometimes with a faint greenish or bluish sheen from microscopic cuticle texture rather than true pigment
characters.behaviour_habitat: Lives in permanently dark, humid microhabitats: cave interiors and twilight zones, deep leaf litter, piles of rocks/scree, mine tunnels and similar voids; in Portugal the known species are cave/subterranean-associated and rarely seen in the open; spiders spend most of their life on or under their web and are reclusive

field_questions.ask: Is the spider tiny (a few millimetres, legs included roughly pea-sized or smaller) and pale/whitish with very long thin legs?
field_questions.yes_means: Consistent with Leptonetidae rather than larger, darker cave spiders

field_questions.ask: Was it found in a cave, mine, deep crevice, or under rocks/in leaf litter in a permanently damp, dark spot?
field_questions.yes_means: Matches the strong cave/subterranean habitat association of this family

field_questions.ask: Can you see only a sparse, irregular sheet of silk (not a neat orb or a dense funnel) with the spider resting underneath it?
field_questions.yes_means: Matches the minimal sheet-web behaviour typical of leptonetids

field_questions.ask: Does it look like it may have very small eyes or none that you can make out, even with a macro photo?
field_questions.yes_means: Consistent with the reduced/absent eyes seen in many cave-restricted leptonetid populations

lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Pholcids are much larger (6-10+ mm body) with extremely long legs and build large loose tangle webs; leptonetids are far tinier (2-5 mm) with only minimal sheet webs

Telemids are similarly minute and live in similar dark/damp habitats; the two families are very hard to separate by eye and reliably need microscope examination of eye number/arrangement and genitalia

Also small pale spiders in damp leaf litter/caves with reduced eyes; again essentially indistinguishable without microscopic genitalic examination, though this family is not known to occur in mainland Portugal's cave fauna the way Leptonetidae and Telemidae do

source.title: Leptonetidae - identification key (genus level)
source.authors: Nentwig, Blick, Bosmans, Gloor, Hänggi & Kropf (eds.), Spiders of Europe / araneae.nmbe.ch

deeper_key.notes: This online key is built ONLY for adult MALES, using pedipalp characters (spination and shape of femur, tibia and tarsus) that require a hand lens or microscope to see reliably - they are not visible in a normal field photo. Females of this family are not covered by the key at all. Even at genus level the key cannot be done by eye in the field; for the three Portuguese species (Leptoneta berlandi, Leptoneta conimbricensis, Teloleptoneta synthetica) genus can sometimes be inferred from known cave locality and prior records, but confirming species identity reliably requires an adult male specimen examined under magnification, and in many leptonetid groups species-level separation needs comparison of genitalic (palpal) structures against the original taxonomic descriptions (e.g. Machado & Ribera 1986 for the two Leptoneta species, Machado 1951 for Teloleptoneta synthetica). Couplets 6-8 of the online key (separating Paraleptoneta, Sulcia, Cataleptoneta and Barusia by pedipalp femur spine arrangement and tarsus depression) are summarized only, not transcribed step-by-step, since none of those genera occur in Portugal; full text is at the source URL. Treat any field or photo ID beyond family, or at best genus from locality context, as provisional.

couplets.character: Sex of specimen

options.text: Male
Female

couplets.character: Pedipalp femur

options.text: Without strong spines
With strong spines

couplets.character: Pedipalp tibia

options.text: With strong spines
With few/small spines or none

couplets.character: Pedipalp tarsus appendices

options.text: Present
Absent

couplets.character: Pedipalp tibia, apical appendices

options.text: Two characteristic appendices present
Absent
options.leads_to: 6 (genera not present in Portugal: Paraleptoneta, Sulcia, Cataleptoneta, Barusia - truncated, see source)

records.found_check: No specific found-species photos were supplied by the user for this family (they have not yet photographed any Leptonetidae). The family characters above (tiny size, pale colour, six or reduced eyes, minimal sheet web, cave/litter habitat) are consistent with the three Portuguese species on record: Leptoneta berlandi, Leptoneta conimbricensis, and Teloleptoneta synthetica, all of which are subterranean/cave-associated Iberian endemics per the World Spider Catalog.

characters.web: Pirate spiders do not build their own prey-capture web. They are araneophagic (spider-eating) wanderers: they invade other spiders' webs and pluck/vibrate the silk strands to mimic a struggling insect or a courting mate, luring the resident spider close before biting and eating it. Some species also build a simple non-snare retreat or scaffold of dry silk lines, but there is no orb, sheet, or funnel snare typical of web-building families.
characters.eyes: Eight eyes in two slightly curved rows on a raised, often tubercle-like head region; the eye area is usually distinctly darker (almost black) than the rest of the carapace, giving a 'helmeted' look.
characters.body_shape: Small, somewhat globular abdomen that often bears one or more pairs of small bumps/tubercles on the rear half, giving an irregular, knobbly outline rather than a smooth oval. The carapace (prosoma) is comparatively narrow at the front (clypeus narrow in Mimetus, broader in Ero) and the whole body looks compact and round-shouldered.
characters.legs: The most distinctive feature: the front two pairs of legs (tibia and metatarsus I and II) carry a single row of long, stiff spines alternating with shorter spines in between — like a tiny saw-edged comb. In Ero the legs are all roughly equal length and the body is nearly as wide as long; in Mimetus the front legs are clearly longer than the back legs and the body is more elongate.
characters.colour: Mostly yellowish to reddish-brown with darker brown-to-black markings: dark eye region, a dark line down the middle of the head, legs ringed (annulated) with red-brown to black bands, and the abdomen patterned with black, reddish and grey markings on a paler yellowish background.
characters.behaviour_habitat: Found in low vegetation — trees, bushes, shrubs and leaf litter near the ground — usually in or right next to the webs of other spiders, which they raid for food. They are cryptic, slow-moving and rarely seen out in the open, which is why they are 'difficult to find' even where common. Body length in Portuguese species is small, roughly 2.5-5 mm.

source.title: Mimetidae genus key, Spiders of Europe (araneae.nmbe.ch)
source.authors: Nentwig, W., Blick, T., Bosmans, R., Gloor, D., Hänggi, A. & Kropf, C.

deeper_key.notes: The online key on araneae.nmbe.ch only resolves Mimetidae to GENUS (Ero vs Mimetus) in a single couplet; it does not provide species-level couplets. Of the 7 Portuguese species, 6 are in Ero and 1 (Mimetus laevigatus) is in Mimetus, so this genus couplet is still useful as a first split. Beyond genus, separating the Ero species (and confirming Mimetus laevigatus) from each other reliably requires examination of the epigyne (females) or palp/conductor shape (males) under a microscope — body pattern and size overlap too much between Ero species for confident naked-eye or photo-only species ID. Treat any photo-based species call within Ero as provisional.

couplets.character: Leg and body proportions

options.text: Legs about equal length; prosoma nearly as wide as long; clypeus hardly narrower than the ocular (eye) area
Anterior (front) legs longer than posterior legs; prosoma longer than wide; clypeus much narrower than the ocular area

field_questions.ask: Is the spider sitting motionless inside or right beside another spider's web (rather than its own snare)?
field_questions.yes_means: Consistent with Mimetidae's habit of invading other spiders' webs to hunt them; pirate spiders build no capture web of their own.

field_questions.ask: Looking closely at the front two pairs of legs (with a hand lens or zoomed photo), can you see a row of long spines with shorter spines tucked in between, like a tiny comb?
field_questions.yes_means: This spination pattern is the clearest diagnostic feature of Mimetidae and is hard to confuse with other families.

field_questions.ask: Does the abdomen have small bumps or tubercles on its rear half, giving a knobbly rather than smooth outline?
field_questions.yes_means: Typical of Mimetidae (especially Ero), though some other families also show abdominal tubercles, so use together with the leg-spine check.

field_questions.ask: Are the front legs noticeably longer than the back legs, with a body that looks longer than wide rather than rounded?
field_questions.yes_means: Points to genus Mimetus rather than the rounder, even-legged Ero, which covers 6 of the 7 Portuguese species.

lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Theridiidae (tangle-web/comb-footed spiders) often have a similarly globular, sometimes tuberculate abdomen, but lack the diagnostic comb-like spine row on the front legs and instead have a comb of serrated bristles on the hind tarsus (used to fling silk over prey); they also build their own irregular tangle web rather than invading others' webs.

Some theridiosomatids have a rounded, tuberculate abdomen too, but they build their own small tent-like or orb-like web and are usually much tinier (under 2 mm); Mimetidae instead live as araneophagic intruders with no web of their own and have the characteristic alternating-spine leg rows.

lookalikes.family: Anyphaenidae or other small free-living hunters
lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Free-running hunting spiders without a web can superficially resemble a wandering Mimetidae, but they lack both the dark helmeted eye region with the diagnostic leg spination and the tuberculate abdomen typical of pirate spiders.

records.found_check: No found species were supplied by the user for this family (none photographed yet), so there is nothing to check against the family characters above.

characters.web: free-living ground hunter, no snare web at all — these spiders run down prey rather than trapping it in silk
characters.eyes: eight eyes in two rows; the posterior lateral pair are distinctly larger than the other six eyes — the easiest eye-pattern clue for this family
characters.body_shape: small-to-medium, somewhat flattened, fast-running spider; oval abdomen, moderately long legs relative to body; prosoma (head-thorax shield) often shows a pattern of longitudinal stripes
characters.legs: legs noticeably striped — femur I and II typically show distinct dark brown/grey longitudinal stripes or rows of spots on a yellow-brown background; tarsus tips covered in dense scopula hairs (velvety adhesive pads) rather than tufted claw bundles; males have a characteristic tuft of hair near the front spinnerets
characters.colour: overall yellow-brown to greyish-brown, with a darker continuous lateral band running along the side of the prosoma and dark longitudinal leg stripes — a cryptic, leaf-litter-matching pattern, not brightly marked
characters.behaviour_habitat: ground-dwelling, fast-running nocturnal/diurnal hunters found in leaf litter, low vegetation, grassland, forest floor and similar humid-to-dry ground habitats across Europe; they do not build a web for prey capture and are usually encountered by sweeping vegetation or sieving litter rather than spotted in a web

field_questions.ask: Is there no web at all — just a spider running on the ground or in litter?
field_questions.yes_means: consistent with Miturgidae (Zora) — they are web-less ground hunters

field_questions.ask: Do the front legs (femur I-II) show clear dark longitudinal stripes or rows of dark spots on a paler background?
field_questions.yes_means: strong match for Zora — striped legs are the most visible field character

field_questions.ask: Looking at the top of the head/thorax shield, is there a darker continuous band running along each side?
field_questions.yes_means: matches the lateral prosoma band typical of Zora species

field_questions.ask: Is the spider small (under about 1 cm body length), fast-moving, and found in leaf litter or low ground vegetation rather than up on walls or in corners?
field_questions.yes_means: fits the typical Miturgidae habitat and habit in Portugal

lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Gnaphosidae have two forward-pointing, cylindrical spinnerets clearly visible at the rear tip of the abdomen and duller, more uniform grey-brown colouring without the bold leg stripes; Miturgidae (Zora) show striped legs and the spinnerets are not as prominently tube-like.

lookalikes.family: Eutichuridae / Cheiracanthiidae (yellow sac spiders, formerly placed in Miturgidae)
lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Cheiracanthium-type spiders are pale yellow-green, almost unmarked, with a uniformly coloured body and no leg striping, and they build a silk retreat/sac in rolled leaves — Zora has striped legs, a banded prosoma, and builds no retreat web.

Wolf spiders are generally larger and more robust, with a more obvious eye pattern of four small eyes in a nearly straight front row plus large posterior eyes arranged in a trapezoid, and they often carry an egg sac or carry young on the abdomen; Zora is smaller, slimmer, and lacks this maternal egg-sac-carrying behaviour as a field identifier.

source.title: Genus key to Miturgidae
source.authors: Nentwig, W., Blick, T., Bosmans, R., Gloor, D., Hänggi, A. & Kropf, C.

deeper_key.notes: The online genus key for Miturgidae on araneae.nmbe.ch is very shallow: it contains effectively a single couplet. It separates the genus Prochora (cymbium without spur; male pedipalp tibia about half cymbium length or shorter; epigyne shape distinct) from the alternative leading to Zora. No further couplets are given to separate Zora's ~16-17 European species from one another at this online key — species-level separation within Zora is handled on individual species pages via epigyne/vulva shape and male palp (tibial apophysis, cymbium spur) drawings, which generally require a specimen examined under magnification, not naked-eye or photo identification. So in practice this key reaches genus reliably from the family page, but reaching species requires comparing genitalia illustrations on each species' araneae.nmbe.ch page (often needs a cleared/dissected epigyne or palp under a scope). Portugal's Miturgidae are, as far as could be confirmed in this session, entirely within genus Zora (plus the family historically included Cheiracanthium species, now moved to Eutichuridae) — exact list of the 6 Portuguese Zora species was not independently re-verified against a single authoritative current checklist in this session beyond confirming several species (Z. spinimana, Z. armillata, Z. manicata, Z. silvestris, Z. parallela, Z. pardalis) are valid European Zora taxa with araneae.nmbe.ch pages; a dedicated Portugal-specific occurrence check (e.g. against GBIF Portugal records or the Iberian spider checklist, Zootaxa 2495) would be needed to confirm which exact six occur there.

couplets.character: Male pedipalp cymbium and tibia shape; epigyne shape

options.text: Cymbium without spur; tibia of the male pedipalp about half the cymbium length or shorter; epigyne of a distinct shape
(implicit alternative, not spelled out as separate text in the fetched key) cymbium with spur / tibia longer / epigyne differently shaped

records.found_check: No found-species list was supplied by the user for this family, so no check against found species could be performed. Note: in Portugal/Iberia Miturgidae as currently delimited (post-2018 family split) is represented essentially by genus Zora (plus possibly Prochora, mainly known from Africa/Mediterranean) — the family no longer includes Cheiracanthium (yellow sac spiders), which was moved to Eutichuridae. The appearance characters above (striped legs, banded prosoma, no web, ground-hunting) describe genus Zora specifically and should fit all 6 Portuguese species since they are very likely all Zora.

characters.behaviour_habitat: Lives in leaf litter, moss, and loose rock/scree debris on the forest floor, often in damp, shaded spots; can also turn up in caves and cave entrances (a 'troglophile' - cave-tolerant but not cave-limited). Builds a small three-dimensional web low down in litter or crevices that, seen from above, looks like a tiny version of an orb-weaver's wheel web. Rarely seen because of its tiny size and hidden lifestyle - more likely found by sieving leaf litter than by spotting it in the open.
characters.body_shape: Tiny, rounded, almost globular body - the abdomen (opisthosoma) is swollen and ball-like, much bigger than the small head-and-legs section (prosoma), giving a dwarfish, compact look completely unlike a typical web spider.
characters.colour: Head and leg section pale brownish-yellow; the round abdomen is whitish/pale, sometimes with a faint darker line down the back in males.
characters.eyes: Eight eyes in two rows, but the key field clue is that the front-middle pair of eyes is distinctly smaller than the other six eyes - unusual among small spiders and a quick check under magnification.
characters.legs: Legs pale brownish-yellow, short relative to body. Males have a small thorn-like spur sticking out on the underside of the first leg's metatarsus segment (visible only under magnification) - this is the single most reliable family-level clue but needs a hand lens or scope.
characters.web: Builds a small, irregular three-dimensional snare web in litter/debris, broadly orb-like in silhouette from above (echoing typical Araneidae orb webs) but tiny and tangled rather than a clean flat wheel.

records.common_group: dwarf/midget orb-weavers (sometimes called dwarf cobweb weavers)

deeper_key.notes: The araneae.nmbe.ch genus key for Mysmenidae is short (3 couplets) and gets you to genus reliably from external characters (eye-size ratio, leg-I spur, epigyne shape) visible under a stereo microscope. Going from genus to species, however, requires examining the male palp/embolus shape or the female epigyne internal structure (scapus presence/shape, sclerotization pattern) - these are genitalia-level characters that need a compound microscope and often slide-mounting or clearing of the epigyne. For the single Portuguese species (most likely Trogloneta granulum, the only Mysmenidae with a range reaching into the western/southern European mainland in the sources checked), reaching genus with the naked eye or a phone macro photo is unrealistic - the diagnostic features (eye-size ratio, leg spur, epigyne) are sub-millimetre. In practice, for Mysmenidae in Portugal, confident field identification stops at 'family' (you have a Mysmenidae) and species confirmation needs lab examination or an expert with a scope.

source.title: araneae - Spiders of Europe: Genus key for Mysmenidae
source.authors: Nentwig, W., Blick, T., Bosmans, R., Gloor, D., Hänggi, A., Kropf, C. & Stäubli, A.

couplets.character: Relative size of the anterior median eyes compared to the other eyes

options.text: Anterior median eyes smaller than the other eyes
Anterior median eyes equal to or larger than the other eyes

couplets.character: Male metatarsus I spur and epigyne/leg colouring

options.text: Male metatarsus I without a spur; epigyne without a scapus; legs unicoloured
Other combination of spur/epigyne/leg-colour characters

couplets.character: Leg I morphology and epigyne structure (illustrated couplet, fine differences in male leg I and female epigyne shape)

options.text: Leg I and epigyne characters matching Microdipoena pattern
Leg I and epigyne characters matching Mysmena pattern

field_questions.ask: Is the spider extremely tiny (about the size of a pinhead, roughly 1 mm long) with a noticeably round, ball-shaped abdomen much bigger than its head?
field_questions.yes_means: Consistent with Mysmenidae - they are among the smallest web-building spiders in Portugal.

field_questions.ask: Did you find it in damp leaf litter, under rocks/debris, or near a cave entrance rather than out in the open?
field_questions.yes_means: Matches the hidden, litter-and-crevice habitat typical of this family.

field_questions.ask: If you can see any web, does it look like a tiny, messy 3-D tangle rather than a flat wheel-shaped web or a loose cobweb?
field_questions.yes_means: Matches the small three-dimensional snare typical of Mysmenidae, distinct from the flat wheels of orb-weavers or the messy 2-D tangles of cobweb spiders.

field_questions.ask: Is the body pale brownish-yellow with a whitish, swollen abdomen, with no bold colour pattern or shiny black gloss?
field_questions.yes_means: Matches the plain, pale colouring typical of this family rather than the patterned or glossy look of many other small spiders.

records.found_check: No found species list was supplied for this family in the prompt, so there is nothing to cross-check the family characters against.

lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Anapidae are similarly minute and round-bodied but typically show a hardened (sclerotized) plate or bulge on the upper lip area (labrum) and overall heavier body armour; Mysmenidae lack this and instead show the diagnostic small front-middle eyes or a leg-I spur in males.

Small theridiids (cobweb spiders) build messy 2-D tangle webs and lack the male leg-I spur; Mysmenidae build a small 3-D snare and historically were even classified inside Theridiidae before being split out, so genitalia/spur examination is needed to be sure.

Oonopids are similarly tiny but usually have a hardened dorsal scutum (shield) over the abdomen and often six eyes rather than eight; Mysmenidae have a soft, unarmoured, ball-like abdomen and the full eight-eye arrangement.

characters.web: No snare web. Nemesiidae (the 'tube/trapdoor spiders' or 'curtain-web spiders') are burrowing mygalomorphs. Most Iberian species dig a silk-lined tunnel in the ground, often with a thin silk 'curtain' or collar at the entrance and sometimes radiating silk trip-lines; a true hinged trapdoor lid (like classic trapdoor spiders, family Ctenizidae) is not typical for the Iberian genera. They sit hidden in the burrow and ambush passing prey at night.
characters.eyes: Eight eyes arranged in two close rows on a raised lump (ocular tubercle) at the front of the head — typical of mygalomorphs. The eye group is small relative to the whole head and not usable for naked-eye genus identification; it mainly confirms 'this is a mygalomorph, not a true spider (Araneomorphae)'.
characters.body_shape: Heavy-bodied, compact, mygalomorph build: short thick legs, a rounded-to-oval abdomen, and a relatively flat, robust carapace (prosoma) with the eyes set on a small frontal hump. Chelicerae point straight down/forward (paraxial fangs) rather than pincer-like, another mygalomorph hallmark. Overall they look more 'tarantula-like' in miniature than like a typical garden spider.
characters.legs: Short, stout legs. A dense patch of short, velvety hairs (scopula) covers the underside of the front leg tips (tarsi and metatarsi of legs I and II) — this scopula is one of the defining family traits, used for grip while digging/climbing in the burrow. Males of some genera (e.g. Brachythele) have a spur with twin hooks on the front tibia, used to grip the female during mating — visible only on adult males with a hand lens.
characters.colour: Generally dull and earth-toned: shades of brown, grey-brown or blackish-brown, sometimes with a covering of fine whitish or greyish hairs giving a slightly frosted look. No bright patterns, bands or metallic colours — coloration is camouflage for life on/in soil.
characters.behaviour_habitat: Ground-dwelling burrowers found in dry, often sun-exposed banks, road cuts, olive groves, scrubland and open Mediterranean-type habitat across Portugal, typically in firm soil or among stones on slopes. They rarely wander far from the burrow entrance; sightings are usually of the silk-lined burrow mouth rather than the spider itself, or of wandering adult males (especially after autumn rains) searching for females. Nocturnal and rarely seen in the open during the day.

source.title: Nemesiidae — genus-level dichotomous key, Spiders of Europe
source.authors: Nentwig, W., Blick, T., Bosmans, R., Gloor, D., Hänggi, A., Kropf, C. & Stäubli, A.

deeper_key.notes: The araneae.nmbe.ch key for Nemesiidae stops at GENUS, not species — it separates Iberesia, Nemesia, Brachythele and Raveniola (Amblyocarenum is listed but not placed in the couplets). For the 12 Portuguese species (all in Nemesia and Iberesia), there is no equivalent online dichotomous species key on araneae.nmbe.ch; the only species-level treatment is the technical taxonomic revision (Decae, Cardoso & Selden 2007 in Revista Ibérica de Aracnología, and the 2006 Iberesia paper by Decae & Cardoso), which separates species using male palpal bulb shape, spermathecae shape in females, and spine counts/arrangement on legs — all features requiring a microscope and often dissection or at least a cleared, mounted palp. In the field or from a photo, Portuguese Nemesiidae can realistically be identified to FAMILY (sometimes genus, if spinneret count/shape is visible) but not reliably to species without expert examination of genitalia.

couplets.character: Number of spinnerets

options.text: Two spinnerets (posterior median pair absent)
Four spinnerets (posterior median pair present)

couplets.character: Posterior lateral spinneret shape and spacing of posterior median spinnerets

options.text: Posterior lateral spinnerets short with a domed last segment; posterior median spinnerets close together
Posterior lateral spinnerets long with a finger-like (digitiform) last segment; posterior median spinnerets far apart

couplets.character: Male tibia I armature (this couplet only separates genera not found in Portugal)

options.text: Tibia I with a distal spur bearing twin hooks
Tibia I without a spur, only distal spines

field_questions.ask: Did you find it inside or right at the entrance of a silk-lined burrow in a dirt bank or slope, rather than in a web strung between plants?
field_questions.yes_means: Strongly consistent with Nemesiidae (and other mygalomorphs) — these spiders live in burrows, not aerial webs.

field_questions.ask: Is the body short, thick and 'tarantula-like' (robust legs, no long thin abdomen), in dull brown/grey-brown tones with no bright pattern?
field_questions.yes_means: Consistent with a mygalomorph body plan typical of Nemesiidae.

field_questions.ask: Look at the underside of the two front leg tips with a macro photo or lens — is there a dense brush/pad of short hairs there (versus bare leg tips)?
field_questions.yes_means: That scopula is a defining Nemesiidae family trait, supporting this ID over other burrowing mygalomorphs.

field_questions.ask: Was it found in spring/summer in dry, stony or sandy soil in scrubland, road-cut banks or olive-grove edges (rather than damp woodland leaf litter)?
field_questions.yes_means: Matches the typical habitat reported for Portuguese Nemesia/Iberesia.

lookalikes.family: Ctenizidae (true trapdoor spiders, e.g. Cyrtocarenum/Nemesia-like genera in older literature)
lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Ctenizidae build a hinged, well-camouflaged silk-and-soil 'lid' (true trapdoor) over the burrow entrance with a snug-fitting cork-like flap; Nemesiidae burrows usually have only a thin silk collar/curtain at the rim, no hinged lid. On the spider itself the families overlap heavily in general mygalomorph body shape and are hard to separate without examining spinnerets and leg spination.

Very similar burrowing mygalomorphs with scopulae on the front legs too, but they lack the raised ocular process (eye hump) and recurved/short fovea that Nemesiidae has on the carapace — a subtle character usually needing close photos or a specimen in hand.

Some ground-dwelling wolf spiders also dig burrows and look superficially robust/hairy, but they are true (araneomorph) spiders with diagonally-arranged fangs and a distinctive eye pattern of two rows of differently-sized eyes (large rear-median pair), not the small two-row tubercle eyes of mygalomorphs; wolf spiders also actively run down prey above ground rather than staying hidden at a burrow mouth.

records.found_check: No specific found species list was supplied in this task to check against; the family characters above (mygalomorph burrowers, scopula on front leg tips, 2 vs 4 spinnerets distinguishing Iberesia from Nemesia, dull brown coloration, silk-collared burrow rather than aerial web) are consistent with the genera actually reported from Portugal (Nemesia spp. and Iberesia spp.) per the sources consulted.

characters.web: A loosely tangled, irregular 'scaffold' web of crisscrossing threads, often with sticky droplets on some lines, built in dark, humid recesses (cave walls, cellar corners, crevices) rather than in the open — similar in general untidiness to a house-spider's cobweb, but built by a much smaller, paler animal in deep shade
characters.eyes: Eight eyes in two curved rows is typical for the family, but Portugal's resident cave species (Domitius lusitanicus) is completely blind with no functional eyes at all, a classic adaptation to permanent cave life; the introduced second species (Eidmannella pallida) retains small, well-developed eyes including a single stiff bristle just behind the front-middle pair
characters.body_shape: Small, soft-bodied, theridiid-like spider: rounded oval abdomen, fairly long thin legs relative to body size (more pronounced/elongated in the cave-dwelling species), no obvious spines or armour
characters.legs: Long and slender compared to body size, especially elongated in the blind cave species; females have a row of stiffened, comb-like (serrated) bristles on the underside of the fourth leg's last segment (tarsus IV) — a microscopic but family-diagnostic feature, used like the comb Theridiidae use to pull silk from the spinnerets
characters.colour: Generally pale and washed-out: yellowish, light grey, or yellow-brown with little or no contrasting pattern; the resident Portuguese cave species is further depigmented (whitish/colourless) from living entirely without light, while the introduced species is yellowish-brown with a plain light-grey abdomen
characters.behaviour_habitat: Sedentary, shade- and moisture-loving spiders that sit in or near their scaffold web in dark, humid places: cave walls and ceilings (especially past the twilight zone), cellars, wells, crevices under stones, leaf litter and other below-ground spaces. In Portugal one species (Domitius lusitanicus) is a true troglobiont confined to limestone caves of the Estremenho Karst Massif in central Portugal; the other (Eidmannella pallida) is a non-native, synanthropic troglophile recorded in the Azores and Madeira, often near human structures and crops as well as caves

records.common_group: cave cobweb spiders / scaffold web spiders

source.title: Key to genera of Nesticidae (Spiders of Europe)
source.authors: Nentwig, W., Blick, T., Bosmans, R., Gloor, D., Hänggi, A. & Kropf, C.

deeper_key.notes: The online key reliably separates the European genera of Nesticidae (Aituaria, Carpathonesticus, Eidmannella, Nesticella, Nesticus, Typhlonesticus) using body size, eye presence, and — beyond couplet 3 — fine details of the male pedipalp (paracymbium shape, tegular apophyses, embolus form) that require a microscope and an adult male specimen. Of the two Portuguese genera, Eidmannella keys out cleanly at couplet 2 on size/pattern alone (field-usable with a hand lens); Domitius (the Portuguese cave genus) is not actually a terminus of this particular key version, since the key as fetched ends in Nesticus/Carpathonesticus/Aituaria/Typhlonesticus rather than listing Domitius explicitly — this reflects either a key version gap or Domitius being treated as embedded within a related couplet not retrieved. Species-level identification within either genus (e.g. confirming Domitius lusitanicus or distinguishing Eidmannella pallida from other Eidmannella) requires examining male palp or female epigyne microstructure under magnification; this is not achievable by eye or photo alone. For practical purposes in Portugal, family and genus can often be inferred confidently just from locality and blindness/pigmentation (cave-restricted blind specimens at Estremenho = Domitius lusitanicus; small pale eyed specimens in Azores/Madeira, often near buildings = Eidmannella pallida), since each is currently the only member of its genus reliably recorded in the country.

options.text: Large nesticids, body 3–7 mm
Small nesticids, body < 3 mm

options.text: Dorsal opisthosoma without pattern, light grey, body 2.0–2.4 mm
Opisthosoma unicolorous or patched, body 2.1–2.2 mm

couplets.character: Eye presence (large nesticids)

options.text: Eyeless; male pedipalp with very long embolus
Eyes well developed, reduced, or absent

options.text: Paracymbium entire distally, two unequal tegular apophyses
Different paracymbium/tegular apophysis combination

options.text: Tegular apophysis reduced to a small hook, embolus short and thick
Embolus flat in cross-section, embolic apex modified

field_questions.ask: Is the spider sitting in a small, loose, messy tangle-web (not a neat spiral) inside a cave, cellar, well, or deep crevice — somewhere dark and damp?
field_questions.yes_means: Consistent with Nesticidae habitat and web type rather than an orb-weaver or funnel-web spider

field_questions.ask: Is the spider pale, washed-out yellowish or greyish with little or no bold pattern, and quite small (roughly 2-6 mm body, not counting legs)?
field_questions.yes_means: Matches the typical small, pale, unpatterned look of nesticids as opposed to larger or more vividly marked cobweb spiders

field_questions.ask: Does the spider appear to have no eyes at all, or unusually long, thin legs for its tiny body, and was it found deep inside a true cave (well past the entrance light)?
field_questions.yes_means: Suggests Domitius lusitanicus, Portugal's blind cave-adapted nesticid, found only in caves of the Estremenho Karst Massif

field_questions.ask: Was the spider found near buildings, crops, or in the Azores/Madeira rather than a mainland limestone cave, and does it still have small but visible eyes?
field_questions.yes_means: Suggests Eidmannella pallida, the introduced species recorded from the islands

records.found_check: No specific found species were supplied for this family in the prompt, so no fit-check against found specimens was performed. The two Portuguese species used to ground the characters above (Domitius lusitanicus and Eidmannella pallida) were taken from the project's own GBIF/WSC-grounded layer1_skeleton.json (file: /Users/diogomontenegro/Claude/Projects/Aracnario/data/layer1_skeleton.json) and cross-checked against araneae.nmbe.ch and primary literature; both genuinely fit the family characters described (small size, pale colour, scaffold web, cave/dark-habitat association, serrated tarsus-IV bristles in females).

lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Theridiidae (tangle-web/comb-footed spiders) look extremely similar — same comb of serrated bristles on the hind legs and similar messy cobwebs — but most theridiids are larger, more often boldly patterned, and live in ordinary outdoor/indoor settings (foliage, window corners, sheds) rather than being concentrated in caves, cellars and other deep, dark, humid refuges; true confirmation needs the male palp (very large dark paracymbium in Nesticidae) under magnification

Also tiny, pale, cave-associated spiders in Portugal, but leptonetids have a more elongated, narrow body and very long thin legs without building an obvious scaffold/tangle web — they are more often found wandering or sitting on cave walls without a conspicuous web, whereas nesticids sit in or near a loose tangle web with sticky droplets

Cellar/daddy-long-legs spiders (Pholcidae) share the dark, damp habitat (cellars, caves) and pale colour, but have much longer, thinner legs relative to a tiny cylindrical body, and hang upside down in a flat, irregular web rather than the denser tangled scaffold web of nesticids

characters.web: free-living hunter, no snare web — they do not build capture webs; they actively hunt on foot through leaf litter, soil, bark crevices and indoor cryptic spaces
characters.eyes: most species have 6 eyes clustered tightly together in one compact group at the front of the head (the rare exception, the genus Diblemma, has only 2 eyes); the 6-eyed pattern with no gap between the eyes is the key giveaway versus 8-eyed families
characters.body_shape: tiny, oval to roundish body, often with a hard, shiny, heavily armoured plate (scutum) covering part or all of the upper and/or lower abdomen — gives them a shell-like, almost beetle-like or tick-like look rather than a typical soft spider abdomen
characters.legs: short to moderately short legs relative to body, not long and spindly; some genera (e.g. Orchestina) have a noticeably thickened/broadened fourth pair of leg segments (femora); a few genera have rows of stout spines on the underside of the front leg pairs, visible as small dark bristles under magnification
characters.colour: pale whitish, cream, straw-yellow or faint reddish-brown overall, often translucent-looking; the hardened scutum plates can appear glossy and slightly darker/redder than the rest of the body
characters.behaviour_habitat: extremely small and easily overlooked; lives in leaf litter, moss, soil, under bark and stones, in caves, and very commonly indoors in cellars, bathrooms and damp corners of buildings (several species are synanthropic and spread via human transport); slow, low, scuttling movement rather than jumping or web-sitting

field_questions.ask: Is the spider tiny (about the size of a pinhead to 2-3 mm), pale whitish/cream/straw coloured, with a shiny, hardened, slightly bulging plate on top of (or under) the abdomen rather than a normal soft hairy abdomen?
field_questions.yes_means: Strongly consistent with Oonopidae (goblin spider).

field_questions.ask: Can you see, with a strong loupe or macro photo, that the eyes are all bunched together in one tight cluster near the front of the head, rather than spread in two clear rows across the head?
field_questions.yes_means: Consistent with the 6-eyed Oonopidae pattern (versus 8-eyed look-alike families).

field_questions.ask: Is there no web or silk snare anywhere near the spider — was it just found wandering or hiding under bark, in leaf litter, in a damp indoor corner, or under a stone?
field_questions.yes_means: Consistent with Oonopidae's free-living, non-web-building hunting lifestyle.

field_questions.ask: Is the spider clearly smaller than 2-2.5 mm in body length (not counting legs) — i.e. close to the smallest spiders you have ever seen?
field_questions.yes_means: Supports Oonopidae over the superficially similar but larger Dysderidae (which exceed about 3 mm).

lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Dysderidae look like larger, less-armoured relatives — same general 6-eyed haplogyne body plan, but Dysderidae are noticeably bigger (over about 3 mm, often 9-15 mm) and lack the dense, shiny hardened scutum plates that make Oonopidae look almost beetle-like; Oonopidae are tiny (under about 2.5 mm) with that armoured shell look.

Small theridiids can be a similarly tiny pale size, but they build a loose tangle/cobweb snare and have 8 eyes in two rows, soft (not hardened/shiny) abdomens, and longer, thinner legs; Oonopidae build no web at all and have the tight 6-eye cluster plus the hardened scutum.

Because Oonopidae are so small and shell-like, complete beginners sometimes mistake them for mites or other tiny arthropods; spiders always have exactly 8 legs plus a pair of pedipalps at the front and a clear two-part body (cephalothorax + abdomen joined by a narrow waist), which mites and ricinuleids do not show in the same way.

source.title: Araneae: Spiders of Europe — Identification key, family Oonopidae (genus-level key)
source.authors: Nentwig, W., Blick, T., Bosmans, R., Gloor, D., Hänggi, A., Kropf, C. & Stäubli, A.

deeper_key.notes: The online araneae.nmbe.ch key reaches GENUS level only, using gross external characters (eye count, presence/extent of opisthosomal scutum, leg I spination, femur IV shape, prosoma shape and body length). It does not key out species. For the 12 Portuguese Oonopidae species, reliable species-level identification almost certainly requires examination of the epigyne (females) or palpal bulb/embolus (males) under a microscope or strong stereo loupe, following species descriptions/redescriptions in the World Spider Catalog (wsc.nmbe.ch) and the AMNH Planetary Biodiversity Inventory Oonopidae project literature (research.amnh.org/oonopidae). Field photos alone will, in most cases, only support a confident genus call, not species; some superficially similar species within genera such as Oonops or Ischnothyreus cannot be separated without genitalic examination.
