records.found_check: No specific list of the 12 Portuguese species was supplied with this task, so no per-species check was performed. The family-level characters above (6 eyes in a tight cluster, tiny size ≤2.5 mm, presence of a hardened opisthosomal scutum, no snare web, cryptic litter/bark/indoor habitat) are general Oonopidae traits that should apply across all genera/species recorded in Portugal, since the family is defined largely by this body plan; genus-level differences (femur IV shape, leg spination, scutum extent) are captured in the genus key above and can be used to check fit once the specific Portuguese genera/species list is available.

characters.web: free-living hunter, no snare web. They do not build capture webs at all — only a simple silk retreat/shelter tucked under a stone or in a crevice for resting
characters.eyes: Eight eyes arranged in two rows of four (the standard spider eye pattern), though in some species the outer eyes in each row sit so close together that at a glance it can look like only six eyes are present
characters.body_shape: Compact, smooth-looking spider with a rounded-to-oval abdomen (sometimes elongated, almost olive/torpedo-shaped, roughly twice as long as the cephalothorax) and sparse, fine hair covering; the cephalothorax (front body section) is granulous (finely pebbly-textured) rather than glossy
characters.legs: The single most obvious field character: the first pair of legs is massively thickened, much stouter and more heavily armoured than the other three pairs, and the spider habitually walks slowly holding these front legs raised up in front of it like a pair of clubs or boxing gloves, rather than resting them on the ground
characters.colour: Reddish-brown overall — the front body (prosoma) reddish and granulous with short pale hairs, legs reddish, and the abdomen ranging from uniform reddish-brown to a lighter brown, with light scattered hairs
characters.behaviour_habitat: A nocturnal, ground-dwelling ambush/stalking hunter found in dry, warm habitats — under stones, in leaf litter, or in crevices in dry soils. It walks slowly at night with its thickened front legs raised, gently feels prey before suddenly grabbing it with a rapid, powerful strike; by day it hides in a silk-lined retreat under a rock or in a crack rather than sitting in any kind of snare web

source.title: araneae - Spiders of Europe: Palpimanidae (family page) and species list
source.authors: Nentwig, W., Blick, T., Bosmans, R., Gloor, D., Hänggi, A. & Kropf, C.

deeper_key.notes: araneae.nmbe.ch confirms only ONE genus of Palpimanidae occurs in Europe — Palpimanus — so for Portugal the family-to-genus step is trivial (any Portuguese Palpimanidae found is Palpimanus; the only other European genera, Chedima and Sarascelis, do not occur in Portugal/Europe proper per the site's own note 'Only one genus in Europe: Palpimanus'). However, I could NOT retrieve a transcribable dichotomous text key beyond the family level: the family key page (https://araneae.nmbe.ch/key) gives only the two couplet characters that separate Palpimanidae from Zodariidae ('serrula present' + 'femur I strongly thickened'), and the genus page (https://araneae.nmbe.ch/genkey/66/Palpimanidae) and the species page (https://araneae.nmbe.ch/specieskey/418/Palpimanus) present species differences only as comparative bulbus/epigyne/vulva illustrations, not as written couplets — i.e. araneae.nmbe.ch's species-level 'key' for this genus is image-based genitalia comparison, not text. Genus Palpimanus has 11 species in the European fauna list (gibbulus, aegyptiacus, canariensis, cyprius, denticulatus, maroccanus, obscurus, orientalis, punctatus, sogdianus, uncatus), but only ONE species, Palpimanus gibbulus Dufour, 1820, is recorded from Portugal (confirmed via Branco et al. 2019 and araneae.nmbe.ch distribution data). So for a Portuguese specimen, reaching family (Palpimanidae) and genus (Palpimanus) is reliable from external/visible characters; confirming it is P. gibbulus specifically is currently a non-issue in Portugal because it is the only Palpimanidae species recorded there — but distinguishing it from its 10 congeners elsewhere in the Mediterranean would require comparing male palp (embolus/prong shape) or female epigyne/vulva morphology under a microscope, which araneae.nmbe.ch presents only as labelled photographs/drawings, not as a written dichotomous key. If a future Portuguese record of a second Palpimanus species ever turns up, genitalia examination under magnification would be unavoidable.

field_questions.ask: Are the front (first) pair of legs noticeably thicker, stouter and more armoured-looking than the other three pairs of legs?
field_questions.yes_means: Strongly suggests Palpimanidae (Palpimanus) — this is the family's signature trait

field_questions.ask: When it walks, does it move slowly and hold that thickened front leg pair raised up off the ground in front of its head, rather than walking on all eight legs normally?
field_questions.yes_means: Consistent with Palpimanidae's characteristic slow nocturnal walk with front legs held up like clubs

field_questions.ask: Is the spider reddish-brown all over with a finely pebbly (granular), not shiny, body surface, found under a stone, in leaf litter, or in a dry crevice rather than in any kind of web?
field_questions.yes_means: Matches the colouring and ground/crevice-dwelling, web-less habit of Palpimanus gibbulus, the only Portuguese species

field_questions.ask: Is there no trace of any capture web (sheet, funnel, or tangle of threads) near where you found it — just bare ground, leaf litter, or a silk-lined crack used purely as a hiding spot?
field_questions.yes_means: Confirms the family's free-living, non-web-building hunting style rather than a web-building lookalike family

lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Zodariidae are the closest relatives and the family the araneae.nmbe.ch key splits Palpimanidae from at the very next step; Zodariidae LACK a serrula (a tiny toothed structure on the chelicerae/jaws, not visible without magnification) and their first legs are NOT noticeably thickened — so in the field, look for the obviously bulky, club-like front legs of Palpimanidae, which Zodariidae do not have

Gnaphosidae are also dark/reddish-brown, fast-moving, web-less nocturnal ground hunters found under stones, but their legs are all roughly similar thickness (no oversized front pair) and they have two short, blunt, forward-pointing spinnerets that are usually visible at the abdomen tip — Palpimanidae's front-leg armour and club-carrying gait is the giveaway difference

lookalikes.family: Sparassidae / Philodromidae (running and crab spiders, generally)
lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: These fast ground- or bark-running spiders have evenly proportioned legs all used for fast scuttling; they never carry one leg pair raised and thickened like boxing gloves, which immediately separates them from Palpimanidae

records.found_check: No specific found/photographed species were supplied by the user for this family (the brief states they have not personally photographed any Palpimanidae species yet), so there are no records to cross-check against the family characters above.

records.size_mm_typical: 2-5 (one Iberian genus, Liophrurillus, up to about 5-6)

characters.web: free-living hunter, no snare web — they do not build a web to catch prey, but each spider spins a small silk tube or sac retreat on the ground (under bark, stones, leaf litter, moss) where it hides and molts/lays eggs
characters.eyes: eight eyes in two rows, both rows close together and not very wide compared to the head — a fairly tight, compact eye field, not a stretched-out row, with no enlarged front pair
characters.body_shape: small, somewhat ant-shaped (elongate) body: a flattish, often shiny carapace and an oval to slightly waisted abdomen; many species look pinched or constricted between cephalothorax and abdomen, helping the ant illusion; males of some species have a hardened shield (scutum) on the abdomen
characters.legs: legs are not spiny or hairy-looking, fairly thin and short relative to the body; in many species the front one or two leg pairs are darker or ring-banded with black compared to the rest of the legs
characters.colour: typically orange-brown to dark brown body with paler or bright spots on the abdomen against the darker ground colour; can look glossy; front legs sometimes ringed with black
characters.behaviour_habitat: ground-living, fast-moving daytime hunters found in leaf litter, under stones and bark, in moss, dry meadows, vineyards, heathland and sunny open woodland up to moderate elevation; several species move jerkily and hold their front legs up like antennae to mimic ants (Batesian mimicry), presumably to avoid predators that avoid distasteful ants

field_questions.ask: Is the spider very small (under about 5 mm body length, not counting legs) and moving freely on the ground rather than sitting in a web?
field_questions.yes_means: consistent with Phrurolithidae — they are tiny free-running ground hunters, never trapped in a web

field_questions.ask: Does it look ant-like — slim body, pinched waist, jerky quick movement, sometimes waving its front legs like antennae?
field_questions.yes_means: strong hint of Phrurolithidae (or another ant-mimic family such as Zodariidae or Salticidae — see lookalikes)

field_questions.ask: Are the front one or two pairs of legs noticeably darker or ring-banded compared to the rest of the body, with the body itself orange-brown to dark brown and a few pale spots on the abdomen?
field_questions.yes_means: matches the common look of Phrurolithus, the genus with most Portuguese species in this family

field_questions.ask: Did you find it under a stone, piece of bark, in leaf litter or dry open grassland rather than on a web or on foliage?
field_questions.yes_means: matches typical Phrurolithidae habitat (ground-dwelling, often in dry, sun-exposed places)

lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Corinnidae proper are larger (mostly 5 mm or more) and lack the dense dorso-basal leg bristle pattern of Phrurolithidae; the two families were only split apart taxonomically in 2014 and are reliably separated only by male palp or female epigyne structure, but body size alone (Phrurolithidae usually under 5 mm) is a useful field cue

Liocranidae are similarly small ground-running hunters but generally lack the strong ant-mimicking pinched body shape and leg-banding pattern typical of Phrurolithus; genitalia examination is needed for certainty

Zodariidae are also ground-dwelling ant-mimics but tend to be more uniformly dark/velvety without the bright abdominal spotting typical of Phrurolithus, and differ in leg spination; close inspection is needed to separate confidently

Ant-mimicking jumping spiders have unmistakably large, forward-facing anterior median eyes typical of all Salticidae; Phrurolithidae eyes are all small and roughly equal in size, with no enlarged front pair

source.title: Key to genera of Corinnidae / Phrurolithidae (combined key, content after Wunderlich 2012, hosted at the Phrurolithidae family page on araneae.nmbe.ch)
source.authors: Nentwig, W., Blick, T., Bosmans, R., Gloor, D., Hänggi, A. & Kropf, C. (araneae.nmbe.ch — Spiders of Europe); key content credited to Wunderlich, J. (2012), Identification key to the European genera of the spider families Corinnidae

deeper_key.notes: araneae.nmbe.ch does not host a separate dedicated key for Phrurolithidae; the family page at https://araneae.nmbe.ch/genkey/80/Phrurolithidae explicitly states the genera (Phrurolithus, Liophrurillus, Phrurolinillus, Orthobula, Bosselaerius) run through the same couplets as Corinnidae and Trachelidae genera before splitting off, and directs the user to the combined Corinnidae genus key. That key reaches GENUS only, not species. Within Phrurolithus — the genus with by far the most Portuguese species (e.g. P. festivus, P. minimus, P. latitans and others) — separating species requires examining the male palp (shape of the tibial apophysis) or the female epigyne/vulva under a microscope; araneae.nmbe.ch shows this via genitalia drawings on each species' own data page rather than as a dichotomous key. So the honest ceiling for a naked-eye or phone-photo identification is family, and with a good photo of the leg-banding/colour pattern, often genus (Phrurolithus by elimination, since the other Iberian genera are rare/restricted). Confident species-level identification needs a collected specimen and microscope examination of genitalia, ideally cross-checked against the World Spider Catalog and araneae.nmbe.ch species accounts.

records.found_check: No specific found/observed species list was supplied by the user for this family. The general appearance characters above (small size 2-5 mm, eight small roughly equal eyes, ant-mimicking elongate body, free-living ground hunter with no snare web, orange-to-dark-brown colouring with banded front legs as in Phrurolithus festivus) are consistent with the Portuguese Phrurolithidae fauna, which is dominated by the genus Phrurolithus (e.g. P. festivus, P. minimus, P. latitans and similar small species); the other genera in the family (Liophrurillus, Phrurolinillus, Orthobula, Bosselaerius) are rare or marginal in the Iberian Peninsula by comparison.

characters.web: Free-living hunter, no snare web. These are ground-dwelling spiders that hunt on foot rather than spinning a web to catch prey.
characters.eyes: Eight eyes in two rows; the family is distinguished mainly by a feature you cannot see with the naked eye (plumose/feathered hairs on the underside tips of the spinnerets) rather than by eye arrangement. Anterior median eyes are often dark/black while the lateral eyes can look paler, silvery, or whitish in some genera.
characters.body_shape: Small, fairly flat, ground-running spider with a long, somewhat narrow abdomen; legs comparatively long and slender for the body size. Some genera have a clear central pit (fovea) on the carapace, others lack one - this is a key character but needs a clear top-down photo to see.
characters.legs: Legs long and thin relative to body size, built for fast running rather than climbing or jumping; in one Iberian genus the front and back leg segments closest to the body (trochanters) are unusually elongated, almost as long as the segment before them - a subtle character usually needing close inspection.
characters.colour: Often a combination of brownish, reddish, or orange-brown tones; the example Portuguese species shows a velvety grey-to-brown body with yellow-orange to red-brown legs, and dark chelicerae (fangs).
characters.behaviour_habitat: Ground dwellers found under stones and in litter/soil in warm, dry Mediterranean-type habitats; mostly nocturnal, hiding by day. Not web-builders - they actively hunt small invertebrates at night.

field_questions.ask: Is it found under a stone, log, or in leaf litter/soil rather than in a web?
field_questions.yes_means: Consistent with Prodidomidae - they are free-running ground hunters, not web builders.

field_questions.ask: Does the body look brownish, reddish, or orange-brown overall, with a fairly long, narrow abdomen and long thin legs?
field_questions.yes_means: Consistent with the general look of this family, though many ground spider families share this build.

field_questions.ask: Can you see a clear central pit or dimple on the front body segment (carapace) under a hand lens or zoomed photo?
field_questions.yes_means: Presence or absence of this pit (fovea) is one of the main characters separating the three genera in this family - though it needs close inspection to be reliable.

field_questions.ask: Was it active at night, or found hiding during the day rather than out in the open?
field_questions.yes_means: Matches the typical nocturnal, litter-hiding behaviour of this family.

lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Prodidomidae was historically classified as a subfamily of Gnaphosidae and looks very similar in general shape and ground-hunting habit. The technical separating feature is conspicuous plumose (feathery) hairs on the tips of the spinnerets in Prodidomidae - effectively impossible to see without magnification, so in the field these two families often cannot be told apart confidently.

Similarly small, ground-running, often ant-mimicking or ant-hunting spiders found under stones; Zodariidae usually have a more rounded abdomen and different spinneret structure, but distinguishing the two reliably again needs close examination, not just naked-eye looks.
General body plan (flattish, fast ground runners) overlaps; family-level separation in published keys relies on spinneret and eye micro-characters rather than anything visible in a casual photo.

source.title: araneae.nmbe.ch genus-level key to Prodidomidae
source.authors: Nentwig, W., Blick, T., Bosmans, R., Gloor, D., Hänggi, A. & Kropf, C.

deeper_key.notes: The online genus key only goes to genus level (3 European/Mediterranean genera: Zimiris, Prodidomus, Zimirina) and is very short - just 2 couplets. It relies on the fovea (a central pit on the carapace) and relative trochanter length, both of which require a clear, well-lit photo or specimen in hand to judge reliably; they are not obvious in a casual field photo. Going from genus to species (e.g. confirming Prodidomus amaranthinus vs other Prodidomus species, or Zimirina brevipes vs other Zimirina species) is NOT covered by this online key and in the wider literature typically requires examining the epigyne (female) or palp (male) genitalia under a microscope - this is standard for ground/gnaphosid-type spiders generally. For the two Portuguese species treated here, naked-eye or phone-photo identification can realistically only narrow things down to family or genus with confidence; species-level certainty needs a specimen and genitalic examination referenced against the original species descriptions or a regional revision (e.g. the Iberian checklist by Branco, Morano & Cardoso 2019).

couplets.character: Presence or absence of a fovea (central pit) on the carapace

options.text: Fovea present, short
Fovea absent

couplets.character: Length of trochanters (leg segments near the body) relative to the coxae

options.text: Trochanters (especially legs I and IV) unusually long, about as long as the coxae
Trochanters distinctly shorter than the coxae

records.found_check: No found-species list was supplied for this task, so there is nothing to cross-check against the family characters above. Note for the user: the two Portuguese species are most likely Prodidomus amaranthinus (confirmed on araneae.nmbe.ch with Portugal in its distribution: velvety grey-brown body, yellow-orange to red-brown legs, distinct fovea, 6-10 mm, found under stones) and Zimirina brevipes (listed for the Iberian Peninsula including Portugal on the Arácnidos Ibéricos/GIA database, though I could not retrieve a full araneae.nmbe.ch description page for it - its genus places it in the "fovea absent, trochanters short" branch of the key). If the user has a different confirmed pair of 2 species in mind, the genus-level characters and key above still apply, but the found_check should be redone against the actual confirmed names.

characters.web: free-living hunter, no snare web — it does not spin a web to catch prey; it hides flat in crevices and ambushes prey with an extremely fast pounce
characters.eyes: 8 eyes in two rows on the front of the head: a row of 6 eyes across the front, with 2 more set further back/to the sides — looks different from the eye clusters of most other spiders
characters.body_shape: extremely flat, almost paper-thin body (the giveaway feature) that lets it squeeze into narrow cracks, under bark, behind shutters, in wall crevices and under stones; carapace and abdomen both flattened
characters.legs: long legs that point sideways/outward from the body (laterigrade, crab-like) rather than forward; legs get longer from the front pair to the back pair; legs often show pale/white banding (annulations) against a grey or brown background
characters.colour: mottled grey, brown, yellow or orange overall — a camouflage pattern that blends with bark, stone or wall surfaces; carapace often brown with darker streaks radiating from the centre; abdomen has dark mottling with small pale/white speckles; legs banded grey and white
characters.behaviour_habitat: nocturnal and very fast-moving; hides flush against vertical surfaces such as walls, rocks, tree bark, or under stones and loose bark during the day, often near or inside buildings/outbuildings; when disturbed it flattens itself further against the surface or darts away sideways at high speed rather than dropping or jumping

source.title: Key to genera of Selenopidae (araneae.nmbe.ch genus key)
source.authors: Nentwig, W., Blick, T., Bosmans, R., Gloor, D., Hänggi, A. & Kropf, C. — Spiders of Europe

deeper_key.notes: There is effectively no genus-level branching to transcribe: araneae.nmbe.ch states plainly that only one genus, Selenops, occurs in Europe, so the 'key' for this family in the European fauna is a single statement rather than a dichotomous couplet sequence. The same site's family-level key (araneae.nmbe.ch/key, couplet 18) separates Selenopidae from look-alike families using two eye rows plus the extremely flattened body. Species-level separation within Selenops (e.g. S. radiatus vs other Selenops species recorded in the wider Mediterranean/Macaronesian region) is not covered by an online dichotomous key on araneae.nmbe.ch and in practice requires examination of male pedipalp structures (tibial apophysis, median apophysis branching, conductor shape) or female epigyne/vulva under a microscope, as shown on the species datasheets (e.g. araneae.nmbe.ch/data/2933/Selenops_radiatus). For a single-species Portuguese fauna, reaching family (and effectively genus, since only one genus occurs) is the realistic field ceiling; confirming the exact species would need a specialist or genitalia examination.

field_questions.ask: Is the spider's body almost completely flat, like it has been pressed flat (much flatter than a typical house spider)?
field_questions.yes_means: Strongly consistent with Selenopidae — this is the family's hallmark feature

field_questions.ask: Were the legs sticking out sideways from the body (not bunched together pointing forward) and roughly increasing in length from the front pair to the back pair?
field_questions.yes_means: Consistent with the laterigrade, crab-like leg arrangement typical of Selenopidae

field_questions.ask: Was it found pressed flat against a wall, rock, tree bark, or hiding in a crack/crevice (rather than on a web or out on foliage)?
field_questions.yes_means: Matches the typical Selenopidae habitat and hiding behaviour

field_questions.ask: Did it move in a sudden, very fast dash sideways when disturbed, rather than jumping or dropping on a thread?
field_questions.yes_means: Matches the extremely fast, free-running escape behaviour typical of flatties

lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Philodromids also have laterigrade sideways-pointing legs and flattish bodies, but they are not nearly as extremely flattened as Selenopidae, and in philodromids the second pair of legs is usually the longest; Selenopidae are flatter overall and leg length increases steadily front-to-back rather than peaking at leg II.
Thomisids have a rounder, less flattened body, usually sit on flowers/foliage rather than flush against bark or walls, and lack the extreme dorsoventral flattening of Selenopidae.

Huntsmen are large, fast, laterigrade-legged hunters too, but their bodies are not flattened to the same paper-thin degree, and the leg arrangement/eye row pattern differs; huntsmen are otherwise the closest in general impression (fast wall-runners) but Selenopidae are flatter and smaller in the European fauna.

records.found_check: No species list was provided to check against in this task — only the family-level brief specifying 1 Portuguese species. Based on cross-referencing araneae.nmbe.ch (only genus Selenops in Europe; S. radiatus recorded for Spain and broader Mediterranean) and the World Spider Catalog (S. spixi listed as native only to Brazil/Uruguay/Argentina, not Iberia), the one Portuguese species is almost certainly Selenops radiatus, though no source I fetched explicitly lists "Portugal" by name in its distribution (closest confirmed locality is Spain). This should be flagged as a residual uncertainty: confidence is medium rather than high because I could not find a source explicitly naming Portugal for S. radiatus, only strong circumstantial/geographic support (Iberian Peninsula treatment on Arácnidos Ibéricos, Mediterranean range, confirmed Spain record).

characters.web: Builds a tiny sheet-like (horizontal) web suspended by vertical threads from above, rather than a typical orb web; in the one Portuguese species this sheet-web form is noted as unusual even within the family/genus, which more typically makes minimal, reduced webs
characters.eyes: Only 4 eyes (most related families have 6 or 8), arranged as two pairs (diads) set well apart from each other; eyes are tiny and the spider relies very little on vision
characters.body_shape: Extremely minute spider with a rounded, globular abdomen (opisthosoma) much larger than the small head region (carapace); the carapace is notably low/flattened compared to related families, and the clypeus (area below the front eyes) is unusually tall; the chelicerae (jaws) are fused to each other at the base, which is unusual among spiders
characters.legs: Short, slender legs; the tarsus (last leg segment) is nearly twice as long as the metatarsus before it; the patella carries one long, stiff, upright bristle visible under magnification
characters.colour: Pale yellowish-brown overall - head, jaws and underside yellowish brown, abdomen light yellowish brown, legs somewhat darker than the body
characters.behaviour_habitat: Found only deep inside limestone caves (a troglobiont - lives permanently underground, never seen outdoors or in buildings); extremely localized, known in Portugal only from a single cave system (Frade Cave System, Sesimbra); females hang tiny egg sacs with just 2-3 eggs from the web, mainly December to May; no males have ever been found in the Portuguese population, suggesting it reproduces asexually (parthenogenesis)

field_questions.ask: Is this spider unbelievably tiny - about the size of a pinhead or smaller, roughly half a millimetre in body length, far smaller than almost any other spider you'd notice?
field_questions.yes_means: Consistent with Symphytognathidae - at this size almost nothing else in Portugal overlaps

field_questions.ask: Did you find it deep inside a cave, not near the entrance, not outdoors, and not in a cellar or other man-made structure?
field_questions.yes_means: Consistent with the only Portuguese species, which is a strict cave-dweller (troglobiont) known from just one cave system

field_questions.ask: Does it look like it is sitting in or hanging from a small, flat, horizontal sheet of web strung up by a few threads, rather than a round spiral orb web?
field_questions.yes_means: Matches the unusual sheet-web described for the Portuguese species

field_questions.ask: Is the body pale yellowish-brown all over, with the rounded abdomen noticeably bigger than the tiny head end?
field_questions.yes_means: Matches the colour and body shape described for this family

lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Also tiny, but Mysmenidae have a clearly raised/elevated carapace (not flattened), 6 or 8 eyes rather than 4, and males have a distinctive clasping spine on the front legs - features only visible under a microscope, but the body size and eye count are the best practical clue
Anapidae are similarly minute orb-weaving relatives but have unfused chelicerae and typically 6 eyes; they are not restricted to caves the way the Portuguese Symphytognathidae species is

Theridiosomatid 'ray spiders' are also very small araneoid web-builders but build tensioned orb-like webs they pull into a cone shape, have unfused chelicerae, and live in vegetation/leaf litter rather than deep cave habitats

source.title: araneae.nmbe.ch family-level key (Key to families) and genus page for Symphytognathidae
source.authors: Nentwig, Blick, Bosmans, Gloor, Hanggi, Kropf & Staubli (araneae - Spiders of Europe)

deeper_key.notes: araneae.nmbe.ch's family key (couplet ~20) separates Symphytognathidae from look-alike haplogyne families (e.g. Leptonetidae) using fused chelicerae, body size under 0.6 mm, and cave habitat - this single couplet is the full 'key' needed at the family level. Below family level there is effectively no genus-level branching key to transcribe: the dedicated genus page (araneae.nmbe.ch/genkey/77/Symphytognathidae) states plainly that only one genus, Anapistula, is recorded in Europe, and within Portugal only one species, Anapistula ataecina, is known - so reaching species is automatic once you have confirmed the family and country. For other parts of the world with multiple genera/species (e.g. Patu, Anapistula spp. elsewhere, Crassignatha), proper separation requires examining fused chelicerae shape, eye spacing, leg seta patterns and ultimately female genitalia/male palp structure under a microscope or SEM, as done in the original species description (Cardoso & Scharff 2009, Zootaxa 2246). In Portugal, however, family-level diagnosis (tiny size + cave habitat + fused chelicerae + 4 eyes in two diads) is sufficient to call it Anapistula ataecina with high confidence, since it is the only species in this family known from the country.

records.found_check: Anapistula ataecina matches every family character above: it is extremely minute (0.52 mm body length, within the family's sub-0.6mm size class), has fused chelicerae at the base, only 4 eyes arranged in two widely-spaced diads, a pale yellowish-brown body with a globular, proportionally large abdomen, slender legs with a long bristle-bearing patella, and - critically - it is strictly cave-dwelling (troglobiont), known only from the Frade Cave System near Sesimbra, Portugal. Its sheet-like web is noted in the original description as atypical even for this family/genus (most symphytognathids make smaller, less developed webs), so the web-shape field question is the least diagnostic of the four characters - the size, habitat, eye count and colour are far more reliable.

characters.web: Probably builds a small, irregular sheet web (recorded as such for Cepheia longiseta); webs and biology are very poorly documented because the spiders are so small and rarely collected except by sieving leaf litter or soil/pitfall trapping.
characters.eyes: Not distinctively described in available sources (eight eyes, typical araneoid arrangement); no special eye pattern is used to recognise this family in the field. The real diagnostic feature is internal/microscopic: the chelicerae bear a keel ending in a strong tooth, visible only under magnification.
characters.body_shape: Extremely tiny, compact spiders; abdomen (opisthosoma) described as globular in the one well-documented Portuguese species. Overall body plan is unremarkable and easily mistaken for a juvenile of another family because of the minute size.
characters.legs: Short, unremarkable legs; in Cepheia longiseta metatarsus I has no spines and males lack a spur on metatarsus I (a character used to separate the two genera, not visible without a microscope). Legs are uniformly coloured (not banded).
characters.colour: Female Cepheia longiseta: prosoma olive-brown with a large median blackish blotch and very thin pale lines; abdomen blackish; legs brownish. Overall a dull brown/olive, inconspicuous colouration typical of leaf-litter dwellers.
characters.behaviour_habitat: Free-living, web-building spiders (not kleptoparasitic, unlike some related dwarf-spider families). Found in leaf litter, soil surface and low dry vegetation/fields; one Portuguese mainland species (Cepheia longiseta) is a West Mediterranean leaf-litter/sheet-web species, and the other Portuguese species (Synaphris saphrynis) is known from the Madeira archipelago (Porto Santo, Bugio/Desertas, Selvagens Islands) in dry habitats, collected mainly by pitfall trapping and litter sieving. Considered rare/under-recorded rather than truly rare in nature, simply because of their minute size.

field_questions.ask: Is the spider extremely tiny - body under about 1.5 mm, smaller than a pinhead - and hard to see clearly without magnification?
field_questions.yes_means: Consistent with Synaphridae; this family is among the smallest spiders you can find in Portugal.

field_questions.ask: Was it found in leaf litter, soil, or low dry vegetation (e.g. by sieving litter or in a pitfall trap) rather than on a web in open vegetation?
field_questions.yes_means: Consistent with the known collecting method/habitat for both Portuguese species.

field_questions.ask: Is the abdomen dark (blackish) and rounded/globular, with the legs plain brownish and not banded or patterned?
field_questions.yes_means: Matches the only well-described Portuguese species, Cepheia longiseta; supports (but does not confirm) family identity.

field_questions.ask: Are you in mainland Portugal (suggesting Cepheia longiseta) or in the Madeira archipelago, including Porto Santo, the Desertas or Selvagens Islands (suggesting Synaphris saphrynis)?
field_questions.yes_means: Helps narrow which of the two Portuguese species is more likely, since the two have essentially non-overlapping known ranges in Portuguese territory.

lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Theridiid cobweb spiders are usually larger overall and build a conspicuous tangled 3-D cobweb (often with sticky gumfoot lines to the ground); Synaphridae are far tinier (under ~1.2 mm), build at most a small inconspicuous sheet, and are mostly found in litter rather than on obvious webs.

Many erigonine linyphiids overlap closely in size and in building small sheet webs in litter and low vegetation; reliable separation from Synaphridae requires the cheliceral keel-and-tooth character and genital examination under a microscope - they cannot be told apart confidently by eye in the field.

These are the other extremely minute, leaf-litter-dwelling 'symphytognathoid' families related to Synaphridae; all are similarly tiny and cryptic, and separating them reliably again depends on microscopic characters (spinneret spigot morphology, genitalia), not anything visible in the field.

couplets.character: Metatarsus I in males without a spur; epigyne without scapus; legs unicoloured

options.text: Character present as described, separating the family's two main genera shown in this key
options.leads_to: Cepheia / Synaphris (genus-level split; the key page does not resolve this couplet further into separate branches)

source.title: Spiders of Europe - Genus key, family 70 Synaphridae
source.authors: Nentwig, W., Blick, T., Bosmans, R., Gloor, D., Hanggi, A. & Kropf, C.

deeper_key.notes: The araneae.nmbe.ch genus-level key for Synaphridae is extremely shallow: it presents essentially a single couplet distinguishing genus-level characters (metatarsus I spur absence in males, epigyne lacking a scapus, unicoloured legs) and does not lead further to species. Reliable species-level identification within this family (e.g. separating Cepheia longiseta from various Synaphris species, or among Synaphris species) requires examination of genitalia (male pedipalp bulbus/embolus shape, female epigyne and internal structures) and sometimes spinneret spigot morphology under a microscope or SEM, as used in the primary taxonomic literature (Lopardo, Hormiga & Melic 2007; Marusik et al. 2005). For Portugal specifically, only two species are known (Cepheia longiseta on the mainland, Synaphris saphrynis in the Madeira archipelago), and in practice their ranges barely overlap in Portuguese territory, so locality is currently the most practical field-level clue; this is not a substitute for genitalic examination if genuine confirmation is needed.

characters.web: Builds a small, highly modified orb web (a few cm across) that is pulled into a cone shape by the spider. The spider sits at the hub and holds a tension thread running from the web's centre out to a twig or grass stem; when an insect hits the web, the spider releases or pulls the thread so the whole web springs forward like a catapult/slingshot to snare the prey. Webs are usually built low down, just above the surface of small puddles or among damp streamside/bog vegetation.
characters.eyes: Eight eyes in two rows, eyes roughly similar in size. The front (anterior) row curves backward, the back (posterior) row is fairly straight. The two large front-middle eyes touch each other, side eyes on each side touch each other too, and the carapace is comparatively long in front of the eyes (gives the head a 'long-faced' look). Not somthing you can usually judge without magnification, but the head looks evenly rounded with no obvious dark eye cluster.
characters.body_shape: Tiny spider, overall pear-shaped: the carapace (front body part) is small and rounded, and the abdomen is distinctly globe-like, ball-shaped and shiny. The abdomen is proportionally large compared to the small head/leg section, giving a 'dewdrop with legs' appearance.
characters.legs: Thin, fairly short, unremarkable spiny legs without obvious heavy spination or unusual modification; brown in colour, sometimes with faint dark speckling or banding (annulation). Legs are not used to identify the family by the naked eye, the abdomen shape and web are far more diagnostic.
characters.colour: Carapace light brown with a fine net-like (reticulate) pattern, darker around the eyes. The abdomen is the standout feature: irregularly marked with black, but with bright patches that look strikingly pearly, silvery or metallic, almost like a tiny shining bead. Sternum dark brown.
characters.behaviour_habitat: Found in permanently damp, sheltered places: bog forest, marshes, fens, wet heath, ditches, and especially low vegetation right at the edge of small puddles or slow water. Very small and easy to overlook, the spider waits at the centre of its tensioned web and uses the slingshot mechanism described above to catch tiny flying insects (e.g. midges) passing by. Active mainly from spring through autumn.

source.title: araneae - Spiders of Europe: family Theridiosomatidae (genus key) and species page for Theridiosoma gemmosum
source.authors: Nentwig, W., Blick, T., Bosmans, R., Kropf, C. & Stäubli, A.

deeper_key.notes: There is no multi-couplet dichotomous key needed at genus level for this family in Europe, because araneae.nmbe.ch states explicitly that only one genus, Theridiosoma, occurs in Europe — so 'genus key' collapses to a single identification (family = genus by elimination once you recognise the globular silvery abdomen and conical slingshot web). Within Europe only a small handful of Theridiosoma species are known and in Portugal only T. gemmosum has been recorded (confirmed via Iberian/Portuguese spider checklist literature), so reaching species is realistic from range + habitat + the diagnostic web/body description above without a microscope. However, fully certain species-level confirmation in genera with more than one similar species elsewhere in the world, or distinguishing close relatives if more than one Theridiosoma species is ever found together, would require examining genitalia (epigyne in females, palp in males) under magnification, which araneae.nmbe.ch's own species accounts rely on for original determinations. Treat the field call as 'very likely T. gemmosum if found in Portugal' rather than 'certain without a specialist check'.

field_questions.ask: Is the abdomen a tiny, shiny, ball-like globe (almost bead-like, often with silvery or pearly patches) rather than flat or angular?
field_questions.yes_means: Consistent with Theridiosomatidae (ray spiders) rather than most other small web-builders.

field_questions.ask: Is the web a small, roughly circular (orb) web pulled into a cone shape, with a single thread running from the centre out to a twig, and is the spider sitting in the middle holding that thread taut?
field_questions.yes_means: Strongly suggests Theridiosomatidae's distinctive 'slingshot' tension web, which no other Portuguese spider family builds in this way.

field_questions.ask: Is the spider sitting in very damp, low vegetation right next to standing water (puddle edge, bog, marsh, ditch) rather than in dry scrub, on walls, or indoors?
field_questions.yes_means: Matches the known wet-habitat preference of Theridiosoma gemmosum, the only Portuguese species.

field_questions.ask: Is the spider extremely small (overall body under about 2.5 mm, not counting legs)?
field_questions.yes_means: Consistent with this family; if it is much bigger, look at another family instead.

records.found_check: No specific found specimens were given for this task, so there is nothing to cross-check against. If any spiders are later found with a small shiny globular silvery-patched abdomen, a conical tension/slingshot web suspended over damp ground or puddle edges, and a body under ~2.5 mm, they fit the Theridiosomatidae profile above and are very likely Theridiosoma gemmosum, the only Portuguese representative of the family.

lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Theridiidae build messy, irregular tangle/cobweb structures, not a neat (even if cone-shaped) orb with a single taut trigger thread; Theridiidae abdomens, while sometimes globular, are not typically silvery/pearly and the spiders are usually larger (many mm) than the sub-3mm ray spiders.

Araneidae build large, flat, classic wheel-shaped orb webs without the tension/slingshot trick and without pulling the web into a cone; they are also generally much larger-bodied than the tiny Theridiosomatidae.
Linyphiidae make flat or dome-shaped sheet webs, not circular orb-like webs, and their abdomens are typically elongate or oval rather than spherical and shiny silver.

characters.web: Cribellate hackled-band web: a sheet of bluish-white, fluffy/woolly-looking silk spun close to or on a substrate surface (under stones, bark, in rock crevices, low vegetation), not an open orb or funnel. The silk has a frosted, slightly fuzzy texture from the cribellum (a special silk-spinning plate) combed out by the calamistrum on the hind legs.
characters.eyes: Eight eyes in two rows, fairly close together, typical entelegyne arrangement; not enlarged or distinctively patterned. Not a useful field character by naked eye — too small to assess without magnification.
characters.body_shape: Small to medium spider with a fairly flat, compact body; carapace (prosoma) oval; opisthosoma (abdomen) rounded to oval, sometimes with faint pale paired spots in some genera (e.g. Nurscia) or plain/with one or two indistinct pale marks (Titanoeca). Legs of moderate length, not unusually long or spiny.
characters.legs: Three claws per tarsus (standard); hind legs of females and males carry a calamistrum (a row of curved bristles) used to comb cribellate silk — visible only under magnification, not to the naked eye. Legs otherwise unremarkable, brownish, no strong banding.
characters.colour: Generally dark: brown to blackish-brown prosoma and legs, often with the femora noticeably darker/blackened; opisthosoma dark grey to blackish, sometimes with small pale (whitish) spots arranged in pairs along the back in some species. Overall a dull, dark, unshowy spider.
characters.behaviour_habitat: Ground- and substrate-dwelling hunters that build a low, hackled silk sheet/lining (not a high aerial web) under stones, in crevices, at the base of vegetation, on bark or walls, often in dry, sun-exposed, rocky, or Mediterranean-type habitats; mainly nocturnal, hiding by day in a silk-lined retreat and emerging at night.

records.common_group: hackled mesh weavers (cribellate ground spiders, Titanoeca/Nurscia group)

deeper_key.notes: The araneae.nmbe.ch genus key for Titanoecidae goes only to genus (Titanoeca, Nurscia, Pandava, plus Goeldia listed without characters since it does not occur in the area covered). It does NOT separate species. Species-level identification within Titanoeca and Nurscia in the literature relies on male pedipalp structure (tibial apophysis shape, cymbium proportions) and female epigyne/vulva structure, which require a microscope and often a cleared, dissected vulva to see internal structures — this is not achievable from a field photo or naked-eye inspection. For Portuguese material, genus can realistically be called in the field/photo (look for paired pale opisthosoma spots = Nurscia vs plain/near-plain = Titanoeca), but confirming species (e.g. distinguishing Titanoeca monticola from T. praefica or other regional congeners) needs a specimen examined under magnification by someone using current revisionary literature (Wunderlich, Bosmans and others) or genitalia keys in the World Spider Catalog references — this skill stops at genus for the public-facing key and flags species ID as 'send to specialist / collect voucher' territory.

source.title: Titanoecidae genus-level identification key
source.authors: Nentwig, W., Blick, T., Bosmans, R., Gloor, D., Hänggi, A. & Kropf, C. — araneae.nmbe.ch, Spiders of Europe

couplets.character: Pattern on the opisthosoma (abdomen)

options.text: Opisthosoma with 4-6 pairs of small white spots
Opisthosoma without this pattern (plain, or with at most 1-2 pairs of spots/no spots)

couplets.character: Male pedipalp tibia length/shape relative to cymbium; geography

options.text: Pedipalp tibia about half the length of the cymbium, tibial apophysis tip pointed; native Palearctic (European) species

Pedipalp tibia about equal to cymbium length with a distinct kink, tibial apophysis broad and palm-like with several finger-like projections; epigyne with a broad median septum; South-East Asian species (only found in European hothouses, not wild in Europe)

field_questions.ask: Is there a low, sheet-like patch of fluffy, slightly bluish-white silk lining a crevice, under a stone, or against bark/a wall, rather than a typical hanging web or funnel?
field_questions.yes_means: Consistent with a cribellate hackled-band web as built by Titanoecidae (and some other cribellate families).

field_questions.ask: Does the spider's abdomen show small pale spots arranged in 4-6 neat pairs down the back?
field_questions.yes_means: Points to the genus Nurscia rather than Titanoeca.

field_questions.ask: Is the spider dark brown to blackish overall, with darkened upper leg segments, found under a stone or in a dry crevice by day?
field_questions.yes_means: Consistent with Titanoecidae habitus and habitat, though several other dark ground-dwelling families look similar.

field_questions.ask: Can you see (with a hand lens or macro photo) a row of curved bristles on the spider's hind leg combined with a felted, woolly look to the web silk?
field_questions.yes_means: Confirms it is a cribellate spider (calamistrum + hackled silk), narrowing it to cribellate families like Titanoecidae, Dictynidae, Amaurobiidae, Eresidae rather than non-cribellate look-alikes.

records.found_check: No found species were provided for verification in this task; characters above are drawn from family/genus descriptions and the species page for Titanoeca hispanica (size, colour) and should be checked against any future field finds, especially the calamistrum/cribellum feature which needs magnification to confirm.

lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Also cribellate with hackled silk, but Dictynidae have converging (not parallel) gnathocoxae and lack the short lateral spines on the male chelicerae seen in Titanoecidae; Dictynidae are usually smaller and their webs are often built higher up on vegetation/twig tips rather than low against stones/bark. Reliable separation needs close inspection of mouthpart structure.

Also cribellate, hackled-web builders in crevices/bark, but generally larger-bodied with a more robust build and different genitalic structures; habitat overlap is high so this is not a safe field separation without expert/microscope confirmation.

Convergent in being dark, ground/wall-dwelling hunters in Mediterranean habitats, but Zoropsidae (e.g. Zoropsis) are non-cribellate (no woolly hackled silk, no calamistrum) and noticeably larger and more mottled/patterned on the legs.

characters.web: Free-living hunter, no snare web. Trachelidae do not build webs to catch prey; they actively walk and hunt on the ground or low vegetation at night, and only use silk to spin a small disc-shaped or tubular silk retreat (under bark, stones, or in leaf litter) where they hide by day and where females guard their egg sac.
characters.eyes: Eight eyes in two rows of roughly equal-sized eyes. The front row is slightly curved forward (procurved) and the back row is curved backward (recurved), with the eyes spread fairly evenly across the front of a head that looks broad and flattened from above — hence the common name 'broad-faced sac spiders'. Not something you can judge reliably with the naked eye; what you can see is that the head looks wide and the spider has obvious large forward-pointing fangs (chelicerae).
characters.body_shape: Small, soft-bodied, sac-spider build: a broad, flattened, oval cephalothorax (head/front section) noticeably widened at the front because of large chelicerae, and an oval, unmarked or faintly patterned abdomen. Overall shape is similar to Clubionidae or Liocranidae 'sac spiders' — no spines, no armour, nothing bristly or robust about them.
characters.legs: Legs are of moderate length, fairly thin, without strong spines (a reduction/loss of leg spines is one of the family's defining features compared to related families) and ending in two claws with small tufts of hair. Legs are not flattened or unusually large; nothing about the legs stands out to the naked eye beyond 'fairly plain, smooth, sparse-spined legs'.
characters.colour: Generally dull and cryptic: yellowish, reddish-brown to brown carapace, often a little darker/reddish on the head region, with a pale yellowish-grey to brownish abdomen. Some species (e.g. Trachelas minor) show a darker chocolate-brown patch or lance-shaped band on the rear of the abdomen with faint pale chevrons, but overall these spiders are inconspicuous, not brightly marked.
characters.behaviour_habitat: Ground-dwelling and litter-dwelling nocturnal hunters. Found under stones, bark, in moss, leaf litter, and low vegetation in dry, often warm Mediterranean-type habitats; by day they hide in a thin silk retreat/sac rather than in an open web. They do not capture prey in silk — they catch it by ambush/pursuit on foot.

records.common_group: broad-faced sac spiders (ground-hunting sac spiders, formerly placed with Corinnidae)

source.title: araneae.nmbe.ch family key page and genus key link for Trachelidae (no.81)
source.authors: Nentwig, Blick, Bosmans, Gloor, Hänggi & Kropf (eds.), araneae.nmbe.ch

deeper_key.notes: I checked araneae.nmbe.ch directly. The site's master family key (https://araneae.nmbe.ch/key) lists Trachelidae only as a note redirecting to Corinnidae ('Further family (includes now Cetonana, Metatrachelas, Paratrachelas, Trachelas). See Corinnidae'), because the site's working key still uses the pre-2014 classification where Trachelidae genera were nested under Corinnidae. The page https://araneae.nmbe.ch/genkey/81/Trachelidae exists but only displays illustration plates (palps/epigynes) for the genus Trachelas at https://araneae.nmbe.ch/specieskey/551/Trachelas — it does NOT contain transcribable dichotomous couplets (no 'go to step X' text), only comparison images. No usable text-based key could be found at family, genus, or species level on araneae.nmbe.ch for Trachelidae. Species-level identification of the 5 Portuguese species (Trachelas minor, T. canariensis, Paratrachelas ibericus, P. maculatus, P. validus) genuinely requires comparing male palp and female epigyne/vulva structures under a microscope, following the taxonomic revision by Bosselaers, Urones, Barrientos & Alberdi (2009, Journal of Arachnology 37) and Bosselaers & Bosmans (2010, Zootaxa 2612). In the field or from a photo you can realistically only get to family (and with effort, to genus Trachelas vs Paratrachelas by overall build/coloration patterns described in those papers, but this is not reliable without specimens in hand). Honest ceiling for this dataset: family/genus level only from photos; species requires genitalia dissection or at minimum high-quality macro photos of palp/epigyne matched against the cited revisions.

field_questions.ask: Is the spider NOT sitting in any kind of web, silk sheet, or funnel — instead found wandering on the ground, under a stone, under bark, or in leaf litter?
field_questions.yes_means: Consistent with Trachelidae (and many other free-hunting families) — these spiders do not build prey-catching webs.

field_questions.ask: Does the front of the head/body look unusually wide and flattened compared to a typical 'normal' spider head, with the abdomen plain pale brownish-yellow and largely unmarked (maybe a faint darker patch at the rear)?
field_questions.yes_means: Consistent with the broad-faced, dull sac-spider look of Trachelidae, as opposed to a strongly patterned or shiny spider.

field_questions.ask: Is the spider small (body under about 4 mm, not counting legs) and soft-bodied rather than spiny or armoured-looking?
field_questions.yes_means: Matches the size and build of the Portuguese Trachelidae species, all of which are tiny (under 3.5 mm).

field_questions.ask: Was it found by day hidden inside a small flat silk pocket/sac under a rock, piece of bark, or in litter, rather than out in the open?
field_questions.yes_means: Matches the daytime retreat behaviour typical of this family.

records.found_check: No specific found/observed species were supplied for this task, so there is nothing to cross-check against the family characters above. If species records are added later (e.g. an iNaturalist or GBIF observation labelled Trachelas minor, T. canariensis, Paratrachelas ibericus, P. maculatus, or P. validus), confirm they show the small size (under ~3.5 mm), dull yellow-brown to reddish-brown colouring, broad flattened head, and ground/litter-dwelling habit described above — all are consistent with what is documented for these five species on araneae.nmbe.ch and in the Bosselaers et al. (2009, 2010) revisions.

lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Very similar dull, soft-bodied sac-spider build and similar retreat-building behaviour. Clubionidae tend to be a bit larger and have a more cylindrical, less flattened head/carapace; reliable separation in practice needs the leg spination (Trachelidae have notably reduced leg spines) or genitalia — at naked-eye/photo level these two families can look essentially identical.

Also small, plain-coloured, ground-dwelling wandering hunters with similar silk retreats. Practically indistinguishable from Trachelidae without microscopic leg-spine counts or genitalia examination; treat any small plain brownish ground sac spider as needing expert confirmation between these two families.

Historically Trachelidae genera were classified inside Corinnidae, so the families remain very close in general appearance. Corinnidae more often include ant-mimicking species with a shinier, more elongated, ant-like body shape; if the spider clearly mimics an ant's narrow waist and glossy look, lean Corinnidae, otherwise it is ambiguous without specimens.
